channel.title: Rob Burbea Dharma Talks
channel.description: A podcast archive of Rob Burbea Dharma talks from Dharma Seed, enriched with transcript-derived summaries, chapters, and episode artwork.
channel.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
channel.itunes:summary: A podcast archive of Rob Burbea Dharma talks from Dharma Seed, enriched with transcript-derived summaries, chapters, and episode artwork.

item.title: Perfection and Christ's Blessing
item.description:
What if perfection is not achievement, wholeness, or capacity at all, but something present even in weakness and loss? The talk begins with the image of a body worn down by illness and turns, through the simple touch of one hand washing the other, toward a different way of seeing: perfection as grace, love, and being known. It then reaches for Christ’s blessing as a way of sensing that blessing can be there even in dukkha, without explaining suffering away.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 A different perfection 00:18 Hands, weakness, grace 01:12 Seeing, not achieving 02:21 Christ’s blessing 03:10 Beyond theodicy 03:58 Blessing in dukkha
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62457/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 5)
item.description:
The talk introduces a small exercise in which voice, posture, and movement are combined and then carefully varied to see what they stir in the body and feeling life. Starting from a simple repeated syllable or fragment, the speaker explores changes in gesture, pitch, volume, and melody, inviting a slow, attentive listening for shifts in energy body, emotion, and image. The emphasis stays practical: not on producing a result, but on training sensitivity and noticing subtle effects.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Setting Up the Exercise 01:01 Image, Voice, Movement 03:02 Voicing a Simple Sound 05:45 Open and Crossed Postures 07:04 Varying the Gesture 08:58 Pitch and Loudness 10:55 Noticing the Effects
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62456/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 4)
item.description:
How do movement, gesture, and voice come alive when they are not copied from an idea, but guided from within by intention, felt soulfulness, and immediate feedback? The talk returns to exercise four, showing how blessing can be explored either through familiar forms or by letting the body, energy body, and sound lead into something more open, subtle, and less tied to meaning. It then widens into exercise five, using iconic moments such as Christ on the Via Dolorosa, the Buddha touching the earth, Rosa Parks refusing to move, and Abraham’s hospitality to the angels to show how an image can be embodied without becoming mere acting.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Blessing as lived intention 12:44 Voice beyond meaning 20:23 Relational sensitivity 39:03 Imaginal dyad variations 56:21 Christ and the earth 01:11:22 Abraham's hospitality 01:25:26 Rosa Parks' immovability 01:47:56 Tantric language and Buddha nature 01:56:08 Preliminaries that deepen
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62455/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 3)
item.description:
What if feeling pulled apart is not a sign that you need to get centered, but that you’ve lost contact with a more imaginal way of being? The talk begins with a personal example of pressure and decision-making, then turns to a close critique of centering, emptiness, and the five aggregates as models for the self. From there it moves into Soulmaking language—soul, angel, image, participation—and finally into a dyad practice of blessing through movement, gesture, and voice, first with meaning and then beyond meaning altogether.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and orientation 06:11 What centering implies 17:17 Emptiness and imaginal presence 27:30 Stretch, devotion, and risk 37:24 Soulmaking and the person 52:17 Meaning, participation, and image 01:00:14 First blessing exercise 01:14:13 Beyond ordinary meaning
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62454/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 2)
item.description:
The talk begins with a practical question: if these voice, movement, and gesture exercises matter, why do some people never take them up or let them fade? From there, the speaker moves through the reasons these preliminaries matter for soulmaking, the risk of inertia, and the need to develop sensitivity without turning the practice into either a casual sideline or an obsession. The second half turns to five specific exercise forms—brief emphatic movement, being borne aloft, raging beast, immovable protector, and the serpent—each presented as a way to reopen blocked circuits so movement, voice, image, and soul can work together more freely.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Setting the frame 06:26 Why the preliminaries matter 11:31 Sensitivity and holding back 24:51 Regret and the middle way 32:00 Exercise three begins 42:00 Angels and the raging beast 46:55 Guardian and serpent 50:50 Circuits and consequences 01:05:22 Discernment in life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62453/

item.title: Preliminaries, Regarding Voice, Movement, and Gesture (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the point of practice is not to arrive at calmness, but to open a much larger field of movement, desire, sound, and inquiry? The talk begins by framing these as preliminaries: simple exercises in movement, gesture, voice, and listening that may look small, but are meant to be developed and repeated. From there, the speaker widens into a deeper question about why we practice at all, how our fantasies of the path shape what we can hear, and why even apparently modest trainings can matter in the long run.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the preliminaries 01:56 Movement, voice, and development 07:27 Listening for instructions 10:02 Why this focus matters 19:54 Inertia and structure 34:47 Desire and the path 51:08 What practice is for 01:15:02 Exercise one: movement 01:28:56 Exercise two: voice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/62452/

item.title: Practising with Death and Dying
item.description:
Death here is not approached as a single doctrine, but as a question that keeps slipping between impermanence, nihilism, eternalism, and the way experience is actually shaped by our inherited views. The talk gradually turns from those familiar frames to a phenomenological inquiry grounded in Soulmaking Dharma, where death can be met through emptiness, gift, duty, beauty, and deep relationship rather than reduced to a blank end or a simple afterlife story. A long middle section works through physics examples, then returns to lived experience, including illness, grief, and the sense that a beloved person may remain present as image, even after death.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Question 03:17 Views To Set Aside 11:41 Physics And Perception 23:50 Phenomenology Of Death 43:49 What Helps Death 53:46 Gift, Duty, Beauty 01:11:35 Burying The Beloved 01:29:55 Image And Relationship 02:04:47 A Death Re-Enchanted
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61078/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 6 - An Inconclusive Conclusion, or A Prolegomenon)
item.description:
Can the five precepts really carry the whole weight of ethics, or do they only mark a beginning? The talk moves from that question into a steady critique of complacency in Buddhist ethics, then widens into a discussion of value, hierarchy, and why questions of what is of value may matter more than simple right and wrong. Along the way, it touches on climate change, power abuse, ceremonies, and the practical problem of how much context listeners need before hearing soulmaking and imaginal teachings.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the inquiry 04:06 Why precepts are not enough 09:46 Complacency and shadow 13:38 From rules to values 17:30 Ceremony and safety 19:43 Prerequisites and readiness 27:57 Hartmann and hierarchy 37:18 Nobility and the remote 46:41 Imaginal safeguards
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61077/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 5)
item.description:
How do ethics stay alive once the talk has already unsettled the ground beneath ontology and epistemology? The speaker moves through Buddhadharma, emptiness, Abhidhamma, Nagarjuna, Augustine, Plato, Hartman, and modern science to ask not whether a view is simply true, but what it makes possible for care, virtue, and soulmaking. The talk keeps returning to a practical test: whether a framework deepens sensitivity, dimension, and responsibility, or flattens ethics into something mechanical or empty. It ends by widening the question to beauty, trained discernment, and whether ethics can be grounded in a sense of what is worthwhile and meaningful.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 08:40 Buddhist views of value 13:40 Realism and its limits 17:26 Ethics and modern reductionism 27:29 Ways of looking 33:39 Ontology for soulmaking 40:48 Plato, Hartman, and hierarchy 01:02:19 Eros and Augustine 01:37:17 Beauty, expertise, and democracy
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61068/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 4)
item.description:
What if ethics is not mainly about restraint, but about what kind of human being we take ourselves to be? The talk moves from remembered moments of soulmaking and imaginal experience into a historical inquiry, contrasting a flattened secular-modern sense of humanity with traditions that saw the human as dimensional, related to divinity, cosmos, and a larger telos. Along the way it uses chemo, shame, hiri and ottappa, the Protestant Reformation, Plato, and modern utilitarian or pragmatic ethics to ask whether moral life can still be felt as beautiful, noble, and soul-making rather than merely necessary.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:29 Soulmaking and humanity 12:13 Kinds of suffering 17:25 Flattening the human 24:46 Reformation and rupture 33:07 Grace and ethics 49:47 Telos and soulmaking 01:03:44 Shame, eros, and dignity 01:23:54 What ethics is for
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61079/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 3)
item.description:
What if ethics needs more than rules, more than happiness, and more than a flat picture of the world? The talk moves through older ways of grounding value — divine command, scripture, cosmic hierarchy, rational order — and keeps asking what each one makes possible and what each one leaves out. It then turns to Soulmaking Dharma as a way of restoring dimensionality: not by asserting dogma, but by asking how eros, participation, beauty, and a sensed order might actually shape ethical life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Ethics and dimensionality 09:18 Voluntarism and scripture 14:26 Reformation and modern flattening 28:37 Augustine and ordered love 39:56 Modern cosmos, participatory reality 55:42 Soulmaking and the world 01:26:59 Rationality and the Logos 01:51:08 Limits of rational ethics 02:14:16 What lies behind reduction
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61075/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 2)
item.description:
What makes a life worthwhile, before we even ask what to do? The talk starts by widening ethics beyond rules and prescriptions, then moves into the sense that modern culture has split action from value and left ethics thin, mechanical, or directionless. From there it turns to shame and guilt, the place of hiri and ottappa in Buddhist teaching, and finally to the need for dimensionality: a higher or deeper order in which value, meaning, and ethical weight can actually be felt. It ends by sketching several historical ways ethics has been grounded, from rationality and cosmic order to voluntarism and concern with suffering.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Recap and ethical expansion 04:04 Worthwhile life questions 09:11 Freedom without grounding 18:56 Against mechanical ethics 26:05 Needs of an ethics 34:07 Soulmaking and imaginal practice 50:56 Shame, guilt, and conscience 01:01:15 Why ethics needs depth 01:23:57 Historical sources of dimensionality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61074/

item.title: The Image of Ethics (Part 1)
item.description:
How do we keep ethics from shrinking into law, rights, and a thin sense of what we are allowed to do? The talk argues that ethical life needs a wider frame: a sense of what is worthwhile, beautiful, and sacred, plus room for omission, aspiration, emotion, and love of the virtues. Using examples from climate protest, Nazi Germany, and the limits of standard moral systems, the speaker builds toward an ethics grounded in dimensionality, disciplined eros, and a more expansive way of sensing what matters.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Setting the frame 07:07 Why ethics matters now 14:02 Limits of law and rights 20:23 What ethics needs 28:35 Beyond duty alone 37:11 XR and public rhetoric 58:03 Omission and aspiration 01:16:38 Virtue, image, and ritual 01:33:32 Eros as ethical guidance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61073/

item.title: Pain, as Void and as Sacrament
item.description:
How can pain be met so it is not only endured, but seen more clearly, softened, and sometimes even transformed? The talk moves from simple mindfulness and bare attention, through the role of clinging, aversion, and emptiness-based ways of looking, into more elaborate possibilities from jhana, compassion, and soulmaking. It ends by asking not just how to reduce pain, but how to relate to it as sacred, including a personal account of illness, ritual, and giving back what was received.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the terrain 05:01 A progressive list 08:47 Mindfulness and bare attention 15:13 How pain is fabricated 27:11 Insight ways of looking 42:25 Jhana and malleability 53:02 Why not fully fade? 01:09:38 Mahayana and Vajrayana frames 01:31:40 Sacrifice and soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61071/

item.title: The Ontology of the Soul, and the Soul of Ontology ( ... inevitably)
item.description:
What if the crucial question is not whether something is real, but what a given view of reality lets us see, value, and do? The talk moves from the Buddha’s middle way of emptiness, through the imaginal middle way, into a broader inquiry about conventional truths, soulmaking, and the limits of reductionist psychology. It then widens again to include ethics, eros, time, and healing, asking how our assumptions shape the worlds of self, other, and practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:43 Two Middle Ways 02:39 Imaginal and Emptiness 06:22 Practice and Ontology 18:53 Conventional Reality 25:55 Soul and Reductionism 38:43 Eros and Disclosure 01:02:58 Conventional Truths at Work 01:31:47 What Models Open 01:51:59 Time and Liberation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61069/

item.title: Emptiness and Ways of Looking
item.description:
How do you keep emptiness from collapsing into a slogan, a belief, or a privileged final view? The talk moves through a careful unpacking of “ways of looking,” showing how perception, clinging, and fabrication shape self, world, time, and suffering, and why that means there is no neutral place outside the lens. It then keeps reopening the inquiry: against views of a pure flux, against “seeing through” language that smuggles in realism, and against emptiness being treated as a conclusion rather than a practice that can be picked up and put down. Along the way, it connects emptiness to ethics, trauma, the imaginal, eros, and the question of whether modern practice needs more than insight alone.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:30 Ways of looking 09:48 No final resting place 21:50 Seeing as, not through 31:59 Subtle distortions 46:42 Practice and flexibility 01:01:05 Ethics and imagination 01:21:55 Eros in practice 01:36:54 Quantum parallels and awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61070/

Along the way, it connects emptiness to ethics, trauma, the imaginal, eros, and the question of whether modern practice needs more than insight alone.

Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:30 Ways of looking 09:48 No final resting place 21:50 Seeing as, not through 31:59 Subtle distortions 46:42 Practice and flexibility 01:01:05 Ethics and imagination 01:21:55 Eros in practice 01:36:54 Quantum parallels and awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/61070/

item.title: PS - Playing in the In-Betweens
item.description:
How do you work when jhana is close but not quite complete? The talk moves through the grey areas of practice: loosening rigid schedules, finding a posture that lets awareness inhabit the whole body, and using subtle shifts of attention when pain, dullness, or a partial jhana blocks the way. From there it turns practical in finer and finer ways—how to stabilize a level by returning to an earlier one, how to work with the breath, and how to let stillness, light, or presence deepen on their own.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Postscript 02:22 Responsive Practice Rhythm 08:49 Posture and Embodiment 15:56 Working with Difficulty 18:38 Partial Jhana Skills 22:31 Moving Between Jhanas 30:49 Fourth Jhana and Breath 39:52 Closing Reflections 01:00:02 Dimensionality of Being
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60866/

item.title: Going Forwards
item.description:
What does it mean to carry practice forward without turning it into self-judgment or a race to measure results? The talk returns again and again to a simple but demanding question: how to keep a steady intention alive while staying playful, experimental, and honest about what you actually want. From jhana as one practice stream among many, to questions of desire, group practice, and the need for conceptual frameworks, it keeps widening the field instead of narrowing it to a single attainment. It ends in a quiet dedication, asking that the fruits of practice ripple out through interconnected lives.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:30 Appreciation for effort 02:57 Caring for the heart 07:24 Steady intention, playful practice 10:02 Practice streams and mandala 13:36 Balancing the streams 17:54 Withdrawal from entanglement 21:00 Tools and the bookmark 23:33 Desire, frameworks, and groups 29:22 Group intentions clarified
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60860/

item.title: The End of Time (The Cessation of Perception and Feeling)
item.description:
What if the deepest freedom is not a special state you arrive at, but the letting go of the subtle structures that keep experience building itself? The talk moves from the unconditioned and the deathless into a close reading of jhāna, showing how insight can be used inside absorption to see fabrications, clinging, and even the faintest sense of subject, object, and time. Along the way, it pauses for Pali Canon passages, questions about emptiness and dependent origination, and a sustained argument that these are not separate topics but one spectrum of practice and understanding.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening context 06:00 Unfabricated and emptiness 16:19 Dependent origination 23:54 What the unfabricated means 34:39 Beyond perception 46:14 Experience and knowledge 54:11 Jhana as insight 01:07:06 Sariputta’s discernment 01:15:51 Subject, object, time
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60885/

item.title: The Eighth Jhana (The Realm of Neither Perception Nor Non-Perception)
item.description:
What if the deepest meditation is not a blank space, but a very subtle refusal to land on any object at all? The talk works through the eighth jhana as a state where even nothingness is no longer taken up, and then turns that into a wider question about what perception means, whether it is labeling or the fabrication of experience itself. From there, the talk moves outward into a practical investigation of insight, dependent arising, and the danger of confusing genuine release with aversion or a rigid idea of how practice should unfold.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Entering the eighth jhana 01:58 What perception means 04:04 Perception as fabrication 10:15 Nothingness as last object 14:45 Non-landing and release 27:50 Refinement through the jhanas 34:42 Two paths into the state 46:43 Insight without aversion 59:28 Emptiness and intimacy
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60886/

item.title: The Seventh Jhana (The Realm of Nothingness)
item.description:
What if the deepest stillness is not a blank void, but a way of seeing that lets perceptions fade without force? The talk moves from the Buddha’s description of entering the sphere of nothingness to a careful reading of insight practices that open it: seeing perceptions as conditioned, as not peaceful, and as empty of self. From there it turns practical, asking how desire, conceptual frames, and the malleability of perception shape whether practice leads to fear, freedom, heart-opening, or a more imaginal sense of the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Introducing Nothingness 01:52 Unexpected Openings 08:54 Stepping Stones 15:53 Insight Ways of Looking 21:14 Empty of Self 27:22 Confidence in the Dimension 30:56 No Thingness and Fabrication 44:03 From Samadhi to Insight 56:22 Heart, Desire, and Limits
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60879/

item.title: The Sixth Jhana (The Realm of Infinite Consciousness)
item.description:
What changes when awareness turns back on itself and everything else falls away? The talk starts with the Buddha’s brief description of the realm of infinite consciousness, then slows down to unpack what that could mean in practice: noticing the subtle sense of knowing, tuning to it, and letting it strengthen until consciousness seems to know only itself. From there, the talk compares this state with the vastness of awareness, asks what such experiences really tell us, and keeps returning to the practical problem of how desire, intention, and training shape what becomes available. It ends by widening the frame again: these are not just rare altered states, but perceptions that can be cultivated, worked with, and understood in relation to dependent arising, emptiness, and ordinary life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 05:51 Opening the Topic 06:32 Canonical Description 07:39 Awareness Knows Itself 11:07 How Attention Settles 17:46 Intensity and Release 20:15 Vastness Compared 26:52 After-Effects and Truth 41:40 Paths In and View 59:02 Training, Desire, and Use
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60888/

item.title: The Fifth Jhana (The Realm of Infinite Space)
item.description:
What if the fifth jhana is less a special inner place than a way of sensing that form has no edge? The talk follows the Buddha’s brief description of the realm of infinite space, then keeps returning to the felt shift from solidity to non-solidity, from bounded body to boundless space, and from technical attainment to a quiet release. From there it widens into related questions: how metta can belong to these higher realms, how insight ways of looking can open space directly, and why the deeper issue may be what one actually desires in practice. The talk eventually turns from meditation into a broader reflection on authentic desire, the self on the path, and what makes a life worth living.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the fifth jhana 02:35 The Buddha's description 07:01 What space means 16:57 How it opens 23:36 Fourth to fifth jhana 32:53 Metta and higher realms 42:03 Insight as a way of looking 51:49 Presence and worldviews 01:06:00 Desire, authenticity, and practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60883/

item.title: Jhanas and Insight
item.description:
What if jhana and insight are not two separate steps, but different points on one spectrum of how experience is fabricated? The talk begins by questioning the familiar concentration-then-insight formula, then moves through several models of awakening before settling into a more radical view: that practice changes the way perception itself is organized, loosening clinging, selfing, and the sense of things as solid. From there, the speaker links jhanic absorption, aftereffects on perception, dependent origination, and emptiness, including examples from the formless realms and a note about how these shifts can spill into ordinary life. Quietly and insistently, the talk keeps returning to one question: what is it that changes when a way of looking changes?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:04 Framing the Question 06:00 Questioning the Usual Formula 10:56 Competing Models of Awakening 20:08 Emptiness and Ways of Looking 34:39 One Spectrum of Fabrication 41:25 Practice, Agency, and Caution 51:48 After-Effects on Perception 58:51 Dependent Origination in Practice 01:09:21 Jhana and Insight Both Ways
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60870/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
How do you tell a useful jhana territory from something that only feels close, and what do you do when practice seems to split between opening, probing, and simply enjoying? The talk moves through questions about pīti and sukha, primary nimittas, “satellite” states, and how much to stay with a calm-but-not-quite state versus aiming more directly toward the next jhana. It then widens into a broader frame: why jhana practice stays on its own road, how that differs from soulmaking and other heart practices, and how insight can work by noticing what attention adds to experience. Near the end, the talk turns to the Satipaṭṭhāna and Bahia suttas, reading mindfulness as a study of how experience is fabricated and how it arises and ceases.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening questions 02:08 Primary nimitta and side qualities 08:40 Soulmaking and practice scope 16:50 Pīti, sukha, and self 27:50 Insight and concentration 35:07 Opening, probing, and steadiness 44:41 Sustaining intensity 53:12 Ways of looking at pain 01:02:40 Bahīya and Satipaṭṭhāna
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60878/

item.title: The Fourth Jhana
item.description:
The talk starts by narrowing the fourth jhana to its felt texture: not just calm, but an almost translucent stillness, pure bright awareness, and a whole-body pervasion that can feel like a different realm. From there it keeps opening the question of what that state is for—whether it is an escape from the world, or a way of seeing perception, equanimity, and even the world itself more clearly. The speaker moves between the Buddha’s canonical formulas, practical guidance on how the state deepens, and cautions about reading the texts too narrowly, ending with how this kind of stillness can support insight and flexibility rather than becoming just another fixed achievement.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 From Third to Fourth 04:17 Canonical Description 07:04 Stillness and Brightness 13:35 Perception and Emptiness 26:33 Equanimity Deepens 44:04 Emotion and Intention 47:21 Wrapped in Stillness 01:05:44 Purity, Malleability, Insight 01:20:58 Base Practice and Maturing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60881/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
How does a retreat hold silence without turning it into dryness, and how do desire and equanimity deepen practice without becoming either indulgence or indifference? The talk begins with a quiet request to keep silence outside the Q&A, then moves into a long exchange on intention, risk, and the way desire can shape a life while still being conditioned and changeable. From there it turns to equanimity, the spectrum of clinging across the jhānas, the bodily texture of pīti and sukha, and the difference between jhāna’s movement toward dissolution and imaginal practice’s preservation of two-ness. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to a practical theme: learn the subtle patterns, stay sensitive, and let the practice reveal what is actually happening.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 04:52 Silence on retreat 06:56 Goal and intention 14:35 Desire and devotion 24:17 What equanimity means 36:01 Clinging as a spectrum 45:39 Desire, fabrication, and practice 53:58 Pīti, sukha, and the body 01:02:34 Jhāna and soulmaking 01:20:12 Bypass and closing questions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60872/

item.title: Q & A, and Short Talk
item.description:
Can emotion, pain, and even bodily tightness become part of jhana practice without losing the thread? The talk moves through that question by stressing context and intention first, then widening into the distinction between sadness and being touched, the use of perception in working with pīti and sukha, and the danger of turning practice into a narrow project of comfort or control. Later, it shifts into a more philosophical register: what it means for jhana to depend on causes and conditions, what “radical” points to, and why the deeper gift is not just relief but a changing view of self, experience, and emptiness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 04:12 Context and intention 10:14 Tears and openness 15:26 What radical means 20:43 Steady practice and warning signs 41:58 Jhana mastery and views 01:05:30 Momentum and hidden locks 01:17:10 Pain and perception 01:21:14 Limits of bliss-making 01:34:14 Happiness beyond the cushion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60887/

item.title: The Third Jhana
item.description:
What if the third jhana is less about a fixed set of factors and more about a very refined shift from rapture into peaceful contentment? The talk moves from the canonical description — equanimity, mindfulness, alertness, and the body pervaded by sukha — into a close experiential map: three internal levels, ways of entering by “drinking” the second jhana, and signs that the body and mind are becoming deeply unified. From there it widens into the larger Dharma point that perception itself changes the world we inhabit, so the practice is also an inquiry into dependent arising, emptiness, and how push and pull quiet down.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the frame 02:46 Canonical third jhana 07:01 Peacefulness over rapture 11:25 Three levels inside 15:12 How to enter it 25:07 Oasis and pond 27:05 Working the field 42:08 Stillness and one taste 53:53 Insight, emptiness, and escape
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60891/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
How do you work with meditation experiences so they support practice rather than confuse it? The talk moves from a detailed exploration of ringing in the ears and other nimitta-like phenomena into a wider map of pīti, sukha, subtlety, intensity, and the right object of attention, with repeated reminders not to get lost in labels when the real question is how the practice is going. It then opens into questions of guilt around happiness, jhana as nourishment for service, and how perception, emotion, and hindrances can be understood as part of a larger field of conditions rather than fixed problems.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:42 Ringing and aversion 05:22 Sound as concentration 09:34 Sound as insight 14:42 Subtlety and refinement 25:01 Questions from practice 33:10 Joy and service 48:14 Perception and emptiness 53:07 The bandwidth of happiness 01:10:08 Hindrances and spaciousness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60874/

item.title: True to Your Deepest Desires (Talk and Short Guided Meditation)
item.description:
How do you keep practicing when desire turns into comparison, self-judgment, and a sense of failure? The talk moves from the practical difficulties of questions on retreat to a larger inquiry into how the self gets built around practice, and how that can narrow or distort the path. From there, it turns toward the Four Bases of Power, the role of desire, and the possibility of relating to frustration without abandoning the longing that brought you here. A short guided meditation closes by inviting a listener to sense the beauty of that desire, the patience behind showing up, and the willingness itself as something to cherish.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 04:54 Culture and Self-View 08:57 You Can Do This 15:13 View of Practice 27:27 Radical Intention 32:13 Desire and Frustration 39:53 Sorting the Signals 46:18 A Life Shaped by Wanting 58:55 Desire Through Dukkha
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60889/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
How do you keep practicing when desire turns into comparison, self-judgment, and a sense of failure? The talk moves from the practical difficulties of questions on retreat to a larger inquiry into how the self gets built around practice, and how that can narrow or distort the path. From there, it turns toward the Four Bases of Power, the role of desire, and the possibility of relating to frustration without abandoning the longing that brought you here. A short guided meditation closes by inviting a listener to sense the beauty of that desire, the patience behind showing up, and the willingness itself as something to cherish.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 04:54 Culture and Self-View 08:57 You Can Do This 15:13 View of Practice 27:27 Radical Intention 32:13 Desire and Frustration 39:53 Sorting the Signals 46:18 A Life Shaped by Wanting 58:55 Desire Through Dukkha
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60889/

item.title: The Second Jhana
item.description:
What changes when the second jhana is not just a quieter mind, but a shift from pīti to sukha, from the excitement of entry into a more refined and bodily happiness? The talk reads the Buddha’s descriptions closely, then keeps circling back to practice: how to tell pīti from sukha, how to let the base practice and happiness integrate, and why forcing progress is usually the wrong move. Along the way, the speaker uses images of a ripening mango, a lake filling from below, and even a street interview in Newton Abbot to make the territory feel concrete. The talk ends by widening the frame, asking whether jhana is really about getting rid of thought, or about learning how perception is fabricated and what that means for letting go.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:15 Reading the सूत्र 03:18 What samadhi means 06:31 Lake and spring simile 12:29 Refinement, not force 21:19 Pīti and sukha 25:23 How it matures 28:42 Working with pīti 38:39 Thought and subtlety 46:55 What jhāna gives
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60880/

item.title: Q & A, and Short Talk
item.description:
How do you work with jhāna when the mind keeps wanting either to probe, to open, or to pull back? The talk moves through a series of questions about pīti, peacefulness, subtle hindrances, and the different “bandwidths” of concentration, using images like tuning a radio, drinking from a spring, and not snatching at pleasant experience. It also widens out into practical guidance on retreat life: how openness, connection, and a steady intention can support samadhi without becoming rigid or entangled.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the questions 03:03 Tuning attention 06:39 Receiver and refinement 09:17 Enjoyment before control 15:35 Not snatching at pīti 20:19 Delighting in happiness 26:14 Daily life and familiarity 32:18 First jhāna in context 42:24 Openness and restraint
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60873/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
How do you work with hindrances, fear, and over-efforting without turning them into a story about failure? The talk stays close to the actual texture of practice: recognizing when the mind is poisoned by self-doubt, learning when to back off, and finding softer, warmer states that can steady the system rather than fight it. From there it moves into detailed questions about jhana, especially how happiness, peacefulness, equanimity, and subtle attention change from one level to the next. The discussion keeps returning to one practical theme: the felt quality of practice depends on relationship, and that relationship can be retrained.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Q&A 04:06 Working with effort 14:46 Fear and softening 20:34 Second jhana clarity 28:09 Third jhana territory 30:37 Mapping the later states 41:12 Relationship shapes experience 52:48 Thought, noise, and drifting 01:05:01 Excitement, creativity, and intention
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60875/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
How do you tell whether a quiet, velvety state belongs to jhāna, imaginal practice, or something in between? The talk moves through a practical Q&A on pīti, sukha, ekaggatā, and the shifting emphasis of different jhānic factors, with the speaker repeatedly stressing that the right map depends on what is actually happening in experience. It also returns to the question of how to work with images, energy, and sleepiness without forcing a formula, and why one-pointedness and equanimity need to be read in context rather than as fixed definitions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Pīti and sukha 01:40 One-pointedness in context 03:12 Absorption and direction 04:02 Jhāna or imaginal 05:54 Choosing the emphasis 08:59 Why maps stay partial 12:33 Energy and fatigue 14:17 Images with pīti 16:55 Soft pīti and waking up
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60876/

item.title: The First Jhana, and Playing and Working in (and out of) any Jhana
item.description:
How do you work with jhāna without turning it into a test of whether you’ve gotten it right? The talk starts with the first jhāna’s soap simile and then keeps returning to the same practical question: how to let pīti spread through the body, sustain it, and use it as a living basis for practice rather than a fixed achievement. From there it ranges into the meanings of key Pali terms, the role of signs like light, the place of walking meditation, and how to move in and out of jhāna without losing the sense of play. It stays grounded in concrete instructions for building familiarity, re-entering states, and noticing what actually deepens the mind.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:42 First Jhana Simile 08:47 Later Terms, Earlier Practice 12:08 Forget the Labels 15:40 Pīti as a Sign 19:32 What First Jhana Needs 27:05 Different Practice Grooves 34:51 Work, Play, and SASSIE 40:06 Malleability of Perception 53:02 Enjoyment and Adjustment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60882/

item.title: Developing Piti, Developing Focus, Developing Wellbeing
item.description:
How do you work with pleasure in meditation without grasping at it, dismissing it, or turning it into a project? The talk moves between two broad approaches: steady concentration on one object, and a more receptive way of coaxing well-being through the energy body, imagination, and a gentler quality of attention. From there it widens into samadhi as collectedness and integrity, practical ways to meet constriction, and the reminder that jhana is a matter of perception—something to play with, not snatch at.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Pīti Is 02:16 Forms of Pleasure 06:02 Pleasure and Practice 14:16 Breath and Subtlety 25:05 Collectedness and Integrity 33:15 Working with Constriction 40:03 Perception Attainments 50:29 Spreading and Settling Pīti 01:07:10 Equanimity and Awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60864/

item.title: Insight Ways of Looking, Other Energy Body Possibilities, and Summarized Instructions
item.description:
What if insight is not just something that happens after careful mindfulness, but a way of looking that actively loosens clinging in the moment? The talk begins there, then shows how that loosening can change the felt body, soften pain, and open into pīti and samadhi without snatching at pleasantness. In the second half, it turns into a series of short experiments with the energy body: opening to whole-body space, working with two or three points, imagining lines of energy, and testing how different framings shape experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Insight as a way of looking 11:56 Using insight for practice 12:22 Energy body without breath 18:50 Opening and brightening 24:12 Lines and luminous forms 31:24 What the exercises show 42:09 Big-picture orientation 47:06 Simple concentration instructions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60868/

item.title: Q & A
item.description:
How do subtle emotional shifts, a quiet breath, and even pain become part of one practice of deepening? The talk moves from four linked capacities—sensitivity, attunement, responsiveness, and refinement—into examples from metta, samadhi, and the jhanas, showing how attention can be tuned to increasingly subtle qualities rather than chasing intensity. In the second half, the focus turns to piti and pain: how to work with discomfort without just gritting the teeth, and how relationship and view shape whether energy opens or contracts.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:06 Four related capacities 03:47 Subtle object, subtle mind 06:07 Working with piti and pain 08:41 Resolve and letting be 11:08 What is the playground
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60877/

item.title: Attitude, Effort, Achievement, and View
item.description:
What if the real issue in practice is not how hard you try, but the pressure, self-judgment, and narrow view shaping that effort? The talk moves from the meaning of samadhi, to the problem of achievement timetables, to a slower, subtler question of how attention, attitude, and view either tighten or open the field. From there it widens into a practical reframe: quality over quantity, balanced effort, and eventually jhana as a kind of playing with perception rather than a state to seize.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Frame 02:05 Pressure and Narrowness 05:17 Widening Practice 09:49 Goals Without Tightening 16:50 Quality of Attention 25:09 Imaginal Breath 28:55 Dropping the Snatch 39:58 Jhana, Eros, and Self 54:49 Perception and Emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60861/

item.title: A Hidden Treasure: The Relationship with the Hindrances
item.description:
How do the hindrances become part of jhana practice rather than a sign that practice has gone wrong? The talk moves from a brief correction about metta and energy-body awareness into a detailed, practical exploration of sloth, restlessness, desire, aversion, and doubt, with many concrete antidotes: widening awareness, using the breath differently, shifting to metta, and working with effort levels. It then opens into a subtler frame, where even deep concentration can still contain faint hindrances, and where patience, flexibility, and not taking anything personally become central. In the end, the talk turns from technique to insight, asking listeners to see hindrances as ordinary, workable, and sometimes even as a kind of treasure.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the Talk 02:49 Hindrances and Jhana 05:35 Antidotes, Not Indifference 07:52 Sleepiness and Restlessness 17:04 Sense Desire and Ill Will 22:17 Noise, Doubt, and Questions 27:39 Exchanging Self and Other 30:01 Subtle Hindrances 42:59 Patience and Insight
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60867/

item.title: Focusing on One Point (Intensity, Directionality, Subtlety) (Instructions)
item.description:
How can a narrow breath focus become steady without becoming tense? The talk moves from a practical choice between a small spot and a wider field into a careful unpacking of samadhi: keep a light whole-body background, notice over-efforting early, and treat effort as an ongoing adjustment rather than a fixed technique. From there, the speaker shifts to the felt quality of attention itself, arguing for subtlety, directionality, and just enough intensity to stay receptive rather than forceful. The discussion ends by suggesting that as the object becomes more delicate, the attention should become more delicate too, and that this delicacy can be quietly enjoyable.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Context 02:35 Jhana Direction 08:48 A Narrower Spot 10:45 Samadhi Misconstrued 12:03 Whole Body Background 13:49 Effort As Art 17:02 Quality Over Quantity 18:28 Subtlety, Direction, Intensity 24:25 Delicacy And Delight
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60859/

item.title: An Introduction to the Jhanas
item.description:
What is jhāna really for: a series of neatly labeled attainments, or a way of deepening well-being, freedom, and the training of perception? The talk moves from the Buddha’s four form jhānas and the formless attainments, through cautionary images about not rushing ahead, into a larger argument that practice should build sensitivity, discernment, and the capacity to let go. Rather than treating jhāna as a badge or a test of whether thought has stopped, the speaker keeps returning to what actually changes the being, and how that change relates to insight, emptiness, and the rest of the path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the territory 02:19 Buddha’s four form jhānas 09:38 The formless attainments 11:52 What really matters 24:26 Mastery and marination 33:45 Pitfalls and differing views 48:20 Why practice jhāna 55:04 Perception and emptiness 01:04:30 Walking, rhythm, and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60869/

item.title: Orienting to This Jhana Retreat
item.description:
What if jhana depends less on sealing out experience than on learning to meet it with happiness, openness, and a steadier heart? This opening talk orients the retreat around a flexible, practice-led approach: an open schedule, interviews, a current “playground” level, and a base practice that can include breath, metta, or insight ways of looking when they genuinely open pīti and ease contraction. It also widens into the emotional weather of retreat and life itself—grief, activism, illness, and other pressures—then turns that into a practice of devotion, resolve, gratitude, beauty, and the five precepts as a shared ethical ground.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Welcome and setup 05:42 Open schedule, open mind 10:46 Listening and the playground 22:45 Base practice and pīti 28:31 Insight as support 39:50 Intention and inertia 49:45 Hindrances and emotions 58:35 Grief, devotion, resolve 01:13:42 Kirsten and Sari
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/60865/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 9)
item.description:
What if ethics is not only about reducing suffering, but about learning to recognize higher and lower values, and to feel the difference between them? The talk moves from utilitarianism and virtue ethics into a wider question of moral sensibility, using climate change, species loss, and biodiversity as examples of concerns that can’t be held by survival or usefulness alone. It then turns to the relation between Dharma, soul-making, and meaning, asking whether some values are loved for their own sake even when they are, in a practical sense, useless.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Ethical frameworks 02:47 Limits of utility 07:26 Virtue and efficacy 10:19 Higher and lower values 17:17 Fear and moral depth 27:50 Species loss and care 39:24 Meaning and intention 46:36 Useless values 56:56 Foundations and beyondness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58781/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 8)
item.description:
What if ethics has to answer not only to universal values, but also to the particular calling of an individual soul? The talk moves from the tension between collective duty and personal soul-style, through examples of uncommon virtue, and then into trickier territory: how blocked soul-making can become hatred, and how power, courage, or imaginal opening can change that. It then broadens into a meditation on love of the remote, future generations, and the idea of life as an open-ended moral quest shaped by one’s unique personality.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 05:04 Universal values and soul 16:31 Radiant virtue 32:42 Nobility and new values 42:26 Hatred and powerlessness 01:07:35 Polemic and courage 01:23:30 Love of the remote 01:35:18 Creative spirit and sacrifice 01:46:35 Narrative unity of a life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58780/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 7)
item.description:
What happens when moral life is not a clean choice between right and wrong, but a series of real tensions between goods that both matter? The talk moves through Hartmann’s idea of antinomies of value, contrasting simple oppositions like kindness and unkindness with harder conflicts such as harmony and conflict, individuality and community, or love of the nearest and love of the remote. It then turns to Dharma practice, asking how sila, the seven factors of awakening, and even the image of the Buddha can be understood when values do not all fully fit together at once.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Simple oppositions 01:55 Hartmann and antinomies 10:21 Conflict as a value 17:26 Classical virtue and tragedy 26:50 Balancing practice qualities 30:03 Three kayas and beyondness 35:50 Absolutes and mysticism 53:21 Plural psyche, simple rules 01:09:44 Minimums and hard choices
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58785/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 6)
item.description:
Can ethics be more than a cultural preference, and can conscience point to something objective without turning moral life into a rigid system? The talk moves from Hartmann’s case for an objective hierarchy of values, through the problem of guilt, free will, and personhood, into a more flexible Dharma view that can shift between self, conditions, and responsibility as needed. It then turns to the inner complexity of virtues themselves: self-control, gentleness, altruism, and loyalty can all become distorted if they stand alone, so moral life asks for counterweights, syntheses, and a careful sense of what is higher in a given situation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Framing the inquiry 03:34 Hartmann on values 09:57 Culture and analogy 15:39 Practicing with ideas 21:41 Soul, self, and guilt 30:19 Freedom and moral being 39:42 The good as higher value 48:55 Conflicting virtues 01:03:06 Beyond easy answers
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58779/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 5)
item.description:
What if moral difficulty is not a sign that something has gone wrong, but the very place where values become real? The talk moves from climate action and protest, through examples of courage and sacrifice, into a sustained reflection on Hartmann, Plato, and other traditions to suggest that crisis, conflict, guilt, and the absence of value can all become doorways for soulmaking. It then turns more contemplative, asking how values can be sensed directly, meditated on, and allowed to reshape the self and the world as something more dimensional, more demanding, and more alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Values Become Alive 05:12 Climate Action and Protest 10:55 Crisis as Opportunity 16:31 Absence and Conflict 25:39 Plato and Divine Goodness 34:00 Virtue as Radiance 46:03 Mundus Imaginalis 01:11:48 Remorse and Sensitivity 01:35:37 Meditating on Ideals
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58778/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 4)
item.description:
What if values are not just human preferences, but real ideals that call to be lived? The talk moves from Hartmann and Plato to mathematics, using pi, irrational numbers, imaginary numbers, and the wave function to make a case for a realm of ideal being that shapes physical reality without fully appearing in it. From there it turns practical and intimate: an anesthetic near-death moment, the phrase “Metta, metta,” and the question of what we can actually trust, bow to, and give ourselves to when life falls short of the ideal. The closing movement brings in guilt, devotion, and eros, suggesting that our pain may point back to the values and images we cannot perfectly embody, yet still orient our lives by.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:59 Ontology of values 04:03 Participation and ideas 10:03 The ideal calls forth action 20:29 Mathematics as analogy 36:04 Values and transcendence 40:06 Metta near death 52:03 Dimensionality and soul 01:01:23 Faith beyond outcomes 01:06:56 Guilt, devotion, and healing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58777/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 3)
item.description:
What if virtue is not just a means to an end, but something beautiful in itself? The talk begins by setting Buddhist sila beside an expanded discussion of moral values and virtues, then moves into a contrast between traditions that see the good as serving liberation and a view, drawn from Hartmann, that the morally good stands on its own. From there it opens into questions of human nature, the beauty of a life shaped by virtue, and the tension between doing what is effective and doing what is right when the outcome is uncertain. Examples from Bonhoeffer, the Titanic, Plato, and climate activism keep the discussion grounded in real situations where efficacy and soul-level integrity diverge.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 05:22 Values and virtues 11:27 Intention over result 20:23 Paramitas and release 28:52 Hartmann’s intrinsic good 40:05 Beauty, language, and soul 01:07:38 Modern shifts in virtue 01:24:26 Virtue over efficacy 01:41:01 Double intention
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58776/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 2)
item.description:
What happens when a Dharma path that emphasizes suffering reduction, gentleness, and detachment meets climate collapse, social justice, and the pull of conscience? The talk moves through psychotherapy, Buddhism, and imaginal practice, showing how familiar frameworks can miss ethical force, grief, wrath, beauty, and the need to act. Along the way, the speaker uses stories of a second house, a monastery that would not recycle, and a Black Madonna image to ask what really shapes our choices and what our traditions leave out.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 04:12 Psychotherapy and Dharma 12:12 Frameworks and blind spots 18:35 An imaginal misreading 29:34 Conscience and tradition 48:35 Ethics, time, and future 01:04:58 Dharma and moral courage 01:16:16 Climate and responsibility 01:32:24 Imagining the suffering
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58775/

item.title: Sila and Soul (Part 1)
item.description:
Ethics begins here as an open, unfinished question: how do sila, soulmaking Dharma, and modern life meet when morality is no longer held together by a single shared framework? The talk moves from the Buddha’s skillful/unskillful framing and the five precepts, through the West’s inheritance of guilt, individualism, and inner criticism, into a wider critique of modernity, postmodern relativism, law, democracy, globalization, and the loss of moral education. It then turns toward the possibility that imaginal practice, reverence, and a fuller sense of soul may deepen ethical life rather than stand apart from it, while also widening the range of what moral care can look like.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the subject 03:28 Buddhist and Western frames 09:16 Precepts and simplification 13:38 Culture, guilt, and character 24:33 MacIntyre and modernity 37:30 Rorty, democracy, and power 54:33 Global ethics and moral education 01:17:58 Vinaya and meditation 01:36:46 Soulmaking and moral life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58774/

item.title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 3)
item.description:
What if Dharma is not a single universal truth, but one framework among others, shaped by history, culture, and soul style? The talk moves from a critique of modern and postmodern ways of thinking into a careful questioning of Buddhist universals, then into the claim that Soulmaking Dharma can be understood either as inside Buddha Dharma or as a larger movement that includes it. Along the way, it weighs eclectic borrowing, feminism, colonialism, and the limits of conceptual frameworks, while returning again and again to the idea that teachings may need to stay open, elastic, and ready to break and be rebuilt.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Cultural context 05:58 What counts as universal 13:23 Gender and structure 17:32 Soulmaking and plurality 26:38 Borrowing across traditions 35:01 Postmodern limits 43:53 Dharma as art 51:39 Culture and spirit 58:03 Two ways to place it
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58769/

item.title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 2)
item.description:
How do culture, personality, and worldview quietly shape what we think Dharma is? The talk moves through several contrasting models of awakening and emptiness, showing how each one carries different assumptions about the world, the human being, and what practice is for. It also asks whether images, sacredness, and beauty are provisional stepping stones or essential parts of the path, and ends by returning to the need for honesty, autonomy, and careful inquiry in relation to authority.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 What Shapes Tradition 03:34 Personal Inclinations Matter 06:55 Four Worldviews 10:54 Awakening Through Models 17:30 Images as Stepping Stones 26:37 Practice and Neuroscience 34:02 Many Meanings of Emptiness 50:09 Deepening the View 58:14 Honesty, Authority, and Inquiry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58768/

item.title: Dharma(s) Discovered and Created (Part 1)
item.description:
What makes a tradition alive: shared texts, practices, values, and authority, or the tension between all of these and the freedom to differ? The talk moves through mindfulness, the Buddhist canon, and then wide-ranging comparisons with Judaism, Christianity, and philosophy to show how traditions are shaped by culture, interpretation, and even productive misunderstanding. It keeps returning to a harder question: when does difference enrich a living lineage, and when does it become a fracture or a counterfeit teaching?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 06:26 Mindfulness and tradition 26:52 What traditions share 35:13 Mystery and canonical depth 47:49 Tradition as exchange 01:02:01 Cultural conditioning 01:12:26 Mindfulness revised 01:25:10 Origins and history 01:41:44 Conflict as vitality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58767/

item.title: The Phoenix of Metaphysics (Part 2)
item.description:
What if the urge to know reality, rather than collapse into certainty or shrug into skepticism, opens into a wider middle way? The talk moves from critiques of classical science and positivism into emptiness, imaginal practice, and the claim that concepts can be fruitful even when they are unproven or perhaps untrue. Along the way it brings in physics, metaphysics, and figures like Rorty, Yeats, Corbin, Smolin, and Goethe to ask whether inquiry can stay rigorous without becoming rigid. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from proving final truth to letting eros, beauty, and conceptual frameworks support soulmaking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Framing the problem 08:42 Shrugging and default views 10:50 Imaginal middle way 17:31 Sacredness in emptiness 29:57 Higher truths and physics 47:41 Metaphysics in science 01:04:51 Fruitful falsehoods 01:22:20 Beauty as cause 01:53:53 Inquiry as soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58789/

item.title: The Phoenix of Metaphysics (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the question of what is real is also a question of what a culture learns to respect? The talk moves from ontology and epistemology into the history of modern science, using Galileo, Descartes, Newton, quantum physics, and later philosophy of science to show how the idea of an observer-independent world both rose to dominance and then fractured under closer scrutiny. From there it turns toward the practical stakes for emptiness and sensing the soul: how a way of looking shapes not only knowledge, but ethics, imagination, and the kinds of life a person can trust. It ends by sketching an imaginal middle way, where rigor and openness are held together without collapsing into either literalism or vagueness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the inquiry 06:17 Knowledge and social order 11:28 Classical worldview 25:28 Quantum fracture 34:52 What science reveals 54:43 Paradigms and truth 01:09:38 Truth, models, and realism 01:34:15 Deconstruction and assumptions 01:59:25 Epistemology as practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58788/

item.title: Soulmaking Dyad Practice
item.description:
How do you keep a relational practice from slipping into confusion, craving, or collapse? The talk moves from basic dyad exercises—balance of attention, metta, energy body awareness, and the sense of a temenos—to the more delicate terrain of image, eros, and the ways a shared field can either open or shut down. Along the way, it keeps returning to small but decisive questions: what is happening in me, what is happening between us, and what level of practice is actually appropriate right now?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the practice 03:59 Early exercises and scales 08:19 Why dyads are sensitive 14:06 Prerequisites for practice 20:04 Not rushing ahead 27:56 Attention, gaze, and metta 37:37 Temenos as container 49:09 After the encounter 01:03:49 Shared framework and stages
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58782/

item.title: Logoi and Leanings
item.description:
How do you steer when practice opens a fork: move toward the calm of samadhi, or stay with the imaginal depth and its soulmaking pull? The talk moves through navigation, pacing, and the subtle cues of the energy body, then widens into the role of logoi, conceptual frameworks, and how they can either limit or release what practice can become. Near the end, the speaker turns to symbolic time, causality, and a reading of experience that is less flat and more open to multiple dimensions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the navigation 02:51 Subtle shifts in practice 09:09 The samadhi fork 18:53 Pacing and balance 26:16 Logos and causality 37:37 Energy body and ideas 48:10 Present and future causes 59:20 Water, time, and scripture 01:10:47 Concepts as support
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58772/

item.title: The Invitation of Otherness (Autonomy, Eros, and Intentionality)
item.description:
What does it mean for an image to be other, and yet intimate, autonomous, and responsive? The talk moves from two-ness and erotic tension into the autonomy of the self and the image, then into a careful distinction between narrowing practice around getting something and opening to what the image itself wants. Along the way, it turns to desire as part of self-knowledge, and to the way a fuller intention can let eros, healing, and soul-making unfold together rather than cancel each other out.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Imaginal relationship 03:25 Two-ness and otherness 06:24 Autonomy of the image 11:10 Essence and anchoring 17:54 Autonomy of self 25:51 Eros and wisdom 39:11 Being loved back 51:12 Desire and self-knowledge 01:16:10 Fullness of intention
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58784/

item.title: Dimensionality, Humility, Duty, and Love
item.description:
What changes when an image is not just something seen, but a presence that can be noticed, ignited, and answered? The talk moves from the practical point of the imaginal elements—how they help discern what is genuinely imaginal and how they can be worked with in practice—into revisions on twoness, particularity, and the danger of reducing an image to one meaning. From there it widens into dimensionality, humility, duty, prayer, and love, including a long reflection on how imaginal love differs from metta and how it can reveal the ideal or deepest dimension of a person.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:17 Purpose of the lattice 09:43 Two revisions 14:16 Dimensionality and depth 20:52 Anchors in depth 40:38 Humility and self 51:52 Prayer and desire 01:02:17 Duty and rightness 01:15:52 Particular love 01:33:28 Love as knowing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58770/

From there it widens into dimensionality, humility, duty, prayer, and love, including a long reflection on how imaginal love differs from metta and how it can reveal the ideal or deepest dimension of a person.

Chapters: 01:17 Purpose of the lattice 09:43 Two revisions 14:16 Dimensionality and depth 20:52 Anchors in depth 40:38 Humility and self 51:52 Prayer and desire 01:02:17 Duty and rightness 01:15:52 Particular love 01:33:28 Love as knowing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58770/

item.title: Daimon, Refracted
item.description:
What if deep emptiness does not flatten the self, but opens a wider range of selves that can be played with, inhabited, and seen as empty? The talk moves from Nagarjuna and the Buddha’s middle way into a long exploration of imaginal practice: a fountain of soul flowing into self, other, and world; a wounded blackbird that becomes an image; a queen in a blood-trailing robe; and a naked goddess in a tree. From there it turns to the inner critic, desire, imprinting, Corbin’s angel out ahead, the sefirot, and a short guided meditation on metta and the voice as a way of letting the self become more dimensional and soul-suffused.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Emptiness and self-views 05:45 Middle way, not nihilism 10:45 Soul’s balance and spread 19:54 Desire as entry point 25:44 The wounded blackbird 31:04 Images as shared qualities 47:07 Imprint, genius, and soul 01:00:11 Corbin, Souriau, and angels 01:13:08 The inner critic
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58766/

item.title: "Until All is Liquid" (Emptiness and Sensing with Soul)
item.description:
How does loosening perception without losing form make imaginal practice possible? The talk begins with an alchemical maxim—wait until all is liquid—and uses it to trace a middle territory where things are less fabricated but not yet vanished. From there it moves through emptiness as a support for soulmaking, the danger of turning practice into a “should,” and the shift from striving for more fading to curiosity, ease, and insight into dependent arising. Later it turns to examples from illness, lineage devotion, and prayer, showing how even modest emptiness can deepen image, self-sense, and a sense of sacredness without flattening them.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Until All Is Liquid 06:05 When Emptiness Helps 14:34 Curiosity Over Shoulding 23:38 Partial Fading, Real Insight 33:37 Ways of Looking 47:38 Empty Yet Soulful 01:06:12 Parallels Across Traditions 01:16:17 Emptiness in Daily Practice 01:26:50 Middle Ways and Flexibility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58786/

item.title: Heat and the Material
item.description:
How do emotions become a crucible rather than something to get rid of? The talk traces a careful path between ordinary mindfulness, which can quiet and deconstruct feeling, and soulmaking practice, where emotion is held, enlarged, and sometimes transformed into image, eros, and a more vivid sense of self. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to caveats: not every feeling has one cause, not every person needs more heat, and sometimes the way forward begins by noticing what has been shut down, narrowed, or taken for granted.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening cautions 05:46 Cultural assumptions 12:26 Emotional style and skill 21:58 The crucible of emotion 36:02 From mindfulness to image 51:53 Stretch and mutual dependence 01:03:40 Images and emotion 01:19:31 Wide fields of cause 01:32:51 Practice examples
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58771/

item.title: Opening the Sense of the Energy Body
item.description:
What happens if the energy body is not a fixed thing but a way of sensing that can be opened, widened, and even re-imagined? The talk moves from a practical definition of energy body awareness—felt sense, image, and idea—into a series of adjustments: experimenting rather than fixating, bringing the whole body into contact with the object or other, and noticing how the energy field can become an organ of perception with a more synesthetic quality. It then connects this opening to breath, generosity, devotion, samadhi, and dyadic practice, including the possibility that a shift in one person’s perception can change the whole field.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 02:46 Three aspects of energy 09:43 Experimenting with form 14:05 A nose for essentials 16:57 Whole-body contact 25:31 Opening the field 33:38 Breath, generosity, and opening 48:07 Capacity and path 59:52 Energy as imaginal
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58773/

item.title: The Crucible
item.description:
What kind of vessel can hold an image long enough for it to ferment into something soul-making? The talk moves through that question by way of emotion, vulnerability, and the difference between forcing an image and creating the conditions for it to arrive. It returns again and again to the practical art of receptivity: letting the body lead, staying with what feels truly soulful, and allowing a memory, idea, or encounter to deepen without being flattened or rushed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The crucible 02:57 Emotions and sustainment 11:12 Vulnerability in practice 21:02 Mary and Jacob 33:20 Images from life and art 47:27 How images are received 01:07:33 Art and grace 01:25:52 Finding the eros 01:38:39 Series, patience, and expression
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58783/

item.title: The Way of Image
item.description:
What counts as an image, and when does it become genuinely imaginal? The talk starts by laying out the foundations of Soulmaking Dharma: how ordinary Dharma capacities like mindfulness, metta, samatha, and emotional skill can become roots in their own right, and how clear definitions matter without freezing the practice into something rigid. From there it moves into distinctions that protect the imaginal from being confused with shamanism, energy healing, or ESP, then opens out into many examples of non-visual imagery: compassion sensed through chant, music as a god with a claim on life, a dream of dying soon, a chest opening like a Torah ark, and angelic ministration at the threshold of death.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening frame 06:57 Foundations become roots 11:40 What soul means 17:32 Dyad and boundaries 24:21 Imaginal middle way 31:32 Three kinds of image 44:30 Music as a god 01:00:46 Death, sacredness, land 01:09:10 Soft edges and refinement
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58787/

item.title: Closing Comments From Rob - Soulmaking and Sangha
item.description:
How do you leave a retreat where the heart has been opened, without spilling what feels precious? The talk moves from simple thanks and practical guidance for the days after retreat into a careful warning about sensitivity, enthusiasm, and the need to check the moment and the listener before sharing what has been touched. From there it turns toward the deeper question of how Soulmaking Dharma is meant to be taken up: as something to digest fully, develop, and only then weave into one’s own practice, in solitude and in Sangha.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Thanks 01:23 Leaving Retreat Carefully 03:58 Sharing What Is Precious 07:20 The Gift of Dana 11:43 Imaginal Lineage 13:28 Assimilation and Development 15:43 Sangha and Friendship 17:11 Final Thanks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58609/

item.title: Image, Ethics, and Awakening (Q & A)
item.description:
What happens when ethics, eros, ritual, and imaginal practice are all held in the same room? In this Q&A, the talk moves from precepts as the ground of practice to a broader claim that ethics must keep growing as Soulmaking Dharma deepens, with repeated concern for how images shape action, speech, and responsibility. It also turns practical and personal: ritual, sexual eros, the risks of misunderstanding, the place of service, and a closing question about what kind of life is actually worth living. The tone stays reflective but direct, with several vivid examples and a clear resistance to easy answers.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 06:38 Opening Questions 10:23 Precepts as Basis 13:29 Ethics Deepens 22:42 Ritual and Sacredness 27:15 Imaginal Attention 38:56 Eros and Boundaries 58:15 Dark Night Reframed 01:07:34 The Sister Image 01:18:23 Service and the Path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58612/

item.title: Eros and Desire (Q & A)
item.description:
How does desire stay alive without collapsing into union, craving, or confusion? The talk moves through that question by clarifying twoness, showing why eros depends on retained tension and particularity, and then working back and forth between imaginal practice, emptiness, and the self so the listener can see how each may open into the other. Along the way, the speaker answers worries about narcissism, taboo objects, and overwhelm, and keeps returning to a practical point: the issue is less the object than the quality of the relationship around it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:30 Twoness and relationship 06:31 Eros needs polarity 13:08 Eros for the self 21:45 Imaginal practice and cosmos 37:16 Emptiness opens into image 43:14 Eros and craving 57:10 Body as beloved 01:00:26 Feminine figures of desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58611/

item.title: On Practising with Images (Q & A)
item.description:
What happens when an image starts to include the self, and the self-sense itself begins to feel imaginal? The talk moves through practical questions about how to work with images that are vivid, flat, shifting, or surprisingly erotic, and keeps returning to the need to attune to what feels soulmaking rather than getting lost in story or trying to force a result. It also opens out into how art, beauty, grief, and relationship can all carry this imaginal quality, changing the way one sees oneself and others.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:57 A self seen as image 02:58 How image enters practice 06:27 Divinity, beauty, and culture 12:01 Repeated encounters matter 18:33 Trusting the process 24:12 Art, beauty, and violence 30:04 Boundaries and texture 43:01 Narrative and nectar 56:08 Relationship and desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58613/

item.title: Dukkha and Emotions in Soulmaking Practice
item.description:
How do you work with dukkha when there is no formula, and when what seems like ordinary suffering may need either basic emotional repair or a more imaginal response? The talk moves through a patient, improvised account of anger, depression, energy, and the need to rebuild neglected foundations before anything can become soulmaking. From there it widens into climate grief, sacred responsibility, and the possibility that some inner tensions are not to be harmonized but lived as part of a larger, polytheistic psyche.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 02:23 No formula for dukkha 05:48 Anger and hidden pain 08:22 Foundations first 12:02 Images after repair 14:17 Lattice and leverage 20:26 Crucible of emotion 28:20 Dharma and soul 35:50 Ecological grief and roles 48:21 Polytheistic psyche
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58610/

item.title: A Rite of Angels
item.description:
How do you practice when the mind keeps measuring what’s missing? The talk begins with a quiet return to gratitude: Buddha, Dharma, Sangha, the lineage behind practice, and the many supports that have carried a person to the cushion. It then turns into a more elaborate retreat ritual of walking, watching, chanting, and blessing, where others are met first with welcoming and metta, then increasingly as unique beings, and finally as angel or Buddha nature. The movement is from self-criticism toward a more expansive way of seeing: body, voice, soul, limitation, and potential held together in one field of practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:02 Opening the ritual 02:15 Resting on support 11:42 Setting up the circle 19:19 Seeing the walker 27:00 Chant and form 36:40 Blessing and resonance 46:04 Angel gaze 59:54 Closing vision
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58614/

item.title: The Imaginal-Energy Body (Q & A)
item.description:
How does the body open when practice is no longer limited to bare sensations? The talk moves from a practical, relational beginning on retreat into a careful explanation of the energy body as felt sense, image, and idea, then widens further into imaginal possibility, the roots of soul, and the role of attention in shaping experience. It keeps returning to a simple question: what happens when contraction, eros, and kindness are read as live information rather than fixed states? The final stretch turns to jhana, asking how absorption and imaginal richness can inform one another without collapsing into a single approach.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:07 Retreat opening 06:48 One bite at a time 10:59 What energy body means 15:42 From samadhi to imaginal 22:01 Attunement and development 28:36 Grief, self, and mystery 34:25 Aura, reification, and attention 45:27 Craving and navigation 54:39 Unusual states and soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58615/
