item.title: This Retreat
item.description:
How do you begin a retreat by making room for the whole person, including illness, grief, and uncertainty? The talk opens as a communal welcome, then turns into a frank health update and a collective pause, asking listeners to tend their own hearts and bodies in the presence of mortality. From there it moves into paired sharing, a compassion chant, and an extended practice of moving through the room while sensing the body, the land, and other people as blessed, angelic, or alive with Buddha nature. The talk closes by returning to stillness and framing practice as a way of seeing oneself and others more openly, poetically, and sacredly.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:27 Welcoming the whole person 11:19 A retreat shaped together 18:26 Health, uncertainty, patience 21:29 Meeting mortality as practice 28:21 Compassion chant 33:34 Witnessing in small groups 42:12 Movement and blessing 01:00:08 Stillness and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/58652/

item.title: Of Hunger and Humility
item.description:
How do you keep a practice alive without forcing it? The talk moves from gratitude for the retreat into a careful picture of soulmaking as something natural that grows when it is not blocked by narrow ideas, limited energy, or relational carelessness. It then turns to the practical tension at the center of the teaching: stay hungry for what opens the path, but meet that hunger with discernment, humility, and attention to the vessel that can hold it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:13 Opening Gratitude 02:05 Natural Soulmaking 05:17 Ranges And Openings 08:04 Careful Discernment 10:00 Beauty And Desire 11:57 The Relational Vessel 14:01 Insatiable Yet Careful 15:15 Hunger And Humility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51537/

item.title: Soul Making Dharma (Q & A)
item.description:
What if soul is not a fixed thing but the organ of perceiving soulfully, where experience is both created and discovered at once? In this Q&A, the talk moves through that tension carefully: the looseness of concepts, the role of Logos, the way awakening can be understood as expanding rather than ending, and why language matters for noticing what practice is already doing. It then turns to the pain of soullessness, the healing power of imaginal work, and the delicate balance of effort, receptivity, grace, and energy in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Questions 09:58 What Soul Means 11:15 Create and Discover 14:03 Concepts That Stay Loose 16:33 Awakening Expands 20:21 Dukkha of Soullessness 28:31 Logos and Devotion 31:50 Language and Practice 37:29 Energy and Caution
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51536/

It then turns to the pain of soullessness, the healing power of imaginal work, and the delicate balance of effort, receptivity, grace, and energy in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Questions 09:58 What Soul Means 11:15 Create and Discover 14:03 Concepts That Stay Loose 16:33 Awakening Expands 20:21 Dukkha of Soullessness 28:31 Logos and Devotion 31:50 Language and Practice 37:29 Energy and Caution
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51536/

item.title: Sharing Soul Sense (A Temenos Exercise)
item.description:
How does a shared practice stay intimate without becoming performative? The talk unfolds as a staged relational exercise: people form groups, ground in the body, offer metta, then take turns expressing what is happening in the energy body, the heart, and, later, something that has touched the soul, while others witness silently and track their own responses. Again and again, the emphasis is on mutual effect, on not narrowing experience to only the pleasant or only the difficult, and on keeping goodwill as the quiet basis underneath it all. Near the end, the exercise is named as a relational temenos—a protected space for what is sacred—so the whole talk moves toward careful, reverent, embodied sharing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:15 Setting Up the Exercise 03:55 Grounding and Metta 09:24 First Round: Energy Body 12:29 Witnessing and Response 25:40 Second Round: Heart and Emotion 29:06 Metta as Background 40:09 Soul Desire and Image 49:20 Soulful Sharing 52:25 Closing in Reverence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51535/

item.title: The Spreading of Five Wings
item.description:
What if practice is less about fixing experience than widening its range? The talk gathers five directions of possible expansion: the elements in the constellation, the play of self/other/world/eros, the eros-psyche-logos dynamic, the spectrum from dark or difficult material to light and pure, and the texture of the energy body from subtle to dense. It keeps returning to a simple inquiry: where is the range open, where is it narrowed, and what habits or fears may be shaping that narrowing?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Why Expansion Matters 01:55 The Five Domains 04:00 Self, Other, World 06:03 Eros and Logos 09:11 Dark and Light 11:20 Energy Body Texture 14:16 Imaginal Self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51534/

item.title: Sailing the Oceans of Soul (Q & A)
item.description:
What happens when an image starts to fade: is that emptiness, loss of clinging, or a sign to turn the attention differently? The talk moves through a practical Q&A on imaginal practice, from reframing the Heart Sutra as “form is imaginal, imaginal form” to noticing how eros, discernment, and what the speaker calls the “lens” can either dissolve an image or bring it back into richer detail. It then widens out to movement, stillness, sound, concepts becoming image, and the different ways form, energy, and imagination can interpenetrate without forcing one fixed method.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:49 Heart Sutra Reframed 01:36 Why Images Fade 03:35 Modulating the Fading 04:02 Eros Restores Form 05:06 Particulars and Fluidity 06:04 Form and Emptiness Edge 09:10 Universal Figures 14:42 Discernment Deepens Practice 21:33 Movement and Stillness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51532/

item.title: Wisdom and Desire
item.description:
What if the real question is not how to get rid of desire, but how to relate to it so it shapes life, practice, and world with more wisdom? The talk starts by challenging the usual Buddhist simplification that desire is mainly something to suppress, then moves into a more nuanced map: craving versus eros, and the ways desire can either contract experience or open it into beauty, mystery, and sacredness. From there it becomes increasingly practical, asking how meditation, intention, body awareness, and clearer language can help desire flower rather than collapse into grasping.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Desire Shapes Everything 02:10 Beyond Simple Renunciation 03:58 A Wider Psychology 12:03 Practice and Soulmaking 19:29 Craving Into Eros 27:01 From Flatness to Imaginal Depth 35:41 Dependent Arising of Desire 40:39 Sacredness Beyond Objects 51:42 Closing Distinction
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51531/

item.title: Sensitivity, Receptivity, and Respect in Soul Work (Q & A)
item.description:
What happens when an image is not a private mental picture but an autonomous presence that can deepen, surprise, and even reshape a life? The talk begins by distinguishing narrative images from more timeless, iconic ones, then moves through examples of intensity, disappointment, and the quieter work of staying in relationship with what arises. From there it opens into eros, inspiration, long-term devotion, and the question of whether a life can itself be understood as expressing images rather than merely causing them.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:14 Narrative and iconic images 06:58 The Dartmoor example 08:23 The concertina field 12:26 Intensity and jhāna 20:42 Experiment without judgment 25:43 Eros and inspiration 36:11 Devotion through difficulty 40:43 Life seen from beyond death 49:38 Autonomy and causality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51530/

item.title: Voice, Movement, and the Possibilities of Soul (2)
item.description:
What happens if movement, stillness, voice, and image are treated as things to explore rather than choices to get right? The talk keeps returning to that question through a series of guided experiments: rooting through the feet, loosening the body, stomping, a warrior posture, subtle breathing gestures, a throat hiss, and simple head rolling, each time asking what is actually helpful and what the body, energy, or imagination notices. Near the end, it opens into imaginal practice more explicitly, inviting a presence or image into relationship with the energy body and suggesting that stillness or movement may each deepen the encounter in different ways.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Choosing the practice 04:59 Rooted movement 08:47 Stomping and awareness 13:36 Warrior in space 18:43 Reciprocal space 24:27 Wider horizons 29:08 Voice and subtle motion 36:32 Earth and stars 45:42 Imaginal relationship
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51528/

item.title: Towards the Imaginal
item.description:
Can an image be trusted enough to stay with it, without reducing it to a message or a proof? The talk moves from a practical invitation to listen actively on retreat into a flexible map of practice: samadhi, the energy body, metta, mindfulness, and imaginal work, all treated as an open buffet rather than a fixed sequence. From there it develops a detailed account of what makes something truly imaginal: relational depth, love, beauty, trust, autonomy, two-ness, and a sense of soulmaking that can include dukkha rather than excluding it. It closes by widening the frame again, using theatre and the Buddhist middle way to argue for a disciplined openness to what is neither simply real nor not real.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Listening Proactively 01:46 A Flexible Practice Map 04:32 Working Through Doubt 06:55 Soulmaking as Measure 12:26 What Imaginal Means 18:22 Love and Deliberate Cultivation 22:43 The Full Constellation 29:05 Imaginal Relationship 38:01 Theatre and Middle Way
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51523/

item.title: Trust, Ethics, Image (Q & A)
item.description:
How do you work with aggression, erotic charge, and the fear that imaginal practice might spill into ordinary life? The talk answers by moving from caution and self-inhibition toward a slower, more spacious way of relating to images: holding them in metta, noticing their energy-body resonance, and letting them become less literal and more dimensional. A second question takes up whether an image that feels beautiful but unfinished should be pushed further or left alone, and the response keeps returning to pacing, grace, and not pressuring the psyche. Across the Q&A, the central thread is that trust grows gradually, and what seems dangerous or egoic may open into something more sacred when it is neither flattened nor forced.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 The question of aggression 02:33 Healthy doubt 04:46 Beyond repression 09:23 Trusting the imaginal 12:24 Warrior and seduction 17:10 A question of navigation 21:16 Grace and receptivity 24:36 Images and ethics 28:39 Middle way of images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51520/

item.title: Voice, Movement, and the Possibilities of Soul (1)
item.description:
What happens when movement, voice, and gesture are treated not as extras, but as ways the imaginal and the energy body can come alive? The talk begins by reframing sitting and walking practice around subtle and not-so-subtle movement, then moves into a series of guided experiments: loosening the body, a drunken walk, a fierce warrior expression, a delicate head tilt, a small smile, hissing, and finally paired exercises of witnessing and being witnessed. Across it all, the emphasis stays on sensitivity rather than performance, and on noticing how body, attention, and relationship condition one another.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:14 Opening the exploration 04:28 Why move this way 09:03 Finding your edge 13:34 First movement games 24:28 From gross to subtle 31:11 Small smile and hissing 41:42 Pair witnessing 53:29 Balance and metta
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Heart Art and an Introduction to Image
item.description:
How do emotion, self, and image change the way experience unfolds? The talk begins with energy-body practice and then widens into several ways of meeting difficulty: resting in samadhi, letting dukkha be held in metta, allowing emotion to reveal its own intelligence, and noticing when a self-sense shifts into image. A long example of confusion, emptiness, and then an inner music image shows how suffering can remain present while taking on a more personal, poetic, and holy shape. The second half turns more explicitly to what 'image' means here: not just visual fantasy, but a relational mode of perception that can arise through any sense and that is judged by whether it is soul-making.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:11 Two aims for the session 05:35 Samadhi and difficulty 11:27 Self, story, and healing 13:24 Image meets dukkha 22:36 Holy roots of feeling 28:30 Dynamism and movement 30:49 What image means 46:02 Not every image is imaginal 50:52 How to invite images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51518/

item.title: Growing the Energy Body (Q & A)
item.description:
How do you work with the energy body without turning practice into a fixed method? The talk moves through practical questions about starting with simple awareness, using qualities like peace, happiness, and pīti, and then noticing when it helps to shift attention toward image, emotion, or samadhi. It also asks when to stay with what already works and when to explore what feels awkward, including the possibility of a shared or collective energy body in the room.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the inquiry 02:51 Qualities and tuning 05:28 Working at the edges 10:43 Staying or shifting 16:27 The imaginal fork 21:27 Collective field 26:08 Mysterious resonance 29:26 Wider sensitivity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51515/

item.title: The Energy Body (A Little Bit of What, Why, and How)
item.description:
What changes when the body is taken not as a set of sensations, but as a field of energy, texture, vibration, and image? The talk begins with a simple guided exploration of posture, breath, and open awareness, then widens into why this way of paying attention matters for samadhi, metta, insight, and imaginal practice. From there, it turns to the larger question of how concepts and images shape perception, and why staying flexible with them can open a much broader range of experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:04 Introducing the energy body 02:11 What it feels like 06:12 Posture, space, breath 11:26 Light, metta, and emotion 16:06 Why it matters 20:56 Stillness and practice forks 25:46 A wider definition 39:30 Intention and range
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Opening Talk ('To Kiss a Falcon')
item.description:
What if the foundations of practice are not a fixed doctrine, but a willingness to question what we think we already know? The talk introduces Soulmaking Dharma as something to be condensed, distilled, and approached through skills, qualities, and understandings rather than through an exhaustive list of rules. It then moves into a wider reflection on art, beauty, and the need for discernment, using a birthday poem and a brief reference to John Coltrane to show how something can change our sense of value and belonging without stating a thesis outright.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the Foundations 03:56 Questioning Assumptions 06:39 Opening the Field 09:46 Art, Truth, and Reality 12:35 The Birthday Poem 16:51 Culture and Crisis 19:50 Discernment and Precision 25:18 Playing the Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/51511/

item.title: Nothing Happens By Itself
item.description:
Nothing happens by itself: the talk opens with that claim and unfolds into a practical question about what actually helps soulmaking arise. The speaker names care, attention, and the right conditions as the “temenos” — an enclosed, protected vessel — and shows how even brief imaginal contact can matter when it is properly held. From there, the emphasis shifts from solitary practice to relationship, where images and soulfulness need timing, formality, and mutual care if they are to be shared without being distorted or lost. It ends by returning to an open question: what supports this work, and what gets in its way?
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Nothing Happens Alone 01:48 The Temenos 03:01 Images and Samadhi 04:14 Soulmaking in Relationship 05:06 Creating the Vessel 06:38 Honoring What Is Precious 07:48 An Open Question
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50527/

item.title: Discernment in Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
When does an image, an experience, or an idea become soulmaking—and when does insisting on a fixed meaning flatten it? This Q&A moves from the practical question of how to work with imaginal figures, through the difference between active and receptive image work, to the larger issue of whether something feels soulmaking even if it begins with effort, ambiguity, or a changing sense of autonomy. Later, the talk opens into eros, sexuality, and ontology, asking how desire can turn from horizontal craving toward more dimensional, spacious, and alive experience.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 What Is Imaginal? 03:03 Genealogy of the Term 07:02 Image as Perception 17:01 Soulmaking and Skill 24:43 Active and Receptive 30:32 Autonomy and Trust 34:02 Eros and Sexuality 40:46 Horizontal Desire 46:52 Ontology and Reality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50525/

item.title: The Rhythms of Soulmaking and Elements of the Imaginal (23 - 28)
item.description:
What if soulmaking does not arrive on command, but comes and goes with conditions, like any other practice state? The talk moves through that rhythm first, easing pressure and self-judgment, then widens into a practical map: a palette of practices that nourish the being, a middle way between forcing and neglect, and the sense that blocks can sometimes break open suddenly or shift only gradually. From there it turns to specific imaginal nodes—fullness of intention, duty, meaningfulness, echoing, values, and participation—showing how an image can carry both personal history and something larger than the self, even when the form fades. A few vivid examples, including Aslan and music arising from meditative stillness, help the talk circle back to beauty, nobility, and the possibility of participating in a much wider imaginal world.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the frame 00:57 Self-judgment and conditions 02:58 Pressure and the middle way 04:50 The palette of practice 06:15 Rhythms and blocks 12:45 Fullness of intention 17:12 Duty and its flavor 20:29 Meaning, echoes, and values 29:18 Participation and participation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50524/

What if soulmaking does not arrive on command, but comes and goes with conditions, like any other practice state? The talk moves through that rhythm first, easing pressure and self-judgment, then widens into a practical map: a palette of practices that nourish the being, a middle way between forcing and neglect, and the sense that blocks can sometimes break open suddenly or shift only gradually. From there it turns to specific imaginal nodes—fullness of intention, duty, meaningfulness, echoing, values, and participation—showing how an image can carry both personal history and something larger than the self, even when the form fades. A few vivid examples, including Aslan and music arising from meditative stillness, help the talk circle back to beauty, nobility, and the possibility of participating in a much wider imaginal world.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the frame 00:57 Self-judgment and conditions 02:58 Pressure and the middle way 04:50 The palette of practice 06:15 Rhythms and blocks 12:45 Fullness of intention 17:12 Duty and its flavor 20:29 Meaning, echoes, and values 29:18 Participation and participation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50524/

item.title: A Temenos Exercise (in threes)
item.description:
How can awareness stay open to self, other, and the living field between them? The talk begins with a guided exercise in threes: grounding in the body, widening into energy-body awareness, and bringing metta into the space of meeting another person with open eyes and senses. From there it moves into rounds of speaking and witnessing, with careful attention to how expression, listening, emotion, and the act of being seen change the moment itself. Near the end, the frame opens wider again through a chant and the reminder that practice can include everything—light and dark, stillness and movement, sound and silence.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 01:05 Settling Into Ground 02:10 Opening the Energy Body 03:40 Sensing Self and Others 05:47 Metta in the Circle 08:36 First Round of Sharing 18:45 Dependent Origination in Dialogue 27:06 Second Round: Emotions 32:05 Reverberation and Soul 47:00 Primordial Buddha Chant
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50523/

item.title: Destiny, Autonomy, Love, and Soul (Q & A)
item.description:
How do duty, destiny, autonomy, and love fit together when an image begins to shape a life? The talk starts from a question about duty and gradually opens into a careful account of soulmaking: image, eros, and logos mutually affect one another, and what feels like a calling is both received and participated in. It then turns to darker or terrifying images, asking how to tell what is psychologically real, what is imaginal, and how trust, love, and conceptual framework change what can be met. The closing movement returns to loving and being loved by an image, showing how that relationship can become subtler, more alive, and more person-like.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Question 03:29 Duty and Soulmaking 10:33 Destiny and Choice 17:48 Subtlety and Dark Images 24:59 Conceptual Framework 29:43 Loving and Being Loved 33:54 Personhood of the Image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50522/

item.title: Elements of the Imaginal (15 - 18)
item.description:
What does it mean for an image, a gesture, or a bodily sense to become soul-making without turning it into just another object of thought? The talk moves quickly through four nodes: non-reducibility, autonomy, twoness, and the theatre-like middle way, while also pausing on grace, the shifting relation between imaginal practice and other forms of practice, and the way reductionism flattens experience. Near the end, the emphasis settles on a distinctive kind of reality that is neither simply real nor unreal, and on the tension and distinctness that eros preserves rather than erasing.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and context 04:59 Grace as gift 06:02 Moving between practices 09:14 Nodes are interwoven 11:04 Against reductionism 15:17 Images as primary 17:58 Double autonomy 20:52 Twoness and eros 24:52 Theatre and middle way
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50520/

item.title: Soulmaking Rivers (Q & A)
item.description:
What if the question is not whether God, soul, or imaginal life are invented, but how far that question can be taken? The talk begins with a difficult relationship to divine language, then moves into a careful unpacking of fabrication, emptiness, and the sense that self, meaning, and even godlessness may all need to be investigated rather than assumed. From there it widens into a discussion of temenos, shared image, and how imaginal practice can either loosen into the unfabricated or deepen into a particular, soul-making richness without collapsing into abstraction.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 01:30 God-language and resistance 07:11 Mystery, value, and poetry 14:01 What is fabricated? 21:10 Temenos and shared image 25:40 Open image libraries 31:34 Soul-making and suffering 36:15 Paths toward the unfabricated 42:43 Images fading, essence remaining
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50519/

item.title: A Temenos Exercise (in fours)
item.description:
How do you stay rooted in your own body while meeting three other people with kindness, then let what you feel become something you can actually share? The talk unfolds as a timed group exercise: first settling into the energy body and mutual mettā, then expressing present-moment emotional experience through words, voice, gesture, or movement, while the others witness and notice how they themselves are affected. Near the end, the frame widens again into silence, reflection, and a short Pali blessing for all beings.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Settling and Metta 05:35 Explaining the Choreography 12:18 First Round of Sharing 23:07 Second Stage: Emotional Expression 39:32 After-Effects and Resonance 45:51 Deepening the Witness 58:06 Return to Goodwill 01:01:08 Pali Blessing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50516/

item.title: Transformation in Soul (Q & A)
item.description:
What if the move is not to choose between empty self and healed self, but to see how self can be shaped, loosened, and opened in different ways? In this question-and-answer session, the talk begins with a listener’s uncertainty about images of self, healing, and emptiness, then works through a three-way contrast: letting go of self, building a sturdier self, and sensing the imaginal as a third possibility. From there it turns to anger, grief, and even ordinary boundary-setting, asking how strong emotion can become a doorway to soul rather than just a problem to manage.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:24 Images, healing, emptiness 02:34 What is being healed? 04:46 Divine uniqueness 08:27 Multiplicity and freedom 12:21 Emptiness and play 14:58 All directions are good 18:25 Anger, grief, image 25:30 Sharpness without cruelty 30:19 What soul means
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50515/

item.title: Soulful Movement and Elements of the Imaginal (6 - 10)
item.description:
What if stillness is only one way to discover soul, and movement, sound, and subtle bodily energy are another? The talk opens by questioning the retreat culture of silence, then turns to how imaginal perception can be deepened through noticing, deliberate activation, and a little grain of trust. From there it moves into soft edges, dimensionality, and the sense that images and beings are never just flat appearances, before setting that view beside broader Buddhist framings of emptiness, mindfulness, and fabrication.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Movement and silence 04:09 Opening practice wider 09:12 Seeds, not dogma 15:30 Trust and activation 20:16 Soulmaking and edges 26:18 Dimensionality and divinity 33:10 Three Dharma framings 37:37 Fabrication and freedom 42:57 Closing quiet
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50514/

item.title: Trusting in Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
What if the path is less about getting a practice exactly right and more about learning how soulmaking actually moves through the whole person? In this Q&A, the talk ranges from chanting, movement, and language as sparks for the energy body to the way imaginal perception can shift between embodiment, two-ness, and oneness. It then turns to the harder questions of how this fits with Buddhadharma, why some concepts need fuzzy edges to stay alive, and how to trust a felt compass even when clarity is still emerging.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the field 02:05 Language and energy 04:46 What can spark soulmaking 09:02 Broadening beyond one doorway 10:37 Two-ness in embodiment 17:54 Trusting and refining 19:00 Emptiness and imagination 23:20 Why it stays open 30:43 Clarity with fuzzy edges
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50512/

item.title: To Sense with Soul
item.description:
What if perception is not just seeing what is there, but sensing the world with added depth, aliveness, and relationship? The talk pauses the larger retreat teaching to clarify the phrase “sensing with soul,” contrasting it with the idea of imaginal life as merely private inner imagery. From there, it widens the frame through the Buddha Dharma idea of mind as a sixth sense, then turns to emptiness to show how words like soul and divinity can be used without turning them into fixed entities.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Why the Term Matters 03:11 From Imaginal to Soulful 05:58 The World Filled Out 07:59 What Soul Means 09:28 Empty Words, Useful Language 10:57 Making the Instrument
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50511/

item.title: Elements of the Imaginal (1 - 2)
item.description:
What makes an image more than fantasy, and where does the imaginal begin? The talk opens by distinguishing ordinary imagination and papanca from mindful uses of image in death contemplation, metta, and compassion, then moves into a careful working definition of the imaginal as a spectrum of qualities rather than a yes-or-no category. From there it sketches the first nodes of a larger lattice — energy body, love, eros, beauty, trust, soulmaking, dimensionality, beyondness, reverence, humility, autonomy, twoness, theatre, and more — and shows how noticing or activating one node can open the whole field. The emphasis stays practical: less on fixed doctrine than on giving practitioners a set of distinctions that can deepen how they enter relationship with image.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 02:58 Skillful imagination 05:16 What imaginal means 09:39 The first nodes 18:08 A constellation of qualities 23:57 Why the distinctions matter 27:42 Openings and entry points 30:12 Lattice and energy body
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50509/

item.title: Body and Soul (Q & A)
item.description:
How far can the idea of the body open before it starts to change the kind of practice you’re doing? The talk moves from simple questions about whether organs or body regions can have their own intelligence, into a broader explanation of the energy body as a stretchable field that can be subtle, intense, pleasant, dense, or imaginal. From there it turns to resistance and clinging, showing how relaxing contraction makes experience more fluid, and then to samadhi and the jhanas as a spectrum of body-based well-being. It ends by tying energy-body awareness to soulmaking, where image, felt sense, and concept can all work together or separate into different practice modes.
Location: Freely Given Retreats
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Q&A 01:32 Many Intelligences in the Body 04:06 Growing the Energy Body 06:51 Resistance as Clinging 09:27 Different Energetic Fields 15:12 Samadhi Direction 18:22 Jhana as Spectrum 22:26 Care and Navigation 25:08 Soulmaking, Image, and Opening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50508/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 8 - Talking with Trees)
item.description:
How do perception, speech, and love change when a tree is no longer just an object, but a being that can be met with reverence? The talk moves from body and earth as sacred texts, through examples of trees, stones, and imaginal figures, into a sustained inquiry into how ethical speech and attentive practice refine sensitivity. From there it broadens into questions of values, aesthetics, morality, and the way love can reveal a particular being’s hidden depth without reducing it to a concept or use. It ends by rereading Genesis as a call to leave what is no longer soul-making and to step into a wider dimensionality of life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Body, earth, and icon 05:57 Suzuki and paradise 08:07 How trees communicate 26:41 Speech and sensitivity 34:40 Reverence, culture, and doubt 49:40 Love sees more deeply 01:01:18 Eros, art, and knowing 01:23:09 Values and antinomies 01:46:44 Loneliness and the call
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50484/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 7 - The Scripture of Body and Earth)
item.description:
What if the body, a sound, or a remembered image were not just objects to notice, but texts to be interpreted, and reinterpreted, by the heart? The talk moves from ideas and emotions in practice into a close exploration of how sensing with soul can widen what body, matter, and reality seem to be, using examples from energy-body work, imaginal practice, and a long retreat report about a nun image, grief, and healing. It then turns outward to hermeneutics, sacred scripture, tantra, Sámi yoik, and the three bodies of the Buddha, asking how a more participatory way of listening can make the world feel less flat and more alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Ideas and the Soul 05:04 Eros and the Imaginal Body 10:41 Playing With Body Images 17:34 Three Layers of Body 27:34 Hermeneutics of Existence 37:18 Retreat Report in Practice 50:57 Sound, Prayer, and Yoik 01:07:41 Bodies of the Buddha 01:21:55 Praise and Healing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50483/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 6 - Amitabha Buddha)
item.description:
What if a sacred image, a scripture phrase, or even a name like Amitabha were not something to explain away, but a seed to let gestate in the soul? The talk moves from poetic hearing and the role of ideas in practice into Amitabha, his consort imagery, and the way “limitless light” can also be heard as limitless appearance, opening a field of imaginal perception rather than only a path toward oneness. It then ranges through mantra, deity yoga, Hakuin, Abulafia, and Dharmadhatu to show how emptiness, devotion, and interpretation can transfigure this very world into something like a pure land.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:57 Poetic Hearing 10:38 Amitabha Reconsidered 16:10 Consort And Wisdom 22:17 Deity Yoga Stances 27:49 Pure Land Here Now 36:37 Balance With Akshobhya 46:20 Scripture As Living Land 59:19 Dharmadhatu And World 01:12:49 Yearning Opens Gates
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50482/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 5 - Bodhicitta)
item.description:
What does it really mean to “live life to the full” if that phrase only points to a flat, self-conscious kind of presence? The talk begins with a retreat walk story and then keeps widening the question, asking what mindfulness leaves out when it excludes depth, image, value, and the sense that life has more dimensions than the merely sensory. From there it moves into bodhicitta, Buddha-nature, and the way ideals, images, and devotion can shape a person’s life without collapsing into a generic spiritual goal. The talk closes by bringing the discussion back to something personal: how values, images, and an “angel out ahead” can help a unique life take form.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Living life fully? 01:53 Limits of modern mindfulness 07:16 Beyond flat presence 13:32 World and transcendence 19:43 Meaning, death, and beauty 28:40 Bodhicitta and image 38:29 Buddha-nature as matrix 48:27 Personal blossoming
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50481/

The talk closes by bringing the discussion back to something personal: how values, images, and an “angel out ahead” can help a unique life take form.

Chapters: 00:01 Living life fully? 01:53 Limits of modern mindfulness 07:16 Beyond flat presence 13:32 World and transcendence 19:43 Meaning, death, and beauty 28:40 Bodhicitta and image 38:29 Buddha-nature as matrix 48:27 Personal blossoming
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50481/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 4 - The Three Characteristics)
item.description:
What if the three characteristics are not doctrines to memorize, but lenses you can actually pick up and put down in practice? The talk moves from dependent origination and attention on vedana into a close reconsideration of dukkha, impermanence, and not-self as ways of looking that change perception, reduce fabrication, and can even open into emptiness. From there it widens into a quieter, more expansive question: whether perception itself can be worked with, healed, and eventually seen as participatory, sacred, or divine.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Ideas Become Ways of Looking 14:15 Dependent Origination Revisited 19:33 Working with Vedanā 25:11 Relative Truth and History 29:56 Three Marks Introduced 34:12 Lenses, Not Doctrines 42:18 Turning Toward Their Opposites 50:44 Perception as Practice 01:01:15 Deconstruction and Reconstruction
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50480/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 3)
item.description:
What if the point of practice is not to secure one true picture of reality, but to notice how every picture is shaped by desire, feeling, and angle of view? The talk moves from Nietzsche’s perspectivism to stories of Burmese teachers, then into a critique of reductionist meditation styles that deconstruct beauty, emotion, and the self in order to stay unshaken. From there it asks what emptiness, truth, and care of the soul might mean if there is no single objective view to cling to, and whether practice can open into a wider, more soulful range of sensing rather than a narrower one.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Pluralism and perspective 03:07 Knowing is interpretive 12:18 Practice and emotional scope 17:52 Reductionism in meditation 23:17 Beauty, soul, and humility 29:45 Power behind ideas 40:09 Truth and care of self 47:55 Opening the maps 55:09 Space for soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50479/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 2)
item.description:
If mindfulness is meant to reveal what is true, what happens when the very way of looking is already shaping what appears? The talk moves from Husserl’s critique of positivism and his idea of the life-world into a wider argument that experience, attention, and interpretation are never neutral, but always carried by assumptions about reality, knowledge, and value. It then turns to Midrash and Jewish interpretive practice as a model of participation: truth is not something observed from a distance, but something made alive through debate, care, and the willingness to let what matters shape understanding.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why ideas matter 05:24 Scientific inheritance 10:07 Husserl and crisis 13:10 The life-world 17:49 Apperances and reduction 35:37 Ways of looking 59:13 No raw data 01:20:39 Plurality and participation 01:30:18 Interpretation must matter
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50478/

item.title: Between Ikon and Eidos: Image & Hermeneutics in Meditation (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the chief obstacle to sensing with soul is not lack of practice, but the ideas that quietly decide what counts as real? The talk moves from a case for grounding imaginal perception to a broad critique of modern scientific materialism, tracing how assumptions about reality, mind, and matter shape what practitioners feel able to notice or trust. Along the way it brings in quantum physics, Pauli, Jung, and William James to ask how concepts can either open experience or narrow it into “nothing but” explanations. It closes by turning that question back toward Dharma practice, especially the limits of reductionist readings, emotion-free ideals, and the absence of eros and soul-making.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Grounding Matters 06:17 Ideas in Experience 12:01 Eidos and Interpretation 20:18 Science and Reality 32:04 Pauli, Jung, Symbols 40:15 Abstraction and Reduction 51:05 The Moon Story 59:00 Epistemicide and Value 01:14:33 Dharma and Eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50477/

item.title: What is Awakening? (Part 4)
item.description:
What if awakening is not a single breakthrough, but a widening process in which fabrication, perception, eros, and ideas keep opening and being reworked? The talk moves from a phenomenological inquiry into fabrication and ways of looking, to a critique of the myth of sudden, total enlightenment, and then into the soulmaking paradigm, where awakening includes deconstruction, reconstruction, and the ongoing creation of more spacious images and concepts. Along the way, it questions fixed ideas of liberation, brings in philosophy and theology, and ends by reading Genesis as a call to leave stagnation and keep moving into new territory.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Fabrication and looking 03:30 A possible awakening arc 07:15 Why linear maps fail 10:05 Soulmaking joins in 16:47 The myth of sudden awakening 19:55 Freedom in wider domains 25:38 Concepts must be rebuilt 39:45 Idols and icons 54:02 Go from here
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50503/

item.title: What is Awakening? (Part 3)
item.description:
What if awakening is not a finished state, but a question that keeps opening? The talk moves from debating stream-entry and ethical standards, through a critique of pure origins in Buddhist tradition, into a broader account of awakening as a trajectory of changing ways of looking: fabrication, emptiness, non-duality, and a participatory view that no single framework can close down. In the final arc, a personal cancer diagnosis leads into four practical perspectives that make a real difference in relation to dying: the unfabricated, emptiness felt with mystery and sacredness, imaginal sensing with soul, and a sense of soul duty.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Questioning the Bar 05:21 Ethics and Awakening 15:11 What Awakening Means 16:53 Tradition and Genealogy 27:29 Western Dharma Questions 33:44 Fabrication and Emptiness 40:30 Open-Ended Awakening 48:15 Facing Death Personally 57:20 Doors and Gates
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50502/

item.title: What is Awakening? (Part 2)
item.description:
What if awakening is less a fixed achievement than a way of understanding how self, world, and experience get fabricated? The talk begins by questioning easy, modern readings of stream-entry—like “going with the flow”—and then moves through the Pali Canon’s more demanding language: the ten fetters, sakkāya-diṭṭhi, the opening of the Dharma eye, dependent origination, and the unfabricated. From there it widens into a careful comparison of cessation experiences, gradual insight, and the ways different views of awakening shape practice and the kind of person one becomes.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Many views of awakening 05:20 Against easy definitions 11:18 The first fetters 23:56 Opening the Dharma eye 27:14 The unfabricated and emptiness 36:57 Cessation and discernment 48:25 How insight can move 58:16 Raising the bar 01:10:17 What a soul question asks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50501/

item.title: What is Awakening? (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the first question is not whether awakening exists, but why you want it? The talk begins by opening up the many meanings of awakening and then keeps turning back to motive: measuring up, pleasing authority, or longing for something more genuine. From there it moves through jhana practice, the pressure of spiritual labels like stream entry, and the way tradition, culture, and inner critic can quietly shape what we think liberation should be. The talk then shifts toward a more open-ended possibility, asking whether awakening might be less a final badge and more a living inquiry into freedom, beauty, and soulfulness. Along the way it uses Buddhist references, everyday examples, and a few pointed stories to keep testing what is actually being sought.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 05:17 Beyond the Usual Definition 16:24 Authority and Achievement 20:28 Kindness and the Inner Critic 28:00 Jhana and Motivation 38:06 What Do You Want? 51:46 Training and Rebirth 59:56 Happiness and Finality 01:07:25 Open-Ended Awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50500/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 8)
item.description:
What if a troubling image is not only a trace of past damage, but also a doorway into soul? The talk begins with a close look at a student’s early fantasies and then keeps widening the frame: first through Hillman’s reading of Persephone, then through the claim that our ideas of causality shape what we notice, what we fear, and what we conclude. From there it moves into a broader challenge to narrow psychological stories, bringing in birth, environment, culture, and several different models of development to ask whether we can hold more than one lens at once.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 05:09 The student example 15:37 Persephone and the underworld 19:41 Opening the lens 29:37 Causality as perception 42:43 Beyond the private past 50:27 Competing psychologies 01:02:35 Truth and theory 01:14:47 Plurality of frameworks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50492/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 7)
item.description:
What if the problem is not only suffering itself, but a flat way of seeing that leaves out soul, beauty, and eros? The talk traces how imaginal perception can re-enchant dukkha, how being seen or sensing others with soul can change pain in relationship, and how depression may sometimes be tied to a cut-off from that dimension. From there it opens into larger questions: whether suffering is best understood as something caused in the past, or as part of an open-ended movement toward soulmaking, divinity, and a different reading of the Four Noble Truths.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Imaginal relief 02:33 Being seen fully 07:57 Flatness and depression 12:52 Re-enchanting dukkha 19:45 Imaginal and divine 30:07 Hegel and the negative 42:20 Questions of why 57:37 Four Noble Truths revisited 01:13:02 Soulmaking as the worthwhile
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50491/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 6)
item.description:
How does dukkha change when it is not treated as a problem to be solved, but sensed until it can open into image? The talk moves from the basic distinction between pain and the extra “second arrows” of contraction, into a fuller account of imaginal perception, soulmaking, and the role of intention, assent, and receptivity in that process. It then turns practical again: the speaker explores when emptiness or acceptance are enough, when healing image or action is called for, and how illness, mortality, and social suffering can each become places of depth rather than fixed meaning.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Dukkha Meets Image 02:26 Sensing With Soul 06:20 Tool Among Other Tools 14:31 Second Arrow And Intention 25:37 Perfection, Gift, And Assent 40:10 Mundus Imaginalis Opens 57:00 Illness As Threshold 01:12:02 Healing And Action 01:26:37 Image, Fantasy, And Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50490/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 5)
item.description:
What if the thing that feels like dukkha is not only a problem to solve, but a distorted relationship to an image waiting to be revealed? The talk moves from the question of how imaginal practice can relieve suffering to a series of concrete examples: a difficult work situation, a painful relational longing, and ways a person’s history or self-image can open when seen with soul. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to a careful distinction between taking care of actual conditions and allowing the imaginal to change the felt meaning, pressure, and direction of the situation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 From Dukkha to Image 04:03 Intimacy With Suffering 09:28 Alive in the Moment 12:28 Foucault as Spark 16:53 Situation, Duty, and Soul 25:57 Mutual Arising 29:52 Imaginal Communication 33:41 Eros and the Beloved 44:56 Image at the Core
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50489/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 4 - 'Mastering the Fire')
item.description:
What if the very fire of desire is the thing that needs to be learned, not suppressed? The talk moves through a careful set of distinctions—craving, devotional yearning, practical desire, and eros—while asking how to tell when fire contracts the being and when it opens into soul-making. From there it widens into questions of intensity, discipline, containment, and authenticity: what kind of desire is actually mine, what kind is inherited, and what kind can be held without turning into dukkha?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the theme 02:34 Eros and craving 06:38 Four kinds of desire 15:26 Discernment and context 26:43 Authentic desire 33:20 Tolerance and intensity 46:05 Discipline and containment 01:07:45 Ritual and symbolic fire 01:30:24 Limits of mastery
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50488/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 3)
item.description:
What happens when an image is beautiful, meaningful, and still carries dukkha? The talk continues its close look at realism in imaginal practice, showing how images can be over-literalized into self-judgment, prediction, or duty, and how that rigidity can produce swings between grandiosity and self-abasement, even religious or secular dogmatism. From there, the speaker turns to personal examples of creative loneliness, changing archetypal figures over time, and the way imaginal work can open an image without trying to fix it by force. The later movement widens into harder questions: what healing and liberation mean, how many callings can coexist, and whether eros points to reality, creates it, or participates in it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Realism and dukkha 07:20 Polarized swings 13:28 Creative loneliness 27:17 Images evolve 33:02 Images ask for images 38:10 Healing and liberation 45:17 Impossibility and duty 01:04:48 Full intention, less craving 01:31:47 Auden and ontology
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50487/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 2)
item.description:
What does it take for soulmaking practice to deepen instead of simply generating more movement, more intensity, or more dukkha? The talk keeps returning to pacing: when to open, when to stay with one image or desire, and how to balance imaginal work with steadier practices like samadhi, metta, mindfulness, and emptiness. It also addresses common worries about danger, fantasy, and losing touch with reality, including a discussion of Jungian sub-personalities, the Kabbalistic idea of the breaking of the vessels, and why questioning can be part of growth rather than a threat to it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the path 03:40 Pacing and steadiness 07:10 Retreat balance 10:54 Two practice currents 14:27 Working with fear 21:22 Imaginal figures and autonomy 26:38 Breaking and rebuilding 31:42 Questioning and sexual imaginal 37:23 Darker images and realism
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50486/

item.title: Dukkha and Soulmaking (Part 1)
item.description:
What makes a path sustainable when imaginal practice, desire, and suffering all start to touch each other? The talk moves from the scope of dukkha and soulmaking into a careful map of what this work asks: emotional awareness, body and energy-body sensitivity, the ability to discern eros from craving, and enough insight to know when to let go and when to stay with what is present. It then turns to the emotional life in detail, including different ways of relating to self, the need for flexibility around psychological frameworks, and how the earlier seven instructions can reveal the dependent arising of emotion. In the later part, the emphasis widens to relationship, vulnerability, and temenos: the kind of sacred, careful space needed for imaginal sharing to remain alive rather than collapse.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the subject 03:45 Why care is needed 09:59 Foundational skills 14:06 Sustainable practice 19:55 Working with emotion 47:25 Self, story, and view 01:02:08 Seven instructions revisited 01:09:53 Relationship and vulnerability 01:30:31 Temenos and sacred space
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50485/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 7)
item.description:
What counts as knowing, and why do we trust some kinds of experience while shrinking back from others? The talk moves through that question by comparing ordinary facts, values like beauty and goodness, inner images, and the harder case of sensing soul in the world around us. From a tree’s love, to the Smurfs and Beethoven, to a black cat in a dark basement, the speaker keeps testing where contemporary culture draws the line between imagination, perception, and reality. The talk ends by suggesting that a middle way, and eventually the idea of participation, may open a less cramped way of understanding truth and soul-making.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the epistemic question 03:50 Values and modern skepticism 09:01 Inner images, outer knowing 13:30 Love, beauty, and judgment 19:23 ESP and imaginal contrast 27:14 Middle way in practice 32:56 Black cat metaphor 36:23 Culture and hidden assumptions 46:51 Participation and soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50499/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 6)
item.description:
What if sensing with soul is also a way of knowing values like beauty, love, goodness, and duty, when modern culture tends to treat those as merely subjective? The talk traces how ideas from Galileo through Descartes, Bacon, Locke, and later scientism helped install a hierarchy of what counts as real, and how that hierarchy can quietly limit imagination, practice, and even the way the body is perceived. It also warns that any method of knowing can become a prison when it is treated as absolute, whether in science, religion, or Dharma. The speaker argues that imaginal perception includes a real sensing of values, and shows how modern and religious ideas shape what we think we can know.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Questions 03:02 Values in Imaginal Perception 12:15 Galileo to Descartes 17:30 How Cultures Rank Knowing 25:20 Modes of Being 36:22 Jung and the Prison of View 40:46 Literalism and Interpretation 50:53 Body, Fantasy, and Perception
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50498/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 5)
item.description:
How do you tell the difference between a busy mind and an imaginal opening? The talk keeps returning to that question through concrete examples: a creative project softened by another person's perception, a painful memory reframed in a shared field, and ordinary tenderness turning into an iconic sense of beauty and blessing. From there it widens into the claim that sensing with soul is not a technique to control, but a way of noticing what we value, what we receive from others, and how the energy body, humility, and attention help a life situation become imaginal rather than merely problematic.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the review 03:41 Conditions for sensing 09:49 Grace and receptivity 14:51 A creative project opens 20:19 Pain re-seen 25:02 Nakedness and blessing 36:34 What people transmit 46:13 When fantasy becomes imaginal 01:04:46 Values and next steps
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50497/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 4)
item.description:
What changes when experience becomes less fixed, and can be sensed as already alive with image, value, and sacredness? The talk moves from a broad map of “sensing with soul” to the conditions that support it: intimacy with what is present, sensitivity in the whole energy body, loosening clinging, and then a readiness for whatever imaginal quality is most helpful. It then turns to examples — a student’s emptiness practice turning vedana holy, a ritual with tangerines and a gold bowl, and an eating meditation that opens into interdependence, compassion, and devotion. Near the end, the talk asks how intention itself can help or hinder this kind of sensing, especially when the aim is not just to reduce suffering but to let perception deepen and reconfigure it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 09:35 Four Conditions 12:21 Intimacy and Energy Body 15:38 How the Process Works 20:54 Vedana Becomes Holy 25:48 Tangerines and Gold 42:44 A Meal of Interdependence 01:03:51 Two Perceptions, One World
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50496/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 3)
item.description:
How does perception shift from ordinary seeing into sensing the world with soul, and what actually helps that change happen? The talk moves through that question by treating soulmaking as a dynamic process: sometimes sudden, sometimes gradual, sometimes consolidating before it shifts again. From there, it turns practical, asking where attention needs to be weighted—toward Self, Other, World, the energy body, or desire—so the movement does not get stuck in habit or flatten into a formula.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 02:33 Soulmaking as dynamism 06:00 Different tempos of change 08:00 Self, Other, World 11:37 The energy body 15:11 Desire and eros 17:29 Habit or soul's call 23:39 Reification and looseness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50495/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 2)
item.description:
What is actually being made when experience becomes soulful? The talk returns to that question by unpacking sensing with soul as a movement where Eros, image, and concept interweave: desire reaches toward more, imagery holds that reaching, and understanding gives it shape and durability. It also explores how this dynamic can stretch our ideas, deepen discernment, and open a larger sense of life, while noting that the whole subject resists any tight definition.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What sensing with soul means 05:13 Making and receiving 09:39 What soul names 14:23 Eros, Psyche, Logos 20:40 Beyond and transgression 28:05 Why frameworks matter 33:15 Agile thinking and Feynman 39:52 Undefinable but fertile
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50494/

item.title: Sensing with Soul (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the point of practice is not just to notice experience, but to sense the world, the self, and even perception itself with soul? The talk traces how imaginal practice can spill beyond an inner image into a wider cosmopoetic way of seeing, then distinguishes this from oneness perceptions and more reductionist forms of mindfulness. It moves toward a careful, practical map of sensing with soul: how it can include the senses, thought, reverence, discernment, play, and a deepening equanimity even in the face of illness and death.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:30 From Image to World 05:36 Cosmopoiesis and Oneness 10:40 Introducing Sensing with Soul 17:56 Why It Matters Most 25:29 Philosophy and Examples 41:17 Soul in All Senses 57:22 Four Modes of Contact 01:08:28 Mindfulness and Dependence 01:19:33 Reverence and Play
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50493/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 6)
item.description:
How do imaginal images become more fully imaginal: by being noticed, lightly questioned, deliberately nudged, and held with enough delicacy that one quality can brighten the whole field. The talk moves through a practical list of aspects—meaningfulness, timelessness, the imaginal middle way, participation, and concept—showing how each can be tuned in practice rather than treated as an abstract definition. Along the way, the speaker returns to examples like body-as-light or body-as-jewels, and keeps insisting that impermanence is not denied but held together with a subtler sense of sacredness and poetic truth.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the Teaching 01:49 Amplifying the Image 05:58 Subtlety and Intention 10:31 Bodies of Light and Jewel 13:33 Meaningfulness and Devotion 17:01 Timelessness and Sacred Time 28:09 Holding Impermanence 32:27 Imaginal Middle Way 50:54 Way of Looking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50476/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 5)
item.description:
How can imaginal practice be fuller than self-improvement, and what changes when the image is not there for my growth but for soul-making itself? The talk works through that question by distinguishing intention, autonomy, two-ness, and the way meaning emerges without collapsing an image into a single interpretation. It moves from a reflection on eros, logos, and divinity into concrete examples—a rapturous Dartmoor image, a warrior god figure, and a flying dragon—to show how images can open into deeper relationship, not just symbolism.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:35 Fullness of Intention 18:07 Autonomy and Dependent Arising 23:18 Dartmoor Tentacles 27:45 Two-ness and Free Choice 35:00 Ethics and Freedom 40:26 Soft Edges of Being 52:03 Echoing and Resonance 01:05:11 Meaning and Values
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50475/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 4)
item.description:
What if an imaginal perception is not a single thing, but a whole constellation of elements that can be entered from different doors? The talk moves through that field by tracing how attention, receptivity, eros, beauty, divinity, grace, reverence, humility, trust, and duty can each ignite the others, sometimes all at once and sometimes gradually, like dimmers turning up rather than a switch flipping on. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to the practical question of how to sense these qualities without reducing them: how to stay open, how to tell the difference between contraction and opening, and how to let an image or insight actually reshape the life around it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Imaginal constellation 05:51 Ignition and grace 11:44 Switches and dials 19:36 Elements of the field 27:54 Dimensionality and divinity 40:27 Grace, reverence, humility 50:30 Humility as image 01:09:35 Trust in the image 01:15:04 Duty to consolidate
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50474/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 3)
item.description:
What makes something imaginal: the image itself, or the relationship that brings it to life? The talk moves by loosening fixed definitions and then testing them against practice, arguing that imaginal qualities arise when attention, energy body awareness, meaningfulness, eros, and love begin to light one another up. A lattice of connected nodes becomes the guiding image here, with the emphasis on how noticing, jiggling, or gently empowering one element can open the whole field without reducing it to technique alone. Grace, mystery, and the limits of control stay present throughout.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Recap of the framework 06:07 Relational, not inherent 10:33 The imaginal lattice 19:10 Wiggling a node 26:53 What energy body means 37:17 Soul-making and jhana 43:18 New territory and play 52:22 Love as a key node
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50473/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 2)
item.description:
What makes an image imaginal, rather than just vivid or useful? The talk reworks that question by moving from a recap of imagination as a spectrum, through the roles of duty, trust, and selfless intention, into practical ways of opening to imaginal perception through samadhi, emptiness, dream figures, living people, emotions, and even the body itself. A long personal example shows how an apparently ordinary image can become meaningful through patient attention, while a more dramatic one can still miss the imaginal if trust, love, and depth are absent.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Recapping the terrain 05:35 Duty and trust 09:11 Opening the gates 11:26 Ways into image 19:39 The black coat story 23:39 Vivid but not imaginal 30:36 Patient transformation 35:42 Technique and patience 41:31 Life already imaginal
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50472/

item.title: Aspects of the Imaginal (Part 1)
item.description:
How do you tell the difference between ordinary imagination and something more fully imaginal? The talk opens by situating this as a continuation of earlier soulmaking teachings, then slows down to clarify the word “imaginal,” introduce “sensing with soul,” and map the kinds of practitioners who may need different entry points. From there it moves into a careful distinction between papañca, mindful imagination, and imaginal perception, before broadening into the philosophical questions of what counts as real and how we know it, and finally into a spectrum of experience that invites more depth, reverence, and mutual enrichment rather than rigid definitions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Course framing 05:29 What imaginal means 11:57 Different practitioner entry points 15:39 Ontology and knowing 18:28 Imagination in practice 23:55 Naming the tradition 31:30 Core qualities of the imaginal 39:24 Why lists matter 47:34 A dynamic spectrum
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/50471/

item.title: Shaping Vessels
item.description:
How do practice and soulmaking keep growing after retreat ends? The talk moves from that central question into a search for the kinds of vessels that can support intimacy, shared inquiry, and imaginal life in ordinary settings. Along the way, it considers why people who burn for the path can still feel lonely, and how careful structure, mutuality, and right speech may help what is most alive become more shareable and more fully seen.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Thanks 00:41 Practice Beyond Retreat 02:27 Loneliness In Dharma 05:58 Vulnerability And Simplicity 07:55 Kinds Of Vessel 09:45 Temenos For Images 11:34 Right Speech Reconsidered 13:10 Closing Question
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43947/

item.title: Beauty and the Buddha
item.description:
What if beauty is not a property of things at all, but something that arises in the relation between subject and object, and can be deepened by the way we look? The talk moves from the Pali Canon’s rather stark treatment of sense pleasure, through critiques of reductionist or tragic views of aesthetics, into an account of beauty as eros, resonance, imaginal perception, and eventually divinity and buddhanature. It ends by asking whether the aim of practice is really to reach a final truth, or to support an ever-expanding movement of soulmaking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 02:54 Beauty in the Canon 06:07 Sense Pleasures and Limits 08:54 Radiance and Vividness 15:02 Tragedy or More 21:09 Eros and Resonance 43:09 Emptiness and Dimensions 01:00:04 Divinity and Buddha Nature 01:17:30 Soulmaking Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43934/

item.title: Modes of Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
What if emptiness is not a deflationary view, but a way of opening into mystery, divinity, and a wider range of mystical experience? In this Q&A, the talk moves from the relation between samadhi and emptiness as less-fabricated states, into how imaginal practice can spill into the world as cosmopoiesis, changing how perception itself makes reality feel. It then turns to eros, the pull of image and otherness, and how the path is less about forcing results than trusting what feels most alive as it unfolds in different ways for different people.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 02:19 Emptiness and divinity 07:14 Samadhi and less fabrication 10:39 Cosmopoiesis explained 13:04 The tree example 16:52 Not making it happen 21:02 Trusting eros 23:42 Otherness and merging 26:14 Soulmaking and Tantra 28:59 Hillman and influence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43944/

item.title: Longing, Vulnerability, Anteros
item.description:
What happens when longing is not treated as a problem to overcome, but as a doorway into relation? The talk moves from a difficult book-writing experience, through imaginal practice and the figure of Anteros, into a wider exploration of how devotion can answer us back, especially when vulnerability, grief, and not-knowing are included. It closes by asking how ordinary things, even tangerines, can become holy through the way we look, bless, and participate in their meaning.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 02:02 Book-writing as doorway 05:50 Anteros and reciprocity 07:24 Anxiety into image 14:59 Deity and vulnerability 17:07 Alchemy of desire 20:06 Devotion and image 24:23 Sacredness made visible
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43943/

item.title: Awakening and Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
Can awakening still be possible when long sits, frequent retreats, or a fixed practice style are not available? The talk moves from that practical worry into a widening view of meditation as flexible ways of looking, where insight can arise in walking, in nature, in relationship, and even outside formal practice. From there it turns to jhana, imaginal practice, eros, and devotion, asking how these threads can open both samadhi and a more expansive soulmaking path without reducing any of them to a single formula.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 05:55 Beyond the cushion 09:34 Jhana without dogma 13:52 The unfabricated nearby 15:52 Image into samadhi 24:35 Body and image 28:30 No single framework 30:07 Eros and devotion 37:19 Closing exchange
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43933/

item.title: On Blessed Ground: Fantasies of the Self on the Path
item.description:
What if practice is not mainly a project of reducing suffering, but a field shaped by fantasy, eros, and the images that give it life? The talk starts by questioning the inherited medical model of Buddhism, then opens into other possible ways of understanding the self on the path: as researcher, artist, adventurer, or lover. Rather than trying to strip fantasy away, it asks whether the fantasies already at work are soulmaking—whether they deepen meaning, aliveness, and the sense of discovery.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 06:18 Medical model of practice 09:30 Religion and soul-making 13:27 Researcher and artist 16:39 Adventure and lover 19:50 Fantasy as ground 24:56 Reconstruction and freedom 27:48 What enlivens practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43945/

item.title: Visions of the Beyond
item.description:
What if the first question about practice is not whether your view is correct, but whether it is soulmaking? The talk begins with simple questions about what you love and want from the path, then moves into a wide-ranging comparison of path-conceptions: classical transcendence, one-dimensional craving, a realism of coping, and tantric or soulmaking ways of opening. Along the way, it keeps returning to eros, fantasy, the senses, and the idea that the path may either narrow life or make it more dimensional and alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions 07:22 Culture and Self 12:32 Soulmaking Test 20:02 Eros and Beyond 29:26 Horizontal Craving 42:11 Four Path Imaginations 56:06 Tantra and Imaginal 01:04:40 Open-Ended Awakening 01:23:56 Questions to Carry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43949/

item.title: Energy Body and Image (Q & A)
item.description:
How do you work with experience when the body feels split between something wide, pleasant, and subtle, and something else contracted, jerky, or hard to breathe into? The talk moves from clarifying what is meant by the energy body to showing how breath, attention, and imagination can soften contraction, open movement, and shift what feels pleasant or difficult. It then turns toward imaginal practice: how images arise, how they can be received without grasping, and how relationship itself shapes what unfolds.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:16 Opening Question 03:18 Energy Body as Relation 07:00 Maps and Dependent Arising 10:06 Working with Restlessness 16:03 Meeting Drowning Sensations 19:45 How Imaginal Perception Arises 24:34 Loosening Conceptual Grip 40:23 Samadhi and Fabrication 43:27 Pīti and Relationship
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43936/

item.title: Opening to Desire and the Imaginal (Q & A)
item.description:
How do you work with desire when it seems to open into something spacious, then tighten around an image, or disappear altogether? The talk moves through a series of questions about the energy body, imaginal practice, depression, fear of being flooded by images, and the way eros can show up either as strong feeling or as something very subtle. The speaker keeps returning to a practical thread: notice the fork in the road, learn when to stay with an image, when to rest in the body, and when to loosen back into a wider field. By the end, the emphasis is less on fixing a problem than on sensing how different conditions bring the whole field back to life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:31 Contraction Around Image 01:57 Holding Both Experiences 04:11 Images and Soulmaking 06:08 Desire, Depression, Reification 13:02 Fear Of Flooding 17:27 Pain and Compassion 20:12 Opening To Desire 27:53 Finding The Deeper Wanting 33:59 Eros, Conditions, Subtlety
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43946/

item.title: Instructions: Opening to the Current of Desire
item.description:
A desire is traced back from its fixed object to the deeper current underneath it. The talk lays out a three-step practice: notice dukkha or desire, ask what is really wanted, and then open the body to the energetic movement of that desire rather than clinging to the image. Through several examples, the speaker shows how this can lead either into imaginal experience or into a more spacious sense of being, sometimes without any outer change at all.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice threads 02:12 Introducing desire practice 04:50 Three-step method 09:22 Opening the body 12:28 Freedom and joining 16:23 Craving and hype 20:18 Deeper wants 23:58 Lack and fabrication 27:27 Trusting the current
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43941/

item.title: Clinging, Craving, Eros (Q & A)
item.description:
What if clinging is not something to eliminate, but a range of push-pull movements that can become either contracted and suffering or open and creative? In this question-and-answer session, the talk moves from defining craving, clinging, and eros to practical ways of working with them: bringing in the energy body, loosening the sense of reality, and letting images become more imaginal. Later, it widens into emptiness, soulmaking, and the possibility of relating to anger, old patterns, and even one’s own history as part of a larger transformation rather than a problem to be fixed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Clinging and eros 06:07 Fabrication and suffering 10:10 Transforming craving 19:28 Emptiness without forcing 24:13 Soul and self images 25:49 Anger as alchemy 33:54 How to recognize imaginal 41:11 Relational images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43935/

item.title: Instructions: Letting Go
item.description:
Letting go is presented here not as a single move, but as one thread among several: nourishment, emotional care, imaginal practice, and renunciation all have to be woven together. The talk then lays out four ways of working with desire and craving: return to what nourishes, look beneath desire to the feeling underneath, give craving space until it peaks and passes, and reflect on whether the object will really satisfy. In the later part, the speaker turns to impermanence and then to a practical exercise with mealtimes and the lunch queue, using food to notice craving, fear of scarcity, and the difference between raw sensation and the mind’s story.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Expanding the path 02:58 Renunciation as a thread 06:20 Return to nourishment 11:23 Care for hidden pain 13:42 Give craving space 17:37 Reflect on desire 19:50 Impermanence and dukkha 24:06 Eating as practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43940/

item.title: Eros and Equanimity in the Movements of Soul
item.description:
How does desire deepen practice without collapsing into grasping, realism, or imbalance? The talk develops a framework in which eros, imaginal perception, and concepts mutually fertilize one another, widening experience when they are not blocked by rigid views, aversion, or a refusal of image. It then turns to the tension between eros and equanimity, asking how the movement of desire can remain balanced, spacious, and sustainable. Near the end, the speaker returns to the key middle way of seeing image as image, and shows how that stance can open both potency and trust in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Terms 02:51 What Soul-Making Means 10:08 The Shape of Eros 24:51 Eros-Psyche-Logos 33:45 Otherness and Beyondness 43:50 Self, World, and Eros 57:34 Staying Balanced 01:10:02 Eros and Equanimity 01:17:44 Seeing Image as Image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43937/

item.title: Faith in Soulmaking (Q & A)
item.description:
How do you stay faithful to one vivid image without closing down the wider imaginal field? The talk moves through that tension in a question-and-answer format, showing how a single image can deepen, how multiple images can appear without “unfaithfulness,” and how soulmaking tends to make things more manifold rather than more fixed. It then turns to the path itself, suggesting that devotion, meaning, and calling can be related to as erotic and imaginal even when they are not visual or easily defined, before ending with practical questions about analysis, heart, and how to keep a practice alive without boxing it in.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:47 One image, many ways 04:19 Manifold imaginal life 07:11 Eros in the path 10:24 Working with vague images 16:53 Intellect and image 21:13 Desire, autonomy, heart 24:51 Analysis as practice 27:17 From craving to eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43939/
