item.title: The Problem of Desire
item.description:
What if desire is not one thing, but three different movements with very different effects? The talk starts by questioning the simple idea that desire is the problem, then turns to the Buddha’s distinction between wholesome and unwholesome desire, before widening into a critique of reducing awakening to mere relief from unpleasantness. From there it moves into a careful reworking of craving, clinging, and eros, with meditation and ways of looking used to show how clinging fabricates experience and how eros can open into intimacy, beauty, and soulmaking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 04:04 Culture and confusion 06:47 Wholesome and unwholesome 09:41 The path is not thin 16:30 Beyond relief 20:06 Three kinds of desire 25:49 Clinging and emptiness 36:55 Defining eros 50:59 Eros, path, and sacredness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/43948/

item.title: From Mindfulness to Divinity: Towards the Tracing of a Phenomenology of Soul (Part 2)
item.description:
What happens when mindfulness keeps widening until it touches eros, imagination, and the sense that anything perceived is already fabricated? The talk traces a path from dependent origination and emptiness into states of less fabrication, then into the way divinity, soulmaking, and the imaginal can arise as lived experience rather than abstract belief. From there it turns to the self, the beloved, and the world as participating in a double movement: toward transcendence and toward the rich particularity of manifestation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Phenomenology of practice 04:26 Stages of divinity 09:31 Opening the imaginal 14:19 Eros and soulmaking 25:16 The self as image 33:15 Beauty and divine mind 48:57 Two movements of eros 01:04:57 Dependent origination and matter 01:19:17 Eternity and time
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40177/

item.title: From Mindfulness to Divinity: Towards the Tracing of a Phenomenology of Soul (Part 1)
item.description:
What happens when mindfulness is widened enough to notice that eros, fantasy, and soul-making are not distractions but part of the texture of being human? The talk moves from that opening claim into a close reading of image, showing how the imaginal can feel theatrical, mysterious, timeless, and relational, and how it can reshape the sense of self, other, and world. From there, it turns to the tension between reducing suffering and allowing soul-making, then widens again into a phenomenological account of divinity, the unfabricated, and the ways practice can open both transcendent and immanent dimensions of experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros and soul-making 04:30 What image reveals 10:13 Subtle fantasies in practice 17:55 Life as image 24:48 The Buddha as fantasy 32:14 Ways of looking 39:31 Pathos and suffering 46:52 Suffering and interpretation 59:20 What divinity means
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40176/

item.title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 2)
item.description:
What if desire is not mainly a problem to be suppressed, but a way of inquiry that can open into soulmaking? The talk moves from questioning assumptions about craving and selfing, to distinguishing eros from papañca, to showing how an unavailable beloved or a teacher-fantasy can become an imaginal opening rather than just something to let go of. It then widens into a detailed argument that desire can arise from abundance, already be present in the imaginal, and even be transformed by how it is seen, held, and understood.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Inquiry as invitation 02:11 Reframing desire 05:15 Eros and the imaginal 10:53 Peace without flattening 14:51 Teacher fantasy and soulmaking 23:09 Psyche loves itself 29:17 Desire beyond lack 53:41 Ways of looking 01:07:51 Alchemy of desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40183/

item.title: Opening The Dharma of Desire (Part 1)
item.description:
When desire shows up as dukkha, is it possible to ask what it really wants rather than only trying to manage the craving? The talk unfolds through a personal story of political upset, then two concrete examples—romance and intellectual hunger—to show how deeper wanting can be felt in the body, opened to, and sometimes revealed as freedom, connection, inquiry, or love. It also pauses to distinguish this from simple acceptance: the speaker keeps returning to preconceptions, lack, posture, and the need for compassion when opening becomes frightening.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Introducing the inquiry 02:12 Origin story 06:45 Three-step practice 17:32 Examples of craving 32:16 Desire becomes inquiry 40:02 Desire and lack 45:11 Experiment, don't flatten 01:02:36 Relation to imaginal practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40182/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
When desire shows up as dukkha, is it possible to ask what it really wants rather than only trying to manage the craving? The talk unfolds through a personal story of political upset, then two concrete examples—romance and intellectual hunger—to show how deeper wanting can be felt in the body, opened to, and sometimes revealed as freedom, connection, inquiry, or love. It also pauses to distinguish this from simple acceptance: the speaker keeps returning to preconceptions, lack, posture, and the need for compassion when opening becomes frightening.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Introducing the inquiry 02:12 Origin story 06:45 Three-step practice 17:32 Examples of craving 32:16 Desire becomes inquiry 40:02 Desire and lack 45:11 Experiment, don't flatten 01:02:36 Relation to imaginal practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40182/

item.title: Eros and the Brahmaviharas
item.description:
What happens when loving-kindness is no longer treated as the whole field of love, and eros is allowed to meet it instead? The talk carefully separates eros from metta, compassion, joy, and equanimity, then shows where they overlap and where they move in different directions: one complexifies and personalizes, the other simplifies and loosens fabrication. From there it becomes a practical inquiry into how erotic imaginal work can support compassion, steady the heart before suffering, and open new possibilities for practice without replacing the Brahmaviharas.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 02:39 Eros and imaginal love 08:12 Shared ground with the Brahmaviharas 13:26 How eros differs 19:47 Particularity and the modern self 26:31 Receiving love 35:15 Less and more fabrication 48:14 Combining practice 58:32 Eros as fuel
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40170/

item.title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 3)
item.description:
What happens when the impulse toward simplicity becomes a shadow that closes off self, sexuality, and body? The talk begins by questioning the habit of treating insight as if it only illuminates, then follows how every view also casts blind spots—using the Four Noble Truths, no-self, and renunciation as examples of teachings that are liberating but double-edged. From there it turns toward recent openness around sexual orientation and gender, imaginal practice, and the way eros can widen perception of the body into something more multifaceted, sensual, and alive. Near the end, the speaker contrasts a narrow, reductionist picture of the body with a view of it as endlessly revealing, a field of possible ways of knowing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Shadow of Insight 05:44 Sexuality and Complexity 09:11 Against Simplism 16:04 Gender and Opening 20:33 Body as Expressive Field 41:33 Erotic Perception 49:25 Freud and Polymorphous Eros 53:18 Infinite Modes of Knowing 01:03:35 Body, Soul, and View
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40186/

item.title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 2)
item.description:
How does sexuality change when it is no longer treated as a fixed fact, but as something shaped by view, culture, and imagination? The talk moves from reductionist ways of talking about sex and love, through stories of Dharma communities and inherited moral judgments, into a sustained reading of the Inanna and Dumuzi myth as a contrasting image of sacred, fertile lovemaking. From there it asks whether sex can be soulmaking rather than merely distracting, and whether a more open, less contracted way of perceiving can reveal holiness, dimensionality, and participation in the cosmic. The final movement turns to modern identity and Foucault, asking how sexuality became so tightly bound up with selfhood, and whether kindness and questioning can open a different relationship to it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Sex as a view 04:17 Reduction and reaction 10:21 Inherited moral frames 14:07 Questioning the binaries 22:46 Inanna and Dumuzi 29:24 Cosmic sexuality 41:16 Sex and soulmaking 53:21 Foucault and identity 01:07:12 Kindness in inquiry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40185/

item.title: Refractions: Of Body, Sensuality, and Sexuality (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the problem is not too much desire, but how quickly desire is pathologized and flattened into ideas of balance, health, or bare attention? The talk moves from intense spiritual longing, through examples like Thomas Merton and the Buddha, into a critique of modern Dharma assumptions about sensuality, practice, and the senses. It then opens into a wider argument about beauty, eros, soulmaking, and why a richer view of fabrication may matter for art, nature, and the ecological imagination.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:08 Intensity and pathologizing 07:32 Merton and total commitment 11:31 Moderation and its limits 18:06 Eros and the senses 28:42 Three views of practice 52:53 Beauty, depth, and belonging 58:44 Practice styles and flatland 01:20:18 Fabrication and sacredness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40184/

item.title: The World and More: Immanence, Tantra, and Transcendence (Part 2)
item.description:
What if eros is not a distraction from awakening but part of its movement, reshaping how a tradition understands the path, the body, and the world? The talk moves through Buddhism, Tantra, Christianity, Kabbalah, and a few intimate examples to test three possibilities at once: that erotic language is symbolic, that it is a vehicle, and that it may also be a real re-sanctification of sexuality itself. Again and again, the speaker returns to the same pressure point: whether practice should strip the world down to literal meaning, or open it into a layered, imaginal, and cosmically participatory field.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros at the Center 06:19 Origins or Telos 09:48 Tradition as Growth 14:28 Sophia and Imaginal Prayer 20:37 Ambiguous Sacred Sex 27:56 Three Tantric Readings 36:41 Sun, Moon, and Cosmos 44:06 Sex as Divine Mystery 55:50 Ritual, World, and Thirst
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40194/

item.title: The World and More: Immanence, Tantra, and Transcendence (Part 1)
item.description:
What if passion is not the problem, but the force that helps the world arise? This talk moves from a critique of how modern insight practice has narrowed its inheritance into a wider weaving of emptiness, tantra, eros, and imaginal practice, using a tantric couplet, Jung, Nietzsche, and Kabbalah as points of comparison. It then deepens into a careful distinction between craving and a holy, inexhaustible eros, with a long imaginal example that shows how erotic and sacred perception can transform how the senses, self, and world are known. The movement of the talk is toward a double perspective: loosening fabrication through emptiness while also allowing divine appearance, devotion, and participation in the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Weaving the threads 02:08 Insight as an evolving tradition 07:44 Tantric verse and Vajrasattva 16:41 Desire and world-making 23:23 World rejection and transcendence 29:21 A living imaginal sequence 41:13 Inexhaustible eros 56:50 Nietzsche and the senses 01:07:13 Buddha mind and Kabbalah
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40193/

item.title: In Love with the Way: Images of Path and of Self
item.description:
What if the path is not mainly a cure to reach, but a field of images that shapes how practice feels, what it promises, and who we imagine ourselves to be on it? The talk begins by unpacking the familiar medical model of practice, then turns to four path-fantasies — medical patient, religious, researcher, and artist — to show how each can open or narrow the way we relate to awakening, tradition, and self. From there it becomes a set of practical inquiries: where fantasy is alive, where it has collapsed into ego-measurement, and how different practices like emptiness, imaginal work, samadhi, metta, and ethics each invite different ways of imagining the path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 02:37 Eros and path fantasy 09:57 Medical model of practice 17:12 Bones need flesh 25:04 Researcher fantasy 37:59 Artist fantasy 47:50 Permission and scope 01:07:17 Five areas of practice 01:28:14 Open-ended path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40178/

item.title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 3)
item.description:
What if practice is not mainly a way to reduce suffering, but a living relationship with eros, image, and soul? The talk contrasts several ways of understanding the path: as medicine, as scientific inquiry, as religious replication of the past, and as an open-ended artistic unfolding. It then asks whether the images of awakening, the self on the path, and even the Buddha and Dharma need to be seen as images so they can remain beautiful, fertile, and not harden into idols.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Eros and the path 04:07 The medical model 08:31 Tradition and replication 12:00 The artistic possibility 13:38 Open-ended practice 22:30 What the soul wants 34:45 Outgrowing fixed images 45:04 Immanence and transcendence 58:17 Icons, not idols
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40189/

item.title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 2)
item.description:
What if the path needs more than restraint, more than bare attention, more than a quiet wish to reduce suffering? In this talk, the speaker explores how eros, imagination, and logos shape what the path can even mean, moving from a critique of papanca and deflationary emptiness into Vajrayana imagery, pragmatism, and the question of how much otherness awakening can hold. The talk keeps returning to the same practical issue: whether practice is narrowing desire away from the world, or opening a richer, more dimensional relationship to life, the senses, and the path itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 05:05 Pleasure and reduction 11:15 Papancha reconsidered 17:34 Cooking and devotion 22:49 Vajrayana and emptiness 29:40 Eros in the path 47:43 History and deflation 01:02:23 Fear and limitation 01:17:33 A larger path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40188/

item.title: 'The Holy Life' (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the path is not mainly about settling into this life, but about a longing for something beyond it? This talk opens by tracing the Buddha’s repeated language of the unfabricated, the end of the world, and the ceasing of the senses, then asks how eros and soul-making get directed when the path is understood as a movement beyond fabrication. From there it moves through jhana, the holy life, the Bodhisattva ideal, and a broader history of world-rejection to ask what the Buddha’s orientation means for practice and for our cultural assumptions about transcendence.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros and the path 02:38 Beyond the senses 05:36 Beyond ordinary perception 08:06 The unfabricated 10:54 Consciousness without attribute 15:24 No coming, no going 19:15 Via negativa and longing 24:06 The soul's more 29:11 The holy life
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40187/

item.title: Doubt and Discernment (Part 2)
item.description:
When does desire open into eros, and when does it contract into craving? The talk moves by careful distinctions: first between eros and craving, then between ordinary comfort and a genuinely imaginal relationship, and then into how emptiness and dependent origination change the way doubt itself can be held. Along the way, the speaker tests common assumptions about images, rest, jhana, and longing, asking not just what is real, but what is potent, soulmaking, and worth caring for.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Practice as preparation 02:07 Eros or craving 05:46 Wise effort 09:58 Questioning and experiment 14:08 Imaginal autonomy 19:09 Not realism, but relation 32:27 Emptiness as basis 47:05 Rest and jhana 01:01:37 Longing and devotion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40169/

item.title: Doubt and Discernment (Part 1)
item.description:
How do you tell the difference between eros, craving, and the imagination’s pull into fantasy? The talk starts with the common doubts that arise around erotic imaginal practice — whether it is escapism, wish fulfillment, or a closing down of life — and keeps turning those questions back into inquiry rather than belief. From there it moves into a careful discernment of what images ask of us, how they shape engagement, and why the path may involve risk, subtlety, and a different sense of reality than flat, materialist assumptions allow. It closes by contrasting a path of oneness and non-engagement with the open-ended, infinitely creative possibilities of the erotic imaginal.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the doubt 03:30 Image asks for translation 06:44 Non-engagement or depth 09:10 Fleeing reality revisited 11:57 Eros versus craving 19:03 Fire, risk, and unbinding 25:59 Doubt, suffering, and path 32:45 Personal movement in practice 49:01 Living relationship and infinity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40168/

item.title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 3)
item.description:
What if the question is not whether an experience is real, but whether it is important, soul-making, and worth refining? The talk uses music, nature, other people, and the cosmos as a way to trace how perception can open into nuance, image, and participation, while also showing how culture pushes us toward a flattened, materialist way of seeing. From there it turns to conceptual frameworks, dependent origination, and the non-separation of chitta and reality, asking what kind of ideas actually support exploration rather than shut it down. Near the end, it widens into developmental psychology and the thought that any theory must eventually crack open, letting more light in.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Music and limited hearing 01:14 Real or soul-making 08:17 Culture and realism 18:15 Frameworks that support 26:02 Soulmaking and participation 34:47 Valid because valuable 46:41 Eros and development 59:27 Self, image, and pathology 01:10:33 Cracks in theory
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40181/

item.title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 2)
item.description:
If a conceptual framework shapes what you can notice, feel, and value, then what should a Dharma framework be able to hold without shrinking experience? The talk works through that question by arguing that a viable logos must support soulmaking, imaginal perception, eros, and the widening of ways of knowing, rather than settling into a narrow or stagnant view. From there it turns to the Pali Canon, scholarship, and modern readings of Buddhism, questioning any claim to neutral authority and instead asking how tradition is always interpreted through an agenda. It ends by pressing the same issue into epistemology itself: what counts as knowledge, who gets to decide, and whether meditation can train us into more flexible ways of looking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What a logos must hold 04:00 Eros and interpretation 09:07 When Dharma narrows 11:58 Whose Pali Canon? 14:58 Scholarship and tradition 20:26 Origins and dialogue 26:29 Epistemicide in Dharma 31:57 Mindfulness is not enough 40:18 Training ways of looking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40180/

item.title: Logos in the Garden of Souls (Part 1)
item.description:
What if practice needs a logos — a living conceptual framework — to hold it together, guide its navigation, and keep it responsive as experience changes? The talk moves from that basic claim into a series of analogies, then widens the frame to include fabrication, dependent origination, emptiness, and the many ways of looking that can shape what is seen and lived. From there it opens into eros, soulmaking, body, relationships, death, earth, beauty, and divinity, asking what kind of framework can include all of that without reducing it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:34 Logos as support 05:19 Trellis and tree 12:00 Concept beyond thought 19:12 Fabrication and emptiness 23:51 What the frame must hold 36:33 History and conditioning 39:14 Modern self and soulmaking 44:44 Holy bodies and Christ 51:19 Plural knowing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40179/

The talk moves from that basic claim into a series of analogies, then widens the frame to include fabrication, dependent origination, emptiness, and the many ways of looking that can shape what is seen and lived. From there it opens into eros, soulmaking, body, relationships, death, earth, beauty, and divinity, asking what kind of framework can include all of that without reducing it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:34 Logos as support 05:19 Trellis and tree 12:00 Concept beyond thought 19:12 Fabrication and emptiness 23:51 What the frame must hold 36:33 History and conditioning 39:14 Modern self and soulmaking 44:44 Holy bodies and Christ 51:19 Plural knowing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40179/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 5)
item.description:
How do you keep imaginal practice from tipping into one-sided intensity? The talk works through balance as a practical art: noticing where attention gets captured, redressing the emphasis, and finding a steadier field in which heart, energy body, image, and desire can support one another. It moves from a concrete example of overexcited heart-energy into a broader map of sexual energy, erotic tension, participation, and the different ways practice can be steered toward less fabrication or toward skillful imaginal making. In the end, the conceptual framework itself is treated as part of the vessel that can hold and guide the work.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Balance in Practice 01:31 Heart and Image 04:31 Intensity and Potency 06:17 Sexual Energy 10:24 Union or Participation 15:55 Shifting the Emphasis 21:23 Degrees of Fabrication 23:16 Framework as Vessel
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40199/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 4)
item.description:
What if the problem is not desire itself, but the way it gets flattened into something only personal, only literal, or only to be let go? The talk moves through cases of unavailable love, shame, grief, and devotional longing to show how opening to the imaginal can enlarge self, other, and world instead of contracting them. It then turns to balance: not suppressing eros, but learning how to include the self in the field, let the image become more dimensional, and sometimes perceive desire as already happening in a sacred time.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Eros and balance 02:16 The unreachable beloved 07:34 Beyond 'let go' 16:11 A transforming story 22:49 Include the self 28:13 Forest and benefactors 36:07 Love in the imaginal 47:06 A balanced constellation 01:04:49 Desire, divinity, and time
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40198/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 3)
item.description:
How do you work with desire without flattening it, obeying it, or becoming rigid? The talk begins with mastery as a fluid balance: learning to navigate, in the heat of practice, between letting go, staying with feeling tone and craving, meeting the underlying emotion with kindness, or deliberately turning toward nourishment and spaciousness. It then moves into imaginal practice, where the speaker explores how to stay with erotic image, notice what makes it hard to bear, and make subtle shifts of emphasis without leaving the field of the practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Mastery as navigation 04:27 Why range matters 08:15 Letting desire pass 12:35 Vedanā and craving 17:26 Kindness for emotion 22:02 Subtle skill in practice 25:21 Skillful redirection 30:01 Opening desire's depth 40:35 Working with eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40197/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 2)
item.description:
How do you keep eros from becoming either a narrow fixation or an ungrounded rush? The talk argues that balance is not a fixed middle, but a dynamic capacity built through samadhi, equanimity, rootedness, inquiry, and the kind of nourishment that makes one less dependent on sense pleasures or ego boosts. It then turns to imaginal practice, using the earth-touching Buddha and Buddhist mandala figures such as Akshobhya, Amitabha, Ratnasambhava, and Amoghasiddhi to show how image, emptiness, emotion, and action can be brought into a workable counterpoint.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Picking up the thread 01:07 Samadhi beyond focus 03:43 Nourishment and balance 07:01 Why understanding matters 11:21 Rootedness and earth 13:53 Spacious equanimity 16:58 Strength, libido, vitality 22:00 Dynamic balance 25:29 Range and inquiry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40196/

item.title: Wisdom, Art, Balance (Part 1)
item.description:
How do we work with eros without either flattening it into craving or being carried away by it? The talk begins with two alchemical maxims—about the strength of the vessel and mastering the fire—and then uses them to explore how practice needs both elasticity and care when desire, image, and soul-making intensify the path. It moves through the dangers of over-simplified views of practice, the role of ethics and emotional skill as prerequisites, and a rethinking of equanimity as steadiness, spaciousness, and balance rather than detachment. By the end, the talk opens a middle way between dismissing eros and concretizing it, while also asking what duty to an image may mean and how it can coexist with freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Two alchemical maxims 01:54 Eros needs a vessel 04:41 Elasticity and shattering 08:56 Fire and its dangers 15:59 Dionysus and dismemberment 20:44 Preliminaries for practice 37:34 Ethics and rootedness 42:34 Energetics and trust 48:28 Equanimity and duty
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40195/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 5 - Otherness and the Art of Disappearing)
item.description:
Can eros really be a movement toward union, or does it preserve otherness and keep relationship alive? In this talk, the speaker revisits that question, contrasting soulmaking eros with older ideas of dissolution and then tracing how meditative oneness, imaginal practice, and relationship can coexist without collapsing into final merger. The talk then widens into a sustained reflection on participation: how images, experience, and Dharma may be both discovered and shaped through the way of looking, leaving the listener with a sense of an endless, re-enchanted path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Eros and otherness 02:02 Oneness and its limits 06:45 Meditative dissolution 10:52 Eros keeps tension 15:38 The birth of us 23:18 Not the art of disappearing 27:28 Participation and sacredness 40:59 Ways of knowing 57:05 Endless Dharma
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40175/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 4)
item.description:
What happens when Eros is allowed to open perception instead of being reduced to appetite or fixed belief? The talk moves through a series of images and examples—Plato’s eros as an interpretive daimon, Kabbalistic readings of endless meaning, a dream of a luminous door, and the contrast between idol and icon—to show how desire can enlarge the sense of self, world, and divinity. It then turns practical, asking how containment, boundaries, and non-acting can preserve erotic tension so it becomes a crucible for soulmaking rather than collapse into one-dimensional gratification.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros Opens Meaning 02:32 Infinite Interpretation 07:04 Buddha Nature Language 09:38 When Eros Narrows 12:29 Dream of the Door 17:17 Relationships And Limits 24:10 Icon, Idol, Concept 29:14 Temenos And Containment 35:36 James On Desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40174/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 3)
item.description:
What happens when eros meets a view that can either open depth or stop it at the surface? The talk keeps circling that question through the dynamics of eros, psyche, and logos, showing how certain ways of looking can nourish mystery, while others flatten experience into one dimension. It moves from broad distinctions between realist reduction, less-fabricating mindfulness, and soulmaking perspectives into concrete examples from sexuality, pornography, relationship, and even suffering itself. By the end, the emphasis is on how a living conceptual framework can re-enchant experience rather than close it down.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 02:46 Eros as open-ended 07:57 Three ways of looking 10:49 Mystery and depth 15:19 Eros and logos 21:43 When view flattens 28:31 Pornography and greed 33:56 Multidimensional soulmaking 41:14 Relationship and re-enchantment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40173/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 2)
item.description:
How does erotic longing widen into a whole field of perception, rather than staying fixed on one person or one desire? The talk moves from a fourfold constellation of self, other, world, and eros into a series of concrete imaginal examples: being held by a goddess, feeling desire shift and deepen, and seeing the world itself become more dimensional, luminous, and responsive. It also pauses over the difference between eros and metta, the way interpretation can turn desire into craving, and the question of whether eros is something narrow or a cosmogonic force that keeps opening into sacredness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the Trajectory 03:41 Dependent Co-Arising 08:50 Images That Expand 14:07 Eros and Metta 20:40 Desire and Interpretation 29:52 Cosmopoiesis Opens 35:43 Examples in Practice 46:42 Samadhi and Sacred Time 58:33 Expanding Sacredness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40172/

item.title: Eros Unfettered (Part 1)
item.description:
What if eros is not a problem to manage, but the movement that opens psyche, image, and world? The talk works through that question by redefining eros as contact, intimacy, and the longing for more, then showing how it expands into the imaginal, into self and world, and into a wider soulmaking process. It moves between concept and example, from Greek figures like Pothos and Himeros to detailed meditative images, including subtle tenderness, ritual union, and darker, more intense forms. It also keeps returning to a practical concern: what happens when the image is trusted, or when it is reduced, dismissed, or met with guilt.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 17:22 Why eros appears 24:52 Desire for contact 44:57 Soul loves soul-making 59:17 Subtle erotic images 01:03:01 Samadhi and transformation 01:18:40 The dark goddess rite 01:33:33 Trust or collapse
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40171/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 5)
item.description:
How do you take up eros, emptiness, or even a teaching itself without grasping it by the wrong handle? Starting from Emerson’s line that “all things have two handles,” the talk moves through desire, imagination, and Nagarjuna’s warning about emptiness, then turns to the more unsettled claim that eros is larger than any fixed definition. From there it becomes a careful inquiry into why delineations matter: how distinctions can deepen perception, create trouble, or open new room for practice. The talk ends by asking which conceptual refinements actually serve soul-making, and why the work should stay open rather than become a closed system.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Two Handles 02:51 Eros As Fire 10:19 Diotima and Meaning 16:51 Limits of Understanding 29:29 Dangers of Delineation 44:28 When Distinctions Help 57:26 An Open Path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40167/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 4)
item.description:
How do eros, image, and practice fit together without collapsing into either craving or a flattened psychology? The talk argues for a wider Dharma language that can recognize fantasy and imaginal life as already present, then uses contrasts like holiday and pilgrimage to show the difference between mere pleasure-seeking and soul-making movement. From there it carefully distinguishes love, sex, eros, and metta, and develops an eros-psyche-logos dynamic in which perception can either solidify through clinging or open, deepen, and become more alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Unfamiliar ideas 03:50 Fantasy and image 09:03 Holiday or pilgrimage 13:30 Find out for yourself 15:51 Eros in practice 21:17 Distinguishing love and eros 28:01 Eros and perception 31:59 Small and large eros
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40166/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 3)
item.description:
How do emptiness, imaginal practice, soul, divinity, and eros fit together without collapsing into either metaphysics or reductionism? The talk moves by way of a careful glossary: it starts from a phenomenological method, then unfolds terms like ways of looking, fabrication, emptiness, image, soul-making, logos, cosmopoiesis, theophany, divinity, and Buddha nature. Rather than treating these as abstractions, the speaker keeps returning to how they alter perception, deepen meaning, and open or close experience in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Entering the framework 05:33 Why these words matter 18:09 Phenomenology and looking 24:27 Emptiness and freedom 29:02 What counts as imaginal 38:00 Soul-making and eros 45:06 The eros-psyche-logos dynamic 58:55 Cosmopoiesis and theophany 01:06:53 Divinity and Buddha nature
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40165/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 2)
item.description:
How can eros be named without flattening it into sexuality, pleasure, or a fixed definition? The talk begins by situating eros inside a larger practice framework, using the hermeneutic circle to show why any single term has to be read in relation to the whole path, the whole Dharma, and the larger web of imaginal and soulmaking teachings. From there it offers a working description of eros as a desire for more contact, more knowing, and more intimacy with the beloved or erotic object, then tests that definition against tension, juiciness, dynamism, arousal, delight, and the difference between soulmaking delight and meditative bliss.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Why This Delineation 02:20 Definitions and Context 05:46 Hermeneutic Circle 10:12 Eros as Desire 15:56 The More and Otherness 24:10 Recognizing Eros 30:10 Juiciness and Arousal 37:03 Delight and Bliss
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40164/

item.title: Dilemmas and Delineations: How did we get here?! (Part 1)
item.description:
What if eros is not a problem to eliminate, but a living force that can be clarified, studied, and even sacralized? The talk begins by asking why this word needs to be distinguished at all, then moves through a teachers’ discussion, a long example of a practitioner wrestling with love and monastic life, and a wider argument that desire is already part of the Dharma’s terrain. By the end, the speaker is sketching a more elaborate practice map in which eros, soulmaking, and emptiness can be held together without collapsing into either craving or renunciation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the Inquiry 03:40 Why Context Matters 10:02 Many Meanings of Eros 13:56 Desire on the Path 18:14 Previous Explorations 30:17 A Monk’s Dilemma 44:48 Eros Beyond Sex 52:50 Relationship and Practice 01:05:24 Toward a Fuller Psychology
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40163/

item.title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 3)
item.description:
What if the problem is not clinging itself, but a too-small idea of what non-clinging is for? The talk starts by questioning the familiar ideal of simply “letting go,” then widens the frame to ask what that ideal means for art, love, activism, family, and the full range of human passion. From there it contrasts ordinary craving with subtler investigation, and finally sets two broad directions side by side: deepening insight into emptiness through clinging, and opening to eros as a movement that can deepen soulfulness, beauty, and sacredness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Returning to non-clinging 02:37 What the ideal leaves out 10:26 Concepts and Dharma fantasy 15:39 Why simple frames fail 22:48 Three directions of inquiry 27:41 What practice is for 37:09 Mindfulness and poetry 47:40 Eros and soulmaking 55:02 Two broad paths
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40192/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
What if the problem is not clinging itself, but a too-small idea of what non-clinging is for? The talk starts by questioning the familiar ideal of simply “letting go,” then widens the frame to ask what that ideal means for art, love, activism, family, and the full range of human passion. From there it contrasts ordinary craving with subtler investigation, and finally sets two broad directions side by side: deepening insight into emptiness through clinging, and opening to eros as a movement that can deepen soulfulness, beauty, and sacredness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Returning to non-clinging 02:37 What the ideal leaves out 10:26 Concepts and Dharma fantasy 15:39 Why simple frames fail 22:48 Three directions of inquiry 27:41 What practice is for 37:09 Mindfulness and poetry 47:40 Eros and soulmaking 55:02 Two broad paths
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40192/

item.title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 2)
item.description:
What if the aim of practice is not to get rid of craving once and for all, but to see how craving, clinging, self, and world are being fabricated in each moment? The talk moves from ordinary meditation experience into a finer-grained exploration of “ways of looking,” showing how releasing contraction can thin out dukkha, loosen self-view, and even change how objects, time, and perception appear. It then widens into Mahayana and Vajrayana territory, where emptiness, appearance, and the role of practice-tools like dependent origination are held together without reducing the path to a simple instruction to “just let go.”
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 05:03 Craving and subtle dukkha 10:47 Self, world, and fabrication 18:04 Ways of looking 24:44 Spectrum of fabrication 30:56 Impermanence and the map 38:49 Empty appearances 46:35 Perception and Mahayana 54:52 Progressive letting go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40191/

item.title: The Way of Non-Clinging (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the common teaching to “just let go” is true, but still too simple to carry the whole path? The talk starts by sorting through the overlapping Pali language of desire, craving, clinging, and fetters, then uses dependent origination to show how these movements actually build suffering in ordinary life. From there it moves into practical ways of meeting craving in real time—through attention to feeling tone, notice of bodily tension, reflection, and the quiet recognition that papanca, or proliferating story, keeps the whole thing spinning. By the end, the talk opens a larger question: whether non-clinging is not only release from suffering, but a doorway into a deeper freedom, emptiness, and a wider sense of beauty and sacredness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the context 04:36 Pali terms and fetters 13:59 Dependent origination map 16:52 Everyday craving examples 19:20 Anatomy of clinging 40:44 Simple letting go 52:56 Working with dukkha 01:04:04 Impermanence and its limits 01:11:32 Beyond simplism
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/40190/

item.title: Tending the Flame
item.description:
The talk begins with the problem of leaving retreat: how to carry openness back into ordinary life without overwhelming yourself or the people around you. From there it moves into the need for Sangha, reading, conversation, and other forms of nourishment that keep the fire of practice alive after silence ends. It then turns to the retreat’s more philosophical edge—how to relate to the imaginal, realism, and the middle way by asking not what is fixedly true, but what is helpful, freeing, and soul-making. The talk closes by widening the frame further, showing how imaginal work, samadhi, metta, mindfulness, emptiness, and daily life can support each other across different periods of practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Leaving Retreat Gently 02:29 Sharing With Care 04:58 Sangha Keeps the Fire 08:32 The Imaginal Middle Way 10:51 Check the Soul-Making 14:57 Practice Beyond Retreat 16:07 A Wider Practice Tapestry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37017/

item.title: This Fire, This Longing (Q & A)
item.description:
What does an image ask of us once it leaves the cushion? The talk moves through that question by considering how imaginal practice can continue off-retreat through reflection, writing, subtle changes in life, or simply reverence, rather than by any fixed rule. It then turns to the tension between healing and devotion, the difference between a mental agenda and the heart’s longing, and how imaginal practice can widen into a change in perception that “re-enchants” the world. In the final stretch, the conversation broadens to image and idea, artistic making, striving and non-striving, and a closing note on responsibility, keeping the focus on what actually helps a practice stay alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:46 Images Off Retreat 06:49 Healing and Devotion 11:59 From Mind to Heart 15:12 Image and Idea 23:32 Dukkha and Soulmaking 25:16 The Artist's Dilemma 29:02 Striving and Result 33:16 What Is Helpful 39:37 Energy Body and Responsibility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37027/

item.title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 3)
item.description:
What if meaningfulness is not something added to life, but something that lets a self come alive? The talk moves from Einstein and Jung on the necessity of meaning, into a delicate argument that images, duties, and ways of seeing must be embodied if they are to stay alive rather than remain fascinating ideas. From there it widens into a series of poetic and philosophical touchstones—Hammarskjöld, Chaim Vital, Nietzsche, Orthodox theosis, Kabbalah, and Buddhist practice—to show how the self can be re-seen as open-ended, multiple, and capable of loving its fate without flattening suffering or difference.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Meaning and the self 02:49 Images need embodiment 07:30 Yes to the more-than-human 11:37 Many roots, many worlds 16:53 Loving fate and divinity 23:50 Seeing truly 30:08 Creating and shaping God 36:15 Levels of self 41:31 Infinite soulmaking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37021/

item.title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 2)
item.description:
What if the self is not just something to be explained, but something to be re-enchanted? The talk moves through the ways reductionist and overly narrow spiritual views flatten experience, then turns to desire, mind, suffering, and imagination as dimensions that may need to be seen as divine, or at least held with far more room. From there it opens into a wider question of causality: whether the past, the present, the future, or the imaginal may all shape what we are becoming, and how practices and views can either close that down or let it unfold.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Why disenchantment happens 04:14 What needs re-enchanting 08:33 Desire, eros, and legitimacy 17:24 Mind and emptiness 21:46 The dark and the good 26:07 Suffering and re-storying 36:49 Frames of causality 46:34 Dreams, future, and imaginal life 01:00:01 Artifice and soul-making
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37020/

item.title: The Gift and the Artifice of Self (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the modern sense of self is not simply mistaken, but thinly imagined? The talk moves through cultural critiques of secular humanism, consumerism, and reductionist views of mind, then turns toward Buddhist, Christian, Islamic, and other frames that treat the person as deeper, more sacred, and more open than a fixed identity. From there it asks what kind of view can actually hold both freedom and suffering, and why flexibility of self-view may be central to re-enchanting life without flattening it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Cultural confusion 03:11 Empty self, wary culture 06:43 Fantasy of origins 10:25 Re-enchanting self 14:16 Secular humanism 20:15 What cultures make room for 26:41 Sacred depth of person 37:13 Process and flexibility 42:04 Dukkha and soul-making
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37019/

item.title: The Imaginal and the Middle Way (Q & A)
item.description:
How do you keep an imaginal practice alive without turning it into belief, fantasy, or a new identity? The talk moves through a series of Q&A exchanges on the middle way: practicing for the Buddha or the divine, holding multiple archetypes without forcing an either/or choice, and using practice to find more cooling, equanimity, and space. It also turns toward sacredness in a more staged, pragmatic way, then finishes by opening up the darker side of the imaginal as part of a wider soul-making field.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Q&A 02:40 Support off retreat 06:15 Practice and intention 09:34 A spectrum of views 15:32 Middle way and wobble 20:37 Renunciation as imaginal 28:39 Permission and practice 33:13 Cooling intense experience 38:35 Making the sacred
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37022/

item.title: Sensing Divinity
item.description:
Can divinity be sensed both as a universal oneness and as something alive in the particulars of a self, another person, or even desire itself? The talk moves between those two modes, tracing how meditation, emptiness, metta, and imaginal practice can cool clinging while also re-enchanting personhood rather than erasing it. It then turns into a guided paired exercise: settling in the body, offering well-wishing, receiving it, and looking for the brightness and mystery of consciousness in the other’s eyes.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Enchantment and divinity 01:51 Universal oneness 04:02 Desire and equanimity 06:50 Personal and imaginal 11:02 Re-enchanting the self 18:32 Perfection and surrender 25:27 Buddha nature and tantra 33:36 Divine fire in particulars 40:10 Metta and the gaze
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37016/

item.title: Creating the Path (Q & A)
item.description:
How does an image become sacred without being reduced to either a fantasy of the mind or an object with powers all its own? In this Q&A, the talk moves from Tara, Mickey Mouse, and the dependent arising of art into a careful middle way: perception is shaped by practice, culture, devotion, and the energy body, and what appears can change as those conditions change. It then turns to erotically charged imaginal connection, the danger of getting swept away, and the question of what a path is really for if freedom is not the only thing you most want.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:19 Imaginal forms 04:54 Art and dependence 06:37 Practice and sacredness 14:26 Cosmopoesis 18:49 Eros and otherness 23:33 Texture and autonomy 29:17 Emptiness and fluidity 32:37 Paths and dangers 40:50 What matters most
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37005/

item.title: From Inertia, Towards the Solutio (A Fragment)
item.description:
How do we loosen the mind’s inertia enough for perception to become more fluid, more creative, and less locked into habit? The talk moves from the basic support of inhabiting the energy body toward opening the senses, especially in walking and standing meditation, where a different awareness of the elements and the landscape can be invited. Rather than forcing a special state, the emphasis is on softening the gaze and attitude so that a more prayerful, sacred way of relating can emerge and be gently supported.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the senses 01:10 Habit and inertia 02:21 Softening perception 03:31 Walking prayerfully 04:49 Body and self-consciousness 06:05 From loosening to creation 07:27 Returning to practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37028/

item.title: Matter, Bodies, Worlds (Part 2)
item.description:
What if the world we take to be solid, single, and objective is partly shaped by the way we look at it? The talk moves from a critique of modern reductionism and mind-matter dualism into Buddhist, Kabbalistic, and tantric ways of seeing many worlds, many bodies, and different levels of time, always returning to experience rather than abstract belief. It then turns practical: how perception can be trained, how the body can be re-enchanted, and how sacredness may be approached in stages without rushing past what is actually workable.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Bottom-Up Assumptions 08:16 Many Worlds, Many Traditions 14:08 Quantum and Mind-Matter 21:01 Mind-Made Body 33:40 Phenomenology and Plotinus 45:28 Energy Body and Alchemy 52:49 Mandala and Mantra 58:40 Time and Timelessness 01:08:55 Pragmatic Closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37012/

item.title: Matter, Bodies, Worlds (Part 1)
item.description:
What changes when matter is not taken as a fixed, self-evident reality, but as something shaped by view, imagination, and practice? The talk begins with a bird-garden story to contrast a reductionist, survival-based reading of life with more enchanted ways of seeing, then moves through self and world, psychotherapy, and the question of whether practice is oriented toward the self or toward the divine. From there it opens into matter, body, space, and time, bringing in emptiness, dependent origination, and critiques of scientism to ask what kinds of worlds our assumptions actually create.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Birds and reductionism 03:53 Self and world together 09:46 Re-enchantment and devotion 18:43 Objects as theophanies 27:26 Matter, mater, origins 31:53 Mature imaginal vision 36:48 Nietzsche on scientism 45:24 Horkheimer, Adorno, and limits 53:19 Science, space, and openness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37011/

item.title: Neither 'Is' nor 'Is Not': Of Emptiness and the Imaginal (Q & A)
item.description:
What happens when truth, emptiness, and image pull in different directions? The talk moves from an ethical question about when life demands a plain response into examples like climate change, art, and personal practice, showing how emptiness need not mean indifference but can support care, responsibility, and re-enchantment. In the second half, the conversation turns to soul-making and imaginal practice: how images can serve growth, or be held as primary presences, and how an image can become lively enough to seem to spread into the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Truth Demands Response 02:48 Ethics And Commitment 04:56 Climate Change And Fantasy 10:19 Art Into Emptiness 14:19 Ways Of Looking 17:15 Plural Souls 25:16 Images And Divinity 30:03 Cosmopoiesis
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37014/

item.title: Hearing All Sounds as Mantra: A Jewelline World
item.description:
What if the point of practice is not to hold one object, but to learn how attention can move: from body well-being, to emotion, to image, to the sensed world, and finally to sound itself? The talk begins by laying out those overlapping directions, then turns into a long guided experiment with Om Mani Padme Hum, first as chant, then as a way of opening the heart, the energy body, and the imagination. From there the listening widens further: sounds in the room, in the wind, in speech, even in pain and awkward noise are invited as mantra, so the practice becomes an exercise in re-sensing the world as a kind of living mandala. The talk ends by stressing that this is not a formula but an invitation to play, test, and find what actually opens perception.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat directions 10:29 Mantra as experiment 15:53 Settling into chant 23:12 Spell and blessing 28:16 Imaginal listening widens 36:21 Walking as mandala 48:14 All sounds as mantra 53:32 Sound, meaning, and play
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37008/

The talk ends by stressing that this is not a formula but an invitation to play, test, and find what actually opens perception.

Chapters: 00:00 Retreat directions 10:29 Mantra as experiment 15:53 Settling into chant 23:12 Spell and blessing 28:16 Imaginal listening widens 36:21 Walking as mandala 48:14 All sounds as mantra 53:32 Sound, meaning, and play
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37008/

item.title: Healing the Imagination (Q & A)
item.description:
What does it mean to trust what works in practice when the mind insists one thing and the body says another? In this Q&A, the talk moves from that question into a practical compass of release, relief, harmony, and loosening in the energy body, especially in imaginal and mindfulness practice. It then widens into questions of lingering with images, the balance of soul and spirit, and how different people may need different paths over time. The closing exchange turns to a strong metta experience and how to work with it when it feels overwhelming, boring, or deeply alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:33 What works in practice 01:55 Energy body as compass 04:26 Release and relief 06:21 Working with images 13:00 Gestures and posture 16:58 Tantra and emptiness 24:10 Soul, spirit, and fit 32:04 Metta as energy
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37007/

item.title: The Movement of Devotion (Live and Shortened Version)
item.description:
The talk asks how practice changes when the aim is not tight concentration but a more open, flexible field of experience. It moves through a careful exploration of what supports imaginal practice: less clinging and fabrication, a blending of the senses, and a heart posture of receptivity, humility, and surrender. From there, it turns practical, showing how images, prayer, devotion, the energy body, and even simple shifts in posture can alter what becomes available in meditation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the retreat 02:27 Openness over concentration 05:29 Experimenting with view 10:19 Flexibility and imaginal work 13:29 What opens the imaginal 16:46 The heart's posture 19:47 Divinity and devotion 25:19 Prayer, receiving, and union 31:30 Body, movement, and discernment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37023/

item.title: The Movement of Devotion (Part 2)
item.description:
Prayer here is taken apart and widened: from asking for help, to praise, blessing, devotion, surrender, and a sense of receiving or emanating something larger than the self. The talk then turns practical, tracing how these qualities show up in body, energy body, posture, mudra, and even subtle shifts in how perception itself is organized. It ends by asking when outer enactment helps and when a quieter inner bowing or inward devotion is more alive and soulmaking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Prayer beyond asking 02:40 Praise and blessing 07:58 Widening perception 15:19 Flavors of devotion 18:54 Body and samadhi 24:48 Subtle movement and mudra 30:29 Touching the earth 36:49 Inner or outer devotion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37025/

item.title: The Movement of Devotion (Part 1)
item.description:
If the point of practice is not to win a battle with thought, what actually changes the mind? The talk moves from samadhi as a more subtle, less grasping relation to thought into a wider experiment with openness, flexible attention, and the energy body, then into imaginal practice as a way of perceiving the world differently. From there it maps the conditions that let devotion, surrender, and imaginative perception become alive: less fabrication, a receptive heart, and a way of sensing that blends body, image, and world. The talk closes by sketching how imaginal practice can move from supplication to harmony and, at times, to a felt union that changes how the cosmos is perceived.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Practice Directions 09:12 Beyond Concentration 15:45 Experimental Practice 23:00 Imaginal Opening 25:09 Four Supporting Factors 33:43 Humility and Surrender 40:16 Preliminaries and Devotion 43:57 Three Modes of Relation 51:28 Transubstantiated Perception
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37024/

item.title: The Art of Perception
item.description:
What if perception is not a neutral report of reality, but a field we help make, inhabit, and transform? The talk moves from historical views of the imaginal, through emptiness and dependent arising, into a detailed account of how image, body, heart, and meaning work together in practice. Along the way it uses art, alchemy, and a series of literary and philosophical examples to argue that seeing image as image can loosen modern certainty without collapsing into fantasy.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The imaginal realm 03:54 Religion and reading 09:27 Knowledge shifts 14:41 Truth and play 19:11 Art of perception 30:40 What makes the imaginal 44:32 Importing meaning 55:04 Imagination as salvation 01:05:55 Questioning the familiar
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37018/

item.title: Energy and Transubstantiation: The Alchemy of the Body (Q & A)
item.description:
How do you work with the energy body without turning it into one fixed experience? The talk moves from clarifying language and the difference between energy body and pīti, into practical guidance on resting with pleasure, steadiness, or agitation, and then into how imagery and imagination can open perception without losing contact with the body. Later it widens further to jhana, the flexibility of the practice, and the idea that perception itself can re-enchant the world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:58 What the Energy Body Means 03:21 Getting Familiar 06:16 Awareness, Space, Core 09:03 What Is Pīti 12:03 Pleasure and Samadhi 20:51 Working With Jhana 23:41 Love Figure and Cosmopoiesis 30:14 Agitation and Care 41:04 Re-Enchantment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37006/

item.title: On Orientations in Practice (Live and Shortened version)
item.description:
Rather than trying to stay continuously mindful, the talk keeps returning to a more flexible question: what changes when practice moves deliberately between different ways of looking? It begins with the energy body as a felt field around the body, then shows how that sensitivity can support samadhi, emotional work, and the signs that a practice is on track. From there, the speaker widens into a critique of entrenched Dharma habits—focus on one object, atomistic attention, strong reality claims, and self-sufficiency—and invites a more experimental, creative, and soul-guided approach.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 01:21 What the energy body is 04:23 Why it matters 09:46 Listening beyond the box 13:47 Loosening entrenched habits 21:08 Flexibility and precision 25:40 Subtlety and soulfulness 32:29 Practice as art 40:02 Sitting and walking instructions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37015/

item.title: "I Sing The Body Electric": On Orientations in Practice
item.description:
What if practice is less about holding one object continuously and more about learning how attention changes the world it meets? The talk begins with the energy body as a subtle field of sensation, then shows how that sensitivity can support samadhi, metta, emotional work, imaginal practice, and insight. From there it turns critical, questioning five common Dharma assumptions—summed up as CRASS—and ends by arguing that flexibility, play, and the attitude of art or poetry can loosen fixed views and open recurring experiences of beauty, sacredness, and timelessness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening: Energy Body 02:25 What Energy Body Means 08:31 Useful Across Practices 15:20 Body Electric 20:01 Walking in the Field 23:26 Five Crass Assumptions 36:53 Crass to Scars 42:45 Precision and Subtlety 54:31 Loosening Into Beauty
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37010/

item.title: Mindfulness and Myth
item.description:
What if mindfulness is not the place where reality is finally revealed, but one possible way of looking among many? The talk moves from a clarification of priorities on the path, through a critique of realism, scientism, and modern disenchantment, toward a case for emptiness and dependent origination as the basis of practice. From there it turns practical: how to hear teachings without forcing them into old boxes, and how imaginal or re-enchanting approaches can open into a wider field rather than clash with the Dharma.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the spectrum 02:33 Personal framework 05:18 Realism everywhere 09:26 Secularism and disenchantment 18:25 Scientism and flat cosmos 26:36 Ways of looking 38:16 Emptiness as basis 01:05:31 Listening structurally 01:16:25 Teaching in chunks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37013/

item.title: Beyond the Search for Freedom
item.description:
What if the path is not mainly about getting less stressed, but about opening more deeply to beauty, sacredness, meaning, and the imaginal? The talk begins by questioning why one person’s happiness seems to come less from technique than from living inside an enchanted cosmos, then widens into a careful challenge to the usual Buddhist framing of practice as coping with suffering. It keeps returning to the tension between self-healing and a more radical orientation toward soulmaking, before ending with Jung’s sense that the deepest human tasks are never fully finished.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat frame 05:04 Matthieu Ricard question 09:25 Coping or opening 16:18 What practice is for 24:25 Buddhism and modern models 31:41 Self, God, and meaning 39:54 Priority reversal 48:03 Who practice serves 55:13 Jung and open-ended freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37003/

item.title: The Nature of Enchantment
item.description:
What if re-enchantment is not a return to nature, but a shift in how perception itself is made? The talk begins by using a Guardian article about trees, stress, and city life as a foil, then turns to what is hidden in modernist and scientistic ways of framing value, reality, and nature. From there it opens into a practice-based account of subtlety, sensitivity, imaginal perception, and the need for depth, dimensionality, and a more poetic way of looking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat framing 03:42 Emptiness as basis 08:23 Trees and critique 14:54 Depth and scientism 20:03 Subtlety in perception 25:11 Kinds of enchantment 29:45 Imaginal and soul 36:51 Poetic language 44:29 Open frameworks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/37026/

item.title: An Ecology of Love (Part 4)
item.description:
What if activism is not only about getting results, but also about preserving the heart that can still respond? The talk moves from self-view and noble action to a critique of how Dharma, science, and modern culture can narrow what we count as real, then opens into a wider view of climate engagement shaped by eros, metta, emptiness, and sacredness. Along the way it uses Wendell Berry, Confucius, and Francis Bacon to ask whether practical effectiveness is enough, or whether the soul and the earth both need a richer way of seeing.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros and self-view 01:57 Berry and Confucius 04:20 Images of nobility 07:07 Duty and archetype 13:46 Dharma and engagement 18:40 The Four Noble Truths 24:49 Clinging and fabrication 30:27 Science and materialism 38:52 Two kinds of activism
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32198/

item.title: An Ecology of Love (Part 3)
item.description:
What happens when love for the world opens into depth instead of narrowing into wanting more? The talk traces eros as a force that can reveal richer, more dimensional perception of nature and the Earth, while also showing how a limited view can distort that movement into greed and endless accumulation. It moves from philosophical clarification to concrete examples like travel, consumption, and planetary limits, then closes by distinguishing eros from metta: one preserves particularity, mystery, and expansion, while the other opens a more universal sense of love.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Eros Opens Nature 01:55 Discovery and Creation 03:34 Against Naive Realism 05:52 Eros Or Greed 08:32 Limits to Growth 11:20 Infinite Inner Expansion 12:35 Travel, Fantasy, and Images 19:51 The Middle Sections 33:34 Metta, Eros, Mystery
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32200/

item.title: An Ecology of Love (Part 2)
item.description:
What happens if love is not just a feeling, but a way perception changes shape? The talk begins with metta as intention, action, and universality, then asks why that expansive, even equanimous, opening does not always lead to activism or care for the natural world. From there it turns toward eros as the desire to connect more, showing how that wanting can deepen into image, fantasy, and a richer, more dimensional sense of nature, matter, and the sacred.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:20 Metta as intention 03:43 Universal love, equanimity 10:10 Eros and pothos 13:26 Boundaries opening 19:47 Nature becomes multidimensional 30:16 Spirit and matter 36:52 Stories from the garden 45:26 Horizontal and vertical 53:11 Poetic sensibility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32201/

item.title: An Ecology of Love (Part 1 - Introduction)
item.description:
What if love for the earth is shaped by the way we see it? The talk begins by pressing awkward questions about what we mean by nature, beauty, and sacredness, then broadens into an argument that environmental action is also a matter of view, perception, and the heart. Along the way, the speaker contrasts metta and eros, reflects on the modern separation of the sacred from public life, and suggests that climate crisis asks for more than technical answers.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening Questions 04:21 Beauty and Wonder 10:32 Sacredness in View 18:31 Modern Secular Culture 24:50 Climate as Values Crisis 30:06 What Activism Needs 37:27 Ecology of Love 44:21 Views Are Not Fixed
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/32199/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 5)
item.description:
What happens when perception is no longer treated as a neutral window, but as something always already shaped by conceiving? The talk moves from a critique of bare attention and fixed ideas of divinity into a series of detailed examples where words, sounds, memories, and images open into the sacred: a tap sounding like jazz, bird song as sacred text, studying at a desk as theophany, and even musical longing as a way into wider seeing. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to the same tension: how to let the imaginal stay alive without forcing it into belief or locking it into a single meaning.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Perception and conceiving 02:16 Vague sacred concepts 07:04 Where God is found 10:19 Not one true divine 15:09 Jesus, mythos, and belief 21:15 Tzedakah and the gift 28:40 Theophany in sound 39:53 Sacred text and sacred world 51:26 Music, grief, and freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31524/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 4)
item.description:
What if the same person, sound, or world can be seen on more than one level at once? The talk builds a careful case for a "vertical spectrum of the imaginal," showing how perception, eros, and conceptual frameworks can open an image into theophany and even a larger sense of world-making, rather than collapsing it into a flat, self-referential reading. Along the way, the speaker pauses to distinguish this from the Buddhist spectrum of fabrication, warns against turning any view into a rigid creed, and returns again and again to the need for conceptual structures that stay loose enough to keep experience open.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Concepts as openings 04:01 Circular understanding 08:27 Structures that break 11:27 Perception is central 20:06 Vertical imaginal spectrum 31:18 From image to world 40:20 Eros stretches the logos 51:15 Dynamism and emptiness 01:05:51 Meaningfulness and lightness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31523/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
What if the same person, sound, or world can be seen on more than one level at once? The talk builds a careful case for a "vertical spectrum of the imaginal," showing how perception, eros, and conceptual frameworks can open an image into theophany and even a larger sense of world-making, rather than collapsing it into a flat, self-referential reading. Along the way, the speaker pauses to distinguish this from the Buddhist spectrum of fabrication, warns against turning any view into a rigid creed, and returns again and again to the need for conceptual structures that stay loose enough to keep experience open.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Concepts as openings 04:01 Circular understanding 08:27 Structures that break 11:27 Perception is central 20:06 Vertical imaginal spectrum 31:18 From image to world 40:20 Eros stretches the logos 51:15 Dynamism and emptiness 01:05:51 Meaningfulness and lightness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31523/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 3)
item.description:
How can words like God, divine, and theophany be opened up so they do not collapse into loaded ideas or simple belief-versus-disbelief debates? The talk moves from the unfabricated and the deathless, through emptiness and dependent origination, toward a broader account of how perception becomes less rigid and how meaning can still be real without being fixed. From there it widens into tantric and imaginal territory, including Buddha nature, Luria’s tzimtzum, and a river-side experience where the cosmos seemed to speak, so that personal and impersonal senses of divinity can be held without forcing them into one frame.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 03:33 Language and Conceptual Frames 10:06 Why Frameworks Matter 15:27 The Unfabricated 22:34 Less Fabricated States 28:15 Universal and Particular 31:13 Fabrication and Insight 43:38 Buddha Nature and Gnosis 53:19 Luria and Divine Contraction
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31522/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 2)
item.description:
What if the issue is not whether an image is real, but how perception itself is shaped by the state of mind? The talk moves from objections to imaginal practice, through emptiness and dependent origination, into Corbin, Avicenna, Blake, and tantric language to show how a less fixed, less fabricated way of seeing can open the world as malleable, luminous, and sometimes divine. It closes by tying imaginal practice, metta, samadhi, and Dzogchen back to a shared question about how perception can be opened and directed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Questions of reality 02:25 Concepts as support 04:53 Fabrication and emptiness 09:46 What counts as real 13:43 The imaginal realm 20:16 Perception follows being 25:27 Avicenna and malleability 30:41 Unfabricating practice 36:02 Tantra and divine appearance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31521/

item.title: A Sacred Universe: Insight, Theophany, Cosmopoesis (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the main question is not whether a view is true, but what it opens and closes in practice? The talk first loosens inherited assumptions about personhood and the cosmos, then contrasts common Dharma views of self and interconnectedness with a more radical non-realist stance grounded in emptiness. From there it moves toward imaginal seeing, theophany, and the idea that experience is co-created through the way of looking, ending with a sense that practice can widen into a different cosmos rather than merely refining an old one.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 02:19 Modern selfhood 05:40 Self as a view 10:02 Ways of looking 17:01 Person and expression 23:26 The imaginal real 28:20 Reality and quantum questions 38:47 Non-realist Dharma 48:56 Cosmopoiesis
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31520/

item.title: Theophany and Cosmopoesis: Instructions
item.description:
How does an image move from private meditation to a whole felt world? The talk tracks three broad avenues of practice—bare attention, emptiness practices that fabricate less, and a third set of imaginal practices that deliberately shape perception—then lingers on how a theophanic sense can be recognized, tuned to, and gently allowed to spread. Along the way it gives concrete examples: mantra heard as divine speech, silence felt as holiness, a poem becoming a way of looking, and even distraction turning into a doorway into a more sacred cosmos.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the ground 03:03 Soulfulness and holiness 05:15 Cosmopoesis and theophany 09:39 Three avenues of practice 15:33 How to steer practice 28:27 Examples from practice 40:55 Mantra and distraction 47:15 Personhood and poetry 53:32 Breath, birdsong, and completion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31540/

item.title: The Love and Demands of the Imaginal (Part 2)
item.description:
What if practice is not for the self at all, but for the unfolding of an imaginal angel, and then for drawing something larger back into the world? The talk moves from Corbin’s phrase about the angel’s individuation into Hasidic, Buddhist, Vajrayana, and Kabbalistic parallels, using ayin, the unfabricated, and emptiness to show how perception can be loosened and made more malleable. From there it turns to the demand of imaginal figures, the open-endedness of soul-making, and the way healing may mean widening what we can see, value, and serve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reframing the Aim 02:25 Hasidic Twofold Movement 07:20 God Beyond Belief 17:03 Beyond Being and Nonbeing 24:12 Buddha Nature and Tantra 29:30 Emptiness Makes Perception Malleable 38:39 Healing World and Purpose 52:35 Demands of the Imaginal
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31537/

item.title: The Love and Demands of the Imaginal (Part 1)
item.description:
What if an image is not a metaphor but a living presence that can love, demand, and stretch us beyond the ordinary self? The talk moves through examples of healing, erotic charge, creative work, and shame to ask how imaginal figures shape practice when they are seen as real in a non-literal, dependent-arising way. It keeps returning to the question of discernment: when is an image asking for action, when is it asking only to be witnessed, and what happens when we honor its autonomy without making it concrete?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Image, not metaphor 02:25 Love and demand 04:27 Middle way, not literalism 08:30 Healing old wounds 11:37 Authority and holiness 17:22 Compassion and eros 22:47 Being stretched 28:11 What is healing? 34:57 Sculptor and Coltrane
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31536/

item.title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (3)
item.description:
How do you keep an image alive without forcing it into a literal meaning or a fixed method? The talk moves through walking meditation as either samadhi or imaginal practice, then turns to how to meet images by asking what they want, how long to stay with them, and whether the relationship feels helpful and soulful. From there it opens into stories of healing, love, reverence, doubt, and the way images can mirror life or spill back into it without needing to be reduced to advice. Near the end, the speaker widens the frame again to the register or “octave” of an image and the way imaginal forms can become portable in daily life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Walking as Practice 04:12 Walking with Images 07:02 What the Figure Wants 09:53 Healing Imaginal Encounters 16:37 Love and Eros 21:45 Images and Life 28:35 Literal and Nonliteral 33:02 Doubt and Experiment 59:52 Octaves and Portability
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31532/
