item.title: Soulmaking (Part 2)
item.description:
Can Dharma be understood not just as lessening fabrication, but as a field where imagination, image, and soulmaking are already at work? The talk moves from a critique of “bare attention” as a complete account, to a wider framing in which practice can reduce suffering, but can also deliberately use images, mythos, and love to deepen perception and openness. Along the way, it asks whether disputes about what is “really Buddhism” are often also disputes about our own fantasies, assumptions, and the size of the conceptual box we are living in. By the end, the question is less whether image belongs in practice than whether our Dharma view is spacious enough for the soul.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Ways of Looking 05:24 Three Practice Avenues 07:39 Fantasy in Dharma 12:02 Self and Emptiness 15:14 Images and Dukkha 20:01 Soulmaking as Priority 26:21 Dharma on Imaginal Ground 38:28 Concepts as Lenses 53:13 Love, Eros, and Opening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31535/

item.title: Soulmaking (Part 1)
item.description:
What if Dharma practice is not about stripping experience down to bare facts, but about learning many different ways of looking, including ones that skillfully shape perception? The talk moves from defining soulfulness as resonance, beauty, importance, and a relationship with death, into a contrast between bare attention, decreasing fabrication, and the deliberate fabrication that can nourish image, myth, and meaning. Along the way it brings in emptiness, Buddha nature, Kabbalah, Jung, hospitals, food, and Homer to show how soulmaking works through relationship rather than belief or literal certainty.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the term 04:10 Soul as making 11:29 Soulfulness and papanca 17:15 Traditions and comparators 24:50 Three modes of practice 30:31 Culture and modernity 36:04 Hospitals and mythos 46:21 Emptiness frees imagination 01:00:21 Mythic life and soul story
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31534/

item.title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (2)
item.description:
What does it mean to stay mindful with an image without collapsing into daydreaming or treating it as a symbol to decode? The talk lays out a practical way of attending, tuning, and opening to imaginal experience: holding an image steadily, noticing its body and energy resonances, and sensing when a figure wants to be seen, entered, or related to differently. Along the way, the speaker uses examples like a river scene with an angelic presence, birdsong heard as sacred text, a wounded bird, and encounters shaped by love and gratitude to show how an image can be known more by its living effect than by visual detail alone.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Mindfulness of Images 05:20 How an Image Is Sensed 15:05 Specificity in Experience 21:46 Detail and Resonance 27:14 Signals in the Energy Body 40:25 Posture and the Wounded Bird 46:21 Views and Conceptual Frames 56:18 What the Image Wants 01:12:58 Three Ways to Relate
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31533/

item.title: Opening, Tuning, and Relating to the Imaginal: Instructions (1)
item.description:
What if imaginal practice works best when perception loosens, rather than when images are made too solid? The talk begins by setting imaginal work in context and then argues for a quieter, more meditative approach centered on the energy body, with images arising from samadhi, emptiness, emotion, deliberate invocation, daydreaming, or even the world itself as image. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to a practical caution: do not literalize the image, and pay attention to its intelligence, its pull, and the subtle shifts in attention that let it become vivid without becoming fixed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice context 04:03 Energy body emphasis 09:18 Seven entry points 13:25 Liquidity and emptiness 20:28 Emotion as doorway 25:47 Deliberate imagining 30:49 Places and daydreams 36:25 The world as image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31531/

item.title: What is an 'Image'? (Part 2)
item.description:
What if an image is not just something seen in the mind, but a living presence that shapes how life is felt? The talk carefully distinguishes imaginal image from literal interpretation, reducing things to parts, or treating experience as merely personal history, and instead explores how image can carry mystery, autonomy, and a sense of soulfulness. From myths like Christ, Persephone, and Eros and Psyche to ordinary memories and relationships, the speaker keeps testing a middle way: not dismissing images, not freezing them into belief, but learning to relate to them as active and meaningful.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What image means 02:36 Poetic and archetypal 06:22 Against flattening 14:39 Fabrication and divinity 20:08 Shards and views 25:06 Telos and destiny 33:44 Myth, memory, papanca 44:01 Stages and cross-pollination 51:59 Seeing as image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31539/

item.title: What is an 'Image'? (Part 1)
item.description:
What is an image when it is not just a picture in the mind, but something that can carry feeling, direction, and depth? The talk moves through ordinary uses of imagination, then into examples from metta, deity practice, meditation sounds, and the body, before widening into poetic and imaginal images that are ambiguous, resonant, and not meant to be decoded. By the end, the speaker is making a careful distinction between a vivid image that is merely striking and one that feels soulful, generative, and worth returning to.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the topic 01:46 Familiar uses of imagination 06:37 Relationship and healing 09:33 Not just visual 16:27 Dreams, music, and dialogue 24:04 Ibn Arabi and presence 26:16 Poetic images 37:31 Iconic and narrative 53:33 Archetypal value
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31538/

item.title: A Little Exercise for the Imagination
item.description:
What if imagination is less about seeing pictures than about sensing a whole presence, a mood, or a resonance? The talk begins as a guided experiment with a peach, apple, rose, bedroom, childhood home, loved one, seaside, forest, and a piece of music, using each image to notice texture, sound, taste, and especially the felt vibe that comes with it. In the second half, the speaker steps back to explain why the exercise matters: different people relate to imagery differently, and imaginal sensitivity can be trained, even when the details are not visual or especially vivid.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Settling In 01:59 The Peach Exercise 04:27 Apple and Rose 07:21 Rooms and Home 10:34 Childhood House 13:15 Someone Loved 19:03 Seaside and Forest 24:54 Music in Imagination 27:08 What the Exercise Shows
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31519/

item.title: Energy Body: Instructions (2)
item.description:
What if the energy body is not a side topic, but the ground that lets samadhi, emotional work, and imaginal practice all connect? The talk moves from that central claim into a practical exploration of how to meet emotions on the midline with steadiness, delicacy, and enough vitality that attention does not get overwhelmed. From there, it turns to allowing, widening awareness, and occasionally letting images arise and be worked with by their effect on the body and heart. Near the end, the speaker distinguishes directing, energizing, and tuning attention, including tuning to qualities like compassion, love, and warmth.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Energy body as foundation 05:06 What emotions include 11:29 Sensitivity and handling 15:17 Midline attention practice 19:57 Allowing and easing 25:41 Opening to images 31:21 Three aspects of attention 42:33 Two practices, fluidly
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31526/

item.title: Energy, Image, Emotion (Part 2)
item.description:
When difficult emotion feels like a trapped vortex, what happens if image can give that energy a channel? The talk moves through anger, depression, and loneliness to show how an image can widen the field of experience, add depth or even beauty, and sometimes turn raw emotion into something more creative. It also keeps testing a basic question: is emotion primary, or do image, energy, and view shape one another in a more mutual way?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Emotion and trapped energy 01:39 Anger and release 04:04 Default moods and hidden energy 08:58 Coltrane and image 12:20 Meaning beyond emotion 16:52 Transformation and assumptions 20:30 Causes and transpersonal feeling 24:04 Mutual conditioning 28:51 Flexible Dharma views
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31528/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
When difficult emotion feels like a trapped vortex, what happens if image can give that energy a channel? The talk moves through anger, depression, and loneliness to show how an image can widen the field of experience, add depth or even beauty, and sometimes turn raw emotion into something more creative. It also keeps testing a basic question: is emotion primary, or do image, energy, and view shape one another in a more mutual way?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Emotion and trapped energy 01:39 Anger and release 04:04 Default moods and hidden energy 08:58 Coltrane and image 12:20 Meaning beyond emotion 16:52 Transformation and assumptions 20:30 Causes and transpersonal feeling 24:04 Mutual conditioning 28:51 Flexible Dharma views
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31528/

item.title: Energy, Image, Emotion (Part 1)
item.description:
The talk asks how to work with emotion through the energy body without reducing practice to a chase for pleasant states. It moves from basic orientation to a clear framework: developing sensitivity, learning to tolerate what arises, cultivating wholesome qualities, discerning what is fruitful, and understanding how emotions are conditioned by view and relationship. From there it becomes practical, showing how attention, breath, spaciousness, and image can be used with both difficulty and ease, including vivid examples of spontaneous imaginal figures that transform pain, emotion, and energy.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Nexus 10:41 Why Train the Energy Body 20:38 Two Practice Directions 23:25 Five Aims With Emotion 29:51 Working With Attention 41:05 Helpful Images in Practice 50:46 Lion, Divinity, and Energy 55:40 Options and Otherness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31527/

item.title: Energy Body: Instructions (1)
item.description:
What if practice is less about narrowing down and more about learning to sense the body as a wider field of energy? The talk first distinguishes simple sensation-awareness from body contemplation and then moves into the energy body as the main basis for the retreat, showing how a broad, felt awareness can support samadhi, emotional skillfulness, and imaginal practice. Much of the second half is a guided exploration: opening awareness beyond the physical body, experimenting with breath, centers, lines, and light, and learning how to work gently with discomfort or with moments of pleasant well-being.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the course 02:16 Three body modes 09:37 What energy body means 14:37 Samadhi and well-being 23:15 Playful practice 29:34 Opening the field 34:05 Breath and centers 54:43 Lines and light 01:07:16 Survey of possibilities
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31525/

item.title: Imaginal Practice: Doorways and Directions (Part 2)
item.description:
What if imagination is not a distraction from practice, but one of the ways we know the world, the self, and even the sacred? The talk moves from a quick recap of imaginal practice into a larger argument: that fantasy, mythos, and perception are already woven into ordinary life, and that Dharma can be understood as learning flexible “ways of looking” rather than fixed instructions. It then turns practical, shifting from philosophy to the retreat’s actual method: slowing down, working with the energy body, and treating the retreat as a place to develop skills and conceptual supports rather than chase experiences.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Recalling the framework 06:11 Why imagination matters 13:52 Ways of knowing 23:29 Key themes ahead 32:35 Self and Dharma 42:48 Ways of looking 49:55 Learning the tools 56:36 Energy body and pace
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31530/

item.title: Imaginal Practice: Doorways and Directions (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the most important thing about an image is not the image itself, but the relationship we form with it? The talk moves from early Buddhist uses of imagination, such as death contemplation and recollection of the Buddha, into tantric, psychotherapeutic, and concentration practices, showing how each gives images a very different role. From there it widens into a cultural critique of modern suspicion toward imagination and an invitation to a more plural, re-enchanted sense of self and world.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the terrain 02:59 Traditional uses of image 06:22 Devotion and qualities 09:42 Helpful and unhelpful fantasy 14:08 Images and frameworks 20:42 Cultural suspicion of images 36:03 Why people resist 44:59 Plural selfhood 55:19 Re-enchanting the cosmos
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/31529/

item.title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part Three)
item.description:
What if image, myth, and fantasy are not side effects of practice, but part of what actually moves us? The talk asks listeners to notice how even the Buddha, the Four Noble Truths, and ideas of awakening are shaped by the mythos we bring to them, then explores a looser, more imaginal way of working with self, emptiness, and liberation. It moves from critique of modern assumptions about the self into Corbin, Jung, alchemy, and Keats, ending by suggesting that Dharma may be as much about beauty, enchantment, and soul-making as it is about reducing suffering.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Image Before Meaning 06:12 What Drives Us 13:33 Corbin and the Imaginal 21:20 Buddha, Devotion, Fantasy 28:09 The Four Truths Reframed 37:26 Beauty and Enchantment 46:22 Image as Epiphany 54:34 Broadening Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/27491/

item.title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part Two)
item.description:
What if the real obstacle is not imagination itself, but the habit of taking experience too literally? The talk moves from common fears about imaginal practice—madness, confusion, losing touch with reality—into a careful argument that mindfulness, poetry, and mythos all operate with assumptions that shape practice whether we notice them or not. It then traces a path from early Buddhist death contemplation and recollection practices, through the Buddha’s transcendent thrust toward the unfabricated, to later Mahayana and Vajrayana ways of validating image and emptiness together. By the end, the speaker suggests that meaningfulness and love are already saturated with image and fantasy, and that exploring that can open something practical rather than merely theoretical.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Setting Up the Question 04:19 Healthy and Unhealthy Imagining 06:35 Metaphor, Canon, and Presence 09:47 Reality and Assumptions 14:05 Modernism and Matter 19:04 Reading the Pali Canon 25:54 Three Paths of Practice 29:59 Mythos, Love, and Meaning
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/27492/

item.title: Image, Mythos, Dharma (Part One)
item.description:
What does it mean when an inner image is not just a passing picture, but a living field of love, fear, memory, and meaning? The talk moves through a series of examples — a thicket and blue bird, a black devil figure, a train ride, a phoenix, a monastery in Thailand, Gaia House itself — to show how image, fantasy, and mythos can shape both practice and perception. From there, it turns toward the danger of flattening these experiences into fixed symbols, and toward the question of how Dharma practice might meet this subtler, more open-ended territory.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 03:52 Opening the territory 05:46 Blue bird and thicket 09:08 Devil, train, and change 13:11 Embodied tradition 15:38 Phoenix and healing 18:14 Fantasy in life and place 24:20 Visionary seeing 28:55 Condensed meaning 34:22 Literalism and reality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/27490/

item.title: In Praise of Restlessness
item.description:
What if the problem is not restlessness itself, but the way we keep confusing agitation, inquiry, and a deeper kind of fire? The talk moves from the hidden metaphysics and myths inside any Dharma view into a set of contrasting images: the seeker, the fugitive, the settled sage, and the Hermes-like wanderer who does not want a final theory. From there it opens into a wide argument that desire, beauty, and even spiritual longing may be part of the path, not obstacles to it, and that practice may be less about reaching a fixed end than about staying with an open-ended work.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 06:06 Metaphysics and myth 15:14 Homelessness and inquiry 24:35 Creative unrest 30:38 Desire on the path 39:55 Pothos and endless work 50:43 Beauty and enchantment 01:03:37 Dharma as art
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26009/

item.title: Buddhism Beyond Modernism
item.description:
What if the real issue on the path is not how well we attend, but what hidden ideas, myths, and metaphysical assumptions are already shaping what we notice? The talk moves from the basics of inquiry — noticing, interpreting, questioning — into a broad critique of modernist Dharma, using Einstein, Kuhn, Heisenberg, and then Kabbalah, philosophy, and Buddhist history to show how frameworks both help and limit what becomes visible. It keeps returning to one difficult claim: that liberation, too, is imagined through a worldview, and that even the urge for “bare attention” may be carrying its own unseen assumptions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting up the inquiry 04:40 Ideas and their power 14:50 Freedom to inquire 21:36 Kuhn and the Dharma 28:41 Modernism examined 35:27 Breaking the vessels 43:36 Styles of inquiry 53:32 Multiple worlds of meaning 01:05:53 Liberation and fantasy
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26007/

item.title: Questioning Awakening
item.description:
What if the real question is not how to practice, but what we already assume practice, liberation, and reality are? The talk starts by questioning the modern habit of treating mindfulness as a coping strategy or awakening as a softened, psychological ideal, then opens into a wider inquiry about the hidden views underneath Dharma language, the Four Noble Truths, and the meaning of freedom. From there it moves through comparisons with the Pali Canon, modern culture, psychology, philosophy, and physics to ask whether our sense of what is real has been too quickly accepted as common sense.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 05:09 Many versions of awakening 14:50 Four truths, many goals 21:08 What do we want? 32:50 Presence and the now 42:02 Mindfulness and reality 46:37 Ontology behind practice 54:14 Modern materialist models 01:06:06 Questioning hidden views
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26010/

item.title: Creative Samadhi
item.description:
What if samadhi is not just concentration on a point, but a way of working that includes the whole body, a steadier energy field, and a deeper openness of heart? The talk starts by questioning a narrow idea of practice, then builds a broader map: the path aims beyond mere presence, and it has two wings, insight and cultivation. From there, it moves into practical advice on deepening samadhi—relaxing effort, valuing quality over quantity, opening the whole body in awareness, and noticing how kindness, gratitude, and letting go support the mind’s settling.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 05:56 The path goes somewhere 10:21 Two wings of practice 16:08 Samadhi as wholeness 19:01 Why focus is not enough 26:13 Ways to deepen 31:05 Whole-body experiment 39:45 Space, letting go, and looking 45:56 Open heart, open practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/26008/

item.title: Ayya Santacitta, Ayya Santussika, David Loy, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 5
item.description:
How do inner practice and public action meet when the climate crisis can no longer be treated as a distant concern? The talk begins by naming the tension between hopelessness and responsibility, then moves from a reexamination of Buddhist practice to concrete organizing: the bodhisattva path, local and national Buddhist networks, and faith-based efforts like People's Climate March and Our Voices. It closes by stressing that awakening has to become both steadiness and courage—an inward groundedness that can support outward pressure, coalition-building, and voting.
Location: Unknown
Additional teachers: Ayya Santacitta, Ayya Santussika, David Loy
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the final call 01:24 What can we actually do? 06:15 Reexamining Buddhist practice 13:32 The bodhisattva path 20:31 Building a Buddhist movement 34:41 Precepts, urgency, and earth 47:20 Questions from the sangha 58:07 Joy, continuity, blessing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29723/

item.title: Bonnie Duran, Chas DiCapua, Thanissara, Vinny Ferraro: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 4
item.description:
How do climate action, Dharma practice, and social justice meet when the deeper problem is disconnection? The talk begins with a climate-and-mindfulness conversation that moves from plant-based ethics and indigenous views of colonization into a sustained exchange about patriarchy, the overvaluing of intellect, and the need to come back into the body. Later, it shifts into practice questions about despair, effectiveness, and what it means to act from integrity rather than outcomes, with readings from Joanna Macy, a Jataka tale, and a closing dedication of merit.
Location: Unknown
Additional teachers: Bonnie Duran, Chas DiCapua, Vinny Ferraro
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and Introductions 02:12 Plant-Based Ethics 06:42 Colonization and Root Poisons 13:28 Rights of Nature 18:43 Technology and Patriarchy 27:10 Returning to the Body 32:24 Belonging and Grief 40:30 Listening and Action 51:19 Questions and Dedication
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29722/

item.title: Catherine McGee, Chris Cullen, James Baraz, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 3
item.description:
What if climate action begins not with urgency alone, but with the way practice lets us meet fear, grief, and responsibility without hardening? The talk moves from an opening reminder of the global climate series into three linked reflections: one speaker explores the meeting place of the silence of practice and the fire of activism, another frames the climate crisis as a problem of reconnection using the fourfold spiral from Active Hope, and a third turns to the need for inspiring vision and a willingness to feel the pain of the crisis clearly. Along the way, the talk uses the Eightfold Path, the Buddha’s response to awakening, Bill McKibben’s Earth, and concrete questions about daily choices and political momentum to bring contemplation and action closer together.
Location: Unknown
Additional teachers: Catherine McGee, Chris Cullen, James Baraz
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and Welcome 04:25 Why This Matters 09:54 Action and View 13:34 Bone-Jangling Reality 17:17 Silence and Fire 20:38 Reconnecting With the Earth 30:50 From Practice to Action 36:04 James on Inspiration 39:53 Questions and Blessing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29721/

item.title: Ayya Anandabodhi, Rev. angel Kyodo williams, Susie Harrington, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 2
item.description:
What does practice ask of us when the climate crisis is no longer abstract, but a room that is already burning? This conversation moves from a grounding breath into a frank exchange about climate action, social justice, and the Buddhist roots of greed, hatred, and delusion in ecological harm. The speakers keep returning to the same pressure point: overwhelm, grief, and fear can either shut us down or be turned into clarity, compassion, and concrete action. Along the way, they move between personal responsibility, systemic critique, and practical ways to join others without retreating into isolation.
Location: Unknown
Additional teachers: Ayya Anandabodhi, Rev. angel Kyodo williams, Susie Harrington
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the conversation 03:34 Climate as practice 09:32 Interbeing and the poisons 18:40 Justice and systemic harm 32:00 Not looking away 39:37 From overwhelm to action 46:38 Questions and next steps 56:51 Facing resistance 01:04:34 Closing blessings
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29720/

item.title: The Four Noble Truths of Responding to Climate Change
item.description:
What does it mean to face climate change as a practice of non-harming, rather than as a problem kept at a distance? The talk moves through the Four Noble Truths: first naming the scale of the suffering and the science of warming, then tracing its causes in fossil fuels, consumer culture, denial, and inequity. From there it turns toward possibility—shared distress, resilience, local and political action, and the idea that a committed minority can help shift what seems fixed.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Ethics and Integrity 04:25 Non-harming and Earth Care 06:33 Four Noble Truths Framework 10:44 The Bad News 23:22 Meeting Distress 25:53 Roots of the Crisis 32:40 Possibility of Transformation 40:29 The Path of Response 51:58 Questions and Closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/25028/

item.title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 3)
item.description:
What if the images and fantasies that arise in meditation are not distractions to ignore, but living presences that shape the whole direction of practice? The talk moves from common Dharma habits of noticing and letting go, through psychological interpretations like childhood or compensation, toward a more radical possibility: to experiment with images as if they carry their own demand, their own beauty, and their own power. Along the way it draws on poems, myths, and Buddhist examples to ask whether the real issue is not the image itself, but the way we conceive it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 02:27 Common responses 04:31 Psychological readings 07:07 Conceiving and experimenting 10:57 Image as living presence 16:20 From emptiness to freedom 21:26 Daemons and angels 28:32 Risk, myth, and ethics 36:19 What is not just ego
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/28027/

item.title: Jack Kornfield, Ruth King, Tara Brach, Thanissara: Mindfulness and Climate Action : 1
item.description:
How do you stay open to the scale of climate grief without collapsing into fear, guilt, or numbness? This conversation begins with a short grounding practice and then moves into a clear-eyed look at the climate crisis as both ecological damage and a symptom of separation, with references to the People’s Climate March, Joanna Macy, Rumi, and a few stories that point back to presence. From there the talk widens into questions of systemic injustice, collective action, and how practice can support sustained response rather than righteous burnout.
Location: Unknown
Additional teachers: Jack Kornfield, Ruth King, Tara Brach
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and grounding 05:26 Climate as shared condition 13:24 Presence and belonging 22:38 Systems and justice 32:19 Dukkha and bodhisattva path 42:46 Act from love 46:28 Questions on practice 56:51 Organizing together 01:05:55 Closing blessings
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/29718/

item.title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 2)
item.description:
What happens when an inner critic is met not as a solid enemy, but as one figure in a wider inner theater? The talk moves through retreat stories of comforting, devouring, erotic, and warrior-like images to show how engaging them with mindfulness and without literalizing can loosen the sense of a fixed self. It then widens further, suggesting that figures from art, myth, and public life can live in the psyche as real sources of resonance, challenge, and nourishment.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Picking Up the Thread 00:47 Inner Critic, Inner Persons 04:14 Mindfulness and Image 07:25 Loosening a Solid Self 10:37 Images as Resources 17:06 Figures in Culture 21:38 Wildness and Shame 26:06 Working Deliberately
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/28028/

item.title: Releasing the Self, Freeing its Demons (Part 1)
item.description:
What if the self is not something to erase, but something to relate to more wisely? The talk begins by questioning a familiar Buddhist simplification about self and aggregates, then argues for a more flexible approach that can make room for complexity, eros, art, and the patterns of modern personality. From there it turns to the inner critic as a concrete example, showing how loving-kindness, deconstruction, or even dialogue can change its force—and sometimes reveal a hidden protector or a grief-struck image of the mother beneath it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Questioning the usual view 02:08 Limits of aggregates 03:41 A skillful way of looking 06:55 Why simplicity fails 11:01 Modern suffering and self 12:26 Meeting the inner critic 14:46 From deconstructing to relating 16:50 The clumsy protector 19:43 Grief and the mother image
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/28026/

item.title: Meditation: Opening to Aliveness
item.description:
Beginning with posture and breath, the meditation settles into a simple inquiry: what most matters right now? The talk then moves through body scanning, softening the face, shoulders, heart, belly, pelvis, legs, and feet, so attention can rest in aliveness rather than in thought. From there, sounds, sensations, and feelings are included as part of the same field, with repeated invitations to notice when the mind has wandered and start fresh. It ends by returning to the sense of being earth, awake and animated in the present moment.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC
Chapters: 00:00 Settling the Body 01:56 Intention and Aum 05:02 Softening the Face 07:06 Heart and Belly 10:58 Pelvis and Earth Body 14:08 Sound and Presence 17:29 Returning Fresh 20:59 Deepening Into Silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/24680/?access_key=ltYFO7cMrx

item.title: Earth's Crisis - On the Edge of the Roof - (on climate change)
item.description:
The talk begins with climate crisis as a waking-up call, then quickly asks why so much suffering can still feel distant. Through Rumi’s warning that we are “at the edge of the roof,” the speaker moves into stories of Parsifal, the “Sutra on the Son’s Flesh,” and contemporary ecology to show how denial, consumption, and spiritual trance keep us from responding. The talk then shifts toward practice: slowing down, feeling grief and fear honestly, remembering our belonging to the web of life, and acting without needing certainty about results.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Washington DC
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the crisis 09:02 Rumi and Parsifal 12:10 Trance and consumption 16:52 Separation and entitlement 25:00 Digital distance 28:52 Earth as belonging 33:03 Feeling and action 42:31 Collective awakening 50:33 Listening and prayer
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/24681/?access_key=jl2Rr7VAc5

item.title: Buddhist Perspective On Climate Change
item.description:
Climate change is first laid out as a concrete, evidence-based problem: warming atmosphere and oceans, shrinking ice, rising seas, and the role of greenhouse gases and human activity. From there, the talk moves into how a Buddhist lens changes the question from abstract blame to ethics, responsibility, and the way greed, consumption, and indifference shape collective harm. It brings in examples from the Northeast, Nepal, and a range of Buddhist texts and stories to widen the moral circle from human beings to animals, ecosystems, and future generations.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:00 Opening frame 01:17 What climate science shows 12:28 Human influence and local impacts 20:14 Nepal and the Himalayas 28:34 Causes of the crisis 32:19 Harmony with nature 40:05 Non-harming and metta 48:05 Greed and responsibility 59:59 Trees and future generations
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23813/

item.title: Practice & Climate Change
item.description:
How do we stay clear and responsive when climate change, fear, and instability press in? The talk begins with practice as refuge and preparation, then moves through resilience, ethical restraint, and the difference between awareness itself and the restless activity of mind. Along the way, it uses stories, sayings, and images like a guesthouse, a bridge, and a cobra to show how to meet difficult conditions without collapsing into reactivity. It ends by returning to simplicity, letting go, and the possibility of responding from a steadier heart.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Practice as preparation 02:46 Climate change and activation 05:36 A shift in consciousness 10:50 Refuge in Buddha Dharma Sangha 16:18 Ethics and conscience 19:30 Courage and resilience 23:21 Knowing awareness directly 29:33 Simplify and let go 40:02 The peace that remains
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23290/

item.title: Wise Action for the Earth
item.description:
The talk asks why the Buddha still matters in the face of ecological crisis, and answers by tracing the same basic pattern from personal suffering to collective climate breakdown: a mistaken sense of separation. From there it turns toward practice, arguing that inner work, wisdom, and compassion have to be joined to outer response if action is not to collapse into burnout or abstraction. It closes with images of awakening into intimacy with the whole earth, through Rumi, Shantideva, and a call for a new bodhisattva-like way of living.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:04 Buddhism and crisis 02:56 Separate self, shared suffering 05:26 Forgetting the self 06:50 From awakening to action 14:14 A collective predicament 26:49 Three kinds of transformation 30:17 Questions and closing images
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23259/

item.title: Right Action on Climate
item.description:
How do emptiness and impermanence become a reason to act, not a reason to look away? The talk begins with the image of the earth as sacred and moves through a sutta story of the Buddha bathing an abandoned sick monk, using it to argue that clear seeing should deepen care. From there it turns toward climate collapse, climate denial, and the emotional weight of knowing too much, then steadily into concrete responses: speaking truthfully, joining others, changing money and diet, and making commitments that can actually be kept.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Earth as sacred 02:01 Why Buddhists hesitate 03:51 The sick monk 06:20 Climate as ethics 09:47 Pressure and renewal 13:28 Resilience through practice 16:32 Concrete commitments 19:58 What to do now 34:10 Questions and everyday choices
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23258/

item.title: Today’s Climate Reality
item.description:
What happens when a climate talk begins by treating the facts as something to look at directly, even if they are hard to bear? The talk moves from the immediate evidence of warming, ice loss, drought, fires, and acidifying oceans into the human systems tied to them: violence, food insecurity, fossil-fuel economics, and the barriers that keep people from acting. It then shifts toward a practical response, arguing for emissions cuts, carbon pricing, keeping most fossil fuels in the ground, and broader changes in poverty, education, and restoration.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:10 Look directly 00:49 What is happening now 04:13 Warming and wildfires 06:39 Impacts on life and conflict 09:17 Maps of impact 15:38 California drought ahead 17:18 The carbon budget 20:14 Why action is blocked 24:53 What to do together
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23256/

item.title: How To Be an Earthling
item.description:
How do you respond when ecological crisis feels like a constant drumbeat of apocalypse? The talk moves through humor, song, and satire—beginning with Gary Snyder’s ecological Pledge and John Muir’s poetry, then widening into a critique of fear, consumerism, and the old stories that place humans outside the web of life. From there it turns to evolution, Earth Day, and a quieter instruction: simplify, protect the earth, and return again and again to here and now.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:05 Ecological pledge 01:26 John Muir and wonder 02:45 Crisis and comic relief 07:06 Liberation and Passover 09:11 Earth Day and Gaia 10:54 Evolution as creation myth 14:36 Made of earth 17:46 Enough stuff 23:57 Here and now
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23514/

item.title: Compassion As We Take Action
item.description:
How do you act for the world without being driven by guilt, anger, or the need to control outcomes? The talk moves through stories, poems, and reflections on climate grief, kinship with animals, and long-horizon social change, all building toward the claim that effective action has to come from love, tenderness, and trust. It then turns into a guided meditation on breath, vastness, and a personally chosen bodhisattva vow, before ending with a blessing cord ceremony rooted in refuge, compassion, and non-harming.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening questions 04:26 Act from love 09:11 Grief into song 11:38 Finding our way 18:01 Interbeing and seeds 22:44 Long horizons 27:25 A bodhisattva vow 30:36 Meditation on vastness 42:51 Blessing cords
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/23513/

item.title: The Eco-Crisis as a Spiritual Crisis
item.description:
What if the ecological crisis is the larger form of the same delusion that makes a person feel separate, anxious, and always lacking? The talk moves from the visible surface of climate breakdown, species loss, and poisoned oceans into a deeper argument about industrial growth, consumer capitalism, and the ways Buddhism can be reduced to coping. Along the way, it uses the image of two icebergs, then turns to Zen teachings, a poem about extinction, and lyrical readings that point toward non-separation rather than escape or mere adjustment.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Fragile Earth 02:01 Seeing the Iceberg 08:50 Reading Extinction 11:20 Beyond Climate Alone 20:08 What Awakening Means 33:46 Growth and Alienation 43:10 Intimacy With the World 49:45 A Spiritual Crisis
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/22994/

item.title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: Earth As Witness: International Dharma Teachers' Statement on Climate Change
item.description:
How do we meet climate change without treating it as something outside our own lives? The talk begins with simple breath-based inquiry and expands into the carbon cycle, the greenhouse effect, and the urgency of rising emissions, then turns toward a Dharma teachers' statement that links suffering, craving, and interdependence to climate response. From there it becomes practical: household changes, mindful consumption, collective action, policy pressure, and a final move into loving-kindness for the Earth. The overall arc is sober but not despairing, holding the crisis as a call to wake up and act together.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Additional teachers: Bob Doppelt
Chapters: 01:53 Opening the conversation 04:57 Breath and interdependence 12:39 The science of warming 17:11 Facing the suffering 19:09 Earth as Witness 31:53 Dharma principles for action 43:55 Hope through visible change 47:46 Individual and systemic shifts 56:57 Tipping points and metta
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21981/

item.title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Three)
item.description:
What if the images and stories that move through practice are not just private material to be managed, but forces that shape how the world appears? The talk pushes past the usual idea of mindfulness as simply staying with experience, and instead turns toward conceptual frameworks, emptiness, and different ways of looking that can either flatten life or open it. Drawing on examples from Buddhist texts, Jung, Hillman, Rilke, myth, and psychotherapy, the speaker keeps asking whether an image is something to integrate—or something larger that asks something of us.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the series 02:43 Less fabrication 05:32 Ways of looking 09:50 Concepts underneath experience 15:16 Images are not parts 25:07 Archetypes and autonomy 37:17 What the image wants 48:50 Myth and soulfulness 01:00:46 Cautions and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21820/

Drawing on examples from Buddhist texts, Jung, Hillman, Rilke, myth, and psychotherapy, the speaker keeps asking whether an image is something to integrate—or something larger that asks something of us.

Chapters: 00:00 Opening the series 02:43 Less fabrication 05:32 Ways of looking 09:50 Concepts underneath experience 15:16 Images are not parts 25:07 Archetypes and autonomy 37:17 What the image wants 48:50 Myth and soulfulness 01:00:46 Cautions and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21820/

item.title: Climate Change, Capitalism, and Buddhadharma
item.description:
How do private property, comparison, and climate collapse feed one another? The talk begins with the urgency of the 2013 Warsaw climate talks and then turns to an early Buddhist origin story, the Aggañña Sutta, to show how craving, hoarding, and fencing off land lead to hierarchy, theft, and violence. From there it moves into practical questions about what can be done now: media silence, divestment, grassroots organizing, and small groups working together rather than facing the crisis alone. It closes by returning to Sangha as a model for collective response and shared responsibility.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the crisis 09:12 Buddhist lens 15:52 Introducing the sutta 25:52 Desire and comparison 32:05 Property and violence 35:59 Climate and capitalism 40:02 How to speak and organize 46:36 Divestment and resistance 52:08 Local action together
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21552/

item.title: The Theatre of Selves (Part Two)
item.description:
Can imagination help reveal emptiness rather than merely decorate experience? The talk moves through that question by showing how images, archetypes, and resonances can loosen self-clinging, shift how practice feels in the body, and open perception beyond fixed biography. It ranges from devotion to Kuan Yin and the warrior tone of Thai forest teaching to visions, self-images, and the distinction between narrative images and more iconic, timeless ones. Again and again, the speaker asks not what an image represents, but what way of looking it opens.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice and emptiness 03:56 Images in practice 08:27 Archetype and presence 13:30 Love and shame reframed 21:47 Self as mythic field 29:51 Deliberate imaginal practice 39:17 Relationships with images 50:14 Iconic, not reductive 59:50 Concepts and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21821/

item.title: The Theatre of Selves (Part One)
item.description:
What if the self is not a single solid thing, but something constructed in different ways of looking? The talk begins with emptiness and not-self, then moves through seven practice entry points for loosening identification, before turning to a more surprising claim: imagination is not a side issue, but one way the Dharma can reveal and soften selfhood. From there it opens into examples from metta, samadhi, inner critic work, and imaginal dialogue, where figures and voices are treated as active perspectives rather than literal truths.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Imagination Matters 05:04 Emptiness and Not-Self 06:39 Seven Ways Through Self 12:18 Limits of Simplifying 18:04 The Imaginal Opening 21:54 Images in Practice 26:59 Working With Inner Critics 35:51 Myths, Parents, and Voices 46:46 Plurality and Dialogue
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21819/

item.title: Wise Responses to the Hindrances
item.description:
What if the five hindrances are not proof that practice is going badly, but invitations to change the way the mind is relating? The talk moves through sense desire, ill will, dullness, restlessness, and doubt, showing how each can be met with a mix of antidotes, body awareness, and a less personal attitude. Again and again, the emphasis falls on loosening self-judgment, widening the frame, and noticing how perception itself is colored by the mind state. By the end, the hindrances are presented not just as obstacles, but as places where insight and freedom can actually begin.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Frame 03:26 Not Personal Failure 07:22 Samadhi Beyond Focus 15:37 Working With Desire 20:24 Aversion and Ill Will 24:29 Listening Underneath 28:32 Dullness and Energy 32:42 Restlessness and Subtle Effort 45:25 Doubt and Perception
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21822/

item.title: All In This Together
item.description:
What does it mean to be responsible for suffering without getting pulled into compulsion, guilt, or despair? The talk moves from Earth Care Week and the reality of climate-linked suffering into the practice question of how inner work and outer action are not separate, using examples from John Francis, a Zen teaching on response, and the image of the Buddha as awake and emptied out. It then turns inward again, showing how retreat can expose loneliness, anger, and the need to fix, and how those very moments can become a wider, more compassionate responsiveness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Earth Care Frame 04:14 Inner and Outer 10:06 We Are the Environment 13:41 No Separation 20:10 Resonance and Loneliness 30:52 Emptying Out 37:23 Who Hears? 43:58 Community Awakening 53:58 Bodhisattva Vow
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21003/

item.title: The Anatomy of Ignorance 2 - The Social Roots of Ignorance and climate Disruption
item.description:
Climate disruption is taken up first as a problem of ignorance: personal, social, and universal. The talk moves from a sobering account of the science and the scale of the carbon budget to a closer look at denial, emotional overwhelm, and the ways individualism can keep larger systems out of view. It then turns practical, asking what can be done at the levels of personal conduct, community, and collective action, with brief attention to divestment, institutional change, and the role of practice in staying open to fear and grief without being paralyzed by them.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the Three Ignorances 06:47 Social Conditioning 14:35 Climate Disruption 24:59 How We Don’t Take It In 28:50 Grief and Processing 33:11 Personal and Local Action 40:09 Collective Mobilization 47:59 Practice and Motivation 51:33 Questions and Final Counsel
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20859/

item.title: The Broader Context of Mindfulness
item.description:
What does mindfulness become when it is more than bare attention? The talk moves from a critique of “McMindfulness” and the easy commercialization of attention practices into stories from hiking, panic, email, military training, and public action, showing mindfulness as something that can pause reactivity, gather wisdom, and lead to wiser conduct. Rather than treating it as a passive witnessing practice, the speaker keeps returning to how clarity, equanimity, and restraint support speech, ethics, and compassionate intervention in the world.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Mindfulness Beyond Attention 03:35 Rooted in Tradition 10:01 Clarity in Action 15:03 Path, Ethics, Wisdom 19:10 Unwise Attention 26:59 Qualities of Presence 33:03 From Awareness to Conduct 43:14 Stories of Intervention 54:31 Practice and Climate
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20771/

item.title: Practicing in Times of Big Changes
item.description:
When a worldview starts to break down, what lets practice stay steady instead of reaching for escape? The talk moves from a long opening chant into a clear argument that change is not forced but seen through, using the butterfly story, the Buddha’s stillness under Mara, and the teaching on paññā as the capacity to be with things as they are. It then turns practical: feeling is named as the crucial point where grasping can be interrupted, before reaction hardens into anger or more becoming.
Location: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Hartford Street Zen Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening chant 20:10 Facing unavoidable change 21:37 Butterfly transformation 24:47 Seeing without forcing 26:07 Mara becomes flowers 27:28 Inner and outer climate 31:01 Paññā and insight 33:21 Where practice interrupts
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20660/

item.title: Follow Your Heartbreak
item.description:
What if the heartbreak of seeing the world clearly is not a detour from practice, but part of the path itself? Drawing on Andrew Harvey’s sacred activism, the talk moves from a sobering view of global crisis into the suggestion that collective upheaval and retreat life follow the same pattern: dissolution, fear, letting go, and eventually a more spacious balance. Along the way, the speaker brings in the Kali Yuga, the progress of insight, the hero’s journey, and images from Buddha, Jesus, and Star Wars to show how breaking down can become a passage to wakefulness and action.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:01 Opening and retreat context 03:06 Andrew Harvey's framing 07:11 Dark night of the species 13:13 Practice through hardship 20:52 Progress of insight 28:56 The hero's journey 33:21 Heartbreak and sacred activism 37:19 Signs of awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20497/

item.title: The Dharma Of Global Climate Change
item.description:
Global climate change is approached here as a question of how we meet what is already happening: with denial and business as usual, or by turning toward reality. The talk moves from the smoke-in-a-room experiment to Buddhist teachings on craving, contentment, interdependence, and wrong view, showing how a sense of separate self feeds both overconsumption and ecological harm. Along the way, it brings in the Buddha, Atisha, and later voices like David Korten and Joanna Macy to ask what kind of response can come from clearer seeing rather than fear. It ends by framing climate change not only as a crisis, but as a demanding opportunity for awakening and a change in how we live with one another and the earth.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Topic 04:15 Smoke and Social Freeze 06:50 Why It Matters 11:55 Craving and Contentment 19:46 Possession and Stewardship 25:05 Interdependence and Story 29:12 Cause, Effect, and Avoidance 40:32 Mind and Separate Self 48:01 From Fear to Awakening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20250/

item.title: Planet Earth as Divine Messenger
item.description:
How do we respond when the same truth that shows up in aging, sickness, and death also appears on a planetary scale? The talk moves from the Buddha’s teaching on dukkha as unsatisfactoriness, through the story of the four sights and daily contemplation of impermanence, into a direct meditation on climate change, ecological responsibility, and the gap between knowing and acting. It closes by asking listeners to stay with discomfort, keep the heart open, and take the next workable step rather than turning away.
Location: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery (Marin Sangha
Chapters: 00:01 Arriving in the Present 02:24 Dukkha and Impermanence 06:31 The Four Sights 10:06 Extending the Contemplation 12:50 Climate as Context 19:29 Home, Ecology, and Ego 23:35 Turning Toward Dissonance 28:39 Earth as Sacred Text 35:57 Ecological Self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20403/

item.title: Learning from Climate Change
item.description:
How do we face climate change without shutting down, and how do we speak about it without avoiding the discomfort it brings? The talk moves from that practical conundrum into a larger argument: climate grief, confusion, and urgency are not signs of failure but part of the process of waking up to a worldview that no longer fits. Along the way, it brings in Thomas Berry, Arne Næss, Joanna Macy, and the Easter Island collapse to show why the response has to be both inward and collective. A final image from Rumi leaves the listener with the sense of standing at the edge of the roof and needing to be still enough to see clearly.
Location: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery
Chapters: 00:00 Naming the dilemma 02:28 Shock and denial 06:00 Discomfort as guidance 08:25 Universe as scripture 14:17 Outgrown worldviews 19:58 Grief and ecological self 24:54 Attention and resilience 32:36 Learning from collapse 39:53 Closing appeal
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20405/

item.title: The Brahma Viharas and the Climate Challenge
item.description:
How do you meet climate fear without collapsing into denial, panic, or resignation? The talk begins from that tension and moves through the brahma-viharas as a lived response: calming the heart with loving-kindness, opening to suffering with compassion, finding uplift in the beauty and life of the world, and widening into equanimity. Along the way, the speaker pairs Buddhist teaching with concrete images—a pelican’s broad view, a sick monk cared for by the Buddha, the reality of children and grandchildren living in a changing world—to show how wisdom and care have to stay together.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Homage 01:01 Practice in Turbulent Times 04:50 Awakening Is Human 06:35 Training Awareness 09:55 Facing Climate Anxiety 15:00 Metta and Compassion 17:09 Mudita and Equanimity 20:09 Ultimate and Conventional 26:26 Ethics and Renunciation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19967/

item.title: 400 and Rising
item.description:
What do we do when the climate crisis feels too big to hold, and how do we stay open without becoming numb? The talk begins with a recent trip through monastery communities and the sight of eagles, then turns toward the sharper question of rising carbon, collective denial, and the feeling of living inside a damaged world. From there it moves through the four Brahma Viharas—loving-kindness, compassion, appreciative joy, and equanimity—as ways to meet grief with clarity and action, without losing sight of the vulnerability of sentient life. A Buddha story about caring for a sick monk finally grounds the whole talk in ordinary responsibility: ultimate truth does not cancel the need to help.
Location: Aloka Vihara Forest Monastery
Chapters: 00:01 Arriving in community 02:23 Hermitage and the eagles 06:36 The illusion of control 12:27 400 parts per million 14:17 Listening for the feminine 19:18 Seeing the larger truth 22:44 Four Brahma Viharas 28:59 Equanimity and karma 33:56 Care for what is sick
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/20399/

item.title: Reflections on the Day
item.description:
It begins with a body: a short, playful sequence of stretches, shakes, and sounds that opens into the claim that changing the world may require more of that kind of embodied attention. From there, the talk moves from climate grief and the film Chasing Ice into stories of people meeting loss directly, then turning toward action through love, weeping, and responsibility. A long closing story about a prison garden and a boy with a gun gathers these threads into one question: how seeing our interdependence can soften fear and make room for care.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:01 Opening into the body 00:49 Motion theater and release 04:43 From me to we 10:27 Glaciers and grief 14:49 A prison garden begins 20:02 Planting and recognition 23:22 Stardust and non-self 25:04 Closing in the hills
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19234/

item.title: Active Hope
item.description:
How do you stay present when the world seems to be moving toward collapse? The talk begins with a rethinking of hope as something you do, not something you possess, and then moves into a wider bodhisattva view: caring enough to step forward, even without a fail-proof plan. From there it opens into ecological grief, the Cousteau plankton warning, stories of nuclear power and tar sands, and a practical insistence on collective action, honest witnessing, and not turning away. In the final stretch, the talk turns more explicitly to the Avatamsaka and Flower Garland vision of interdependence, using it to hold climate crisis, future generations, and present responsibility in one frame.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and the book 01:28 Hope as a verb 03:59 Bodhisattva and bodhicitta 06:59 The plankton shock 13:37 Panic and paralysis 18:41 Working together 24:42 Grief into action 34:07 Service across time 42:05 One hair, all worlds
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19251/

How do you stay present when the world seems to be moving toward collapse? The talk begins with a rethinking of hope as something you do, not something you possess, and then moves into a wider bodhisattva view: caring enough to step forward, even without a fail-proof plan. From there it opens into ecological grief, the Cousteau plankton warning, stories of nuclear power and tar sands, and a practical insistence on collective action, honest witnessing, and not turning away. In the final stretch, the talk turns more explicitly to the Avatamsaka and Flower Garland vision of interdependence, using it to hold climate crisis, future generations, and present responsibility in one frame.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and the book 01:28 Hope as a verb 03:59 Bodhisattva and bodhicitta 06:59 The plankton shock 13:37 Panic and paralysis 18:41 Working together 24:42 Grief into action 34:07 Service across time 42:05 One hair, all worlds
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19251/

item.title: Connecting Inner and Outer Responses to Climate Change
item.description:
How do inner practice and outer response meet when climate disruption asks for more than private steadiness or public outrage? The talk moves from a short Tibetan invocation on awareness, through the risks of Buddhist escapism and activist burnout, into a wide-ranging case for a bodhisattva-like response that can hold suffering without losing clarity. It then becomes practical, naming personal habits, community action, institutional change, and the larger shift in consciousness needed to face climate change together.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 02:14 Opening Invocation 05:32 Why Integration Matters 09:24 The Bodhisattva Ideal 14:29 Finding Your Calling 18:16 Mindfulness Must Act 19:13 Dharma and Climate Practice 24:27 Personal to Systemic Response 28:44 Reflective Exercise 36:07 Closing Readings
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19240/

item.title: Bob Doppelt, James Baraz: From Me to We
item.description:
Climate change is framed here as a test of whether awareness can move faster than fear. The talk begins with a call for sangha and teacher leadership, including a letter asking Buddhist teachers to offer guidance on the crisis, then shifts into a practical framework drawn from Bob Doppelt’s From Me to We. From there it becomes a series of clear Dharma-to-action movements: interdependence, cause and effect, moral conduct, trusteeship, and free will, each tied back to how we live, choose, and take responsibility in a closed planetary system.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Additional teachers: Bob Doppelt
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the gathering 02:13 Sangha and teacher request 09:50 Bob's framework 15:33 Climate as closed system 24:15 Breath and interdependence 30:06 Awareness and systems 35:50 Moral choice 40:32 Trusteeship and restoration 45:09 Free will and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19235/

item.title: Dharma practice and the climate Crisis
item.description:
The talk begins with a plain dilemma: meditation practice is compelling, and the climate crisis is compelling, so how do we not choose one against the other? From there, the speaker moves through examples, from Thich Nhat Hanh’s engaged Buddhism and Merton’s monastic activism to Joanna Macy’s “Great Turning,” to show that inner practice and outer response can belong together in different ways. The second half turns practical and personal, asking where one’s gifts lie, how to avoid burnout and self-righteousness, and why climate action without Dharma can distort, while Dharma without the world can also narrow. It ends with a brief poem about the world being on fire and “no time not to love,” holding urgency and steadiness in the same breath.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat and urgency 04:43 The climate dilemma 14:18 Engaged Buddhism examples 17:31 Joanna Macy's framework 20:24 Vocation and critique 26:38 Practice needs ethics 36:08 Why activism needs practice 41:23 Concrete climate responses 50:18 Questions and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/19116/

item.title: The Necessity of Fantasy
item.description:
What happens when the image of practice narrows to calmness, non-involvement, and personal freedom, while the wider world is asking for responsibility? The talk moves from ethics as a base of the path, through climate change, species loss, and the silence of Dharma communities, into a larger claim that our lives are shaped by the stories and images we live by. By the end, the speaker is asking whether practice needs a wider range of fantasies, myths, and forms of awakening that can honor grief, outrage, engagement, and the scale of our shared life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 06:29 Ethics as foundation 14:41 Climate and responsibility 22:18 Silence and Buddhist culture 28:33 Fantasy and image 32:52 Narrowed images 41:08 Dukkha beyond the self 50:31 Stories that shape culture 58:23 Widening the range
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/18111/

item.title: Approaching the Dharma: Part Two - Liberating Ways of Looking
item.description:
What if insight is not a rare experience to wait for, but a way of looking that can be trained, repeated, and used? The talk starts from that question and moves through not-self, impermanence, unsatisfactoriness, metta, and mindfulness as practical lenses that can loosen clinging and reduce dukkha in the moment. It then pushes further, asking how even the sense of self, object, and awareness are fabricated together, and how deeper insight can make those fabrications begin to dissolve. The talk ends by widening the frame again: practice is less about holding to one correct view than about experimenting with skillful ways of seeing that unbind suffering.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 03:17 Insight, loosely defined 08:03 Insight from experience 13:49 A way of looking 19:00 Impermanence and letting go 25:41 Metta and the Four Noble Truths 32:09 Repetition and deeper insight 39:41 Fabrication and emptiness 48:03 Practice as experimentation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/17961/

item.title: Approaching the Dharma: Part One - Unbinding the World
item.description:
The talk starts with a question about the “end of the world” and slowly turns that into a question about how perception itself is built, sustained, and sometimes undone. Moving through the Bahiya sutta, the speaker argues that “in the seen, just the seen” is not mainly a technique of bare attention, but a clue to how reactivity, selfing, measurement, and story keep experience in place. From there the talk widens into a careful inquiry into fabrication, emptiness, and the limits of any simple claim that either this world or a transcendent beyond is the final answer.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the arc 03:02 The world of perception 08:54 Beyond elements and extremes 13:34 Bahiya's urgency 19:49 Just the seen 28:18 Desire and fabrication 35:45 A spectrum of construction 41:38 Mirage and illusion 49:09 What practice means
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/17960/

item.title: Thoughts and Images in Meditation
item.description:
Thoughts are first treated as a problem to be softened rather than fought: when the mind is buzzing or scattered, the talk keeps returning to the body, to patience, and to a gentler level of attention. From there it moves into more active methods—labeling, questioning beliefs, and noticing how emotion and body sensation feed obsessive loops—before opening into a wider field where thoughts, sounds, and sensations appear and pass like fireworks in space. The later part shifts again, arguing that thought itself is not the issue so much as the views we believe, and then broadens into the skillful use of images, including metta, light imagery, deity practice, and the subtle work of loosening self-view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the topic 02:13 Scattered thought and effort 10:20 Labeling and relieving 17:10 Obsession and belief 23:38 Emotion in the body 29:00 Open awareness 33:18 Skillful use of image 43:48 Views and conceiving
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/17963/

item.title: The Place of Calm
item.description:
What is calm for: relief from stress, clearer seeing, or something more unsettling? The talk starts with the familiar case for calming the mind, then steadily widens into samadhi as a whole field of well-being, energy, sensitivity, and unification—not just one-pointed concentration. From there it moves into the body’s contractions, clinging, nourishment, and the possibility that practice may be less about forcing stillness than about loosening entanglement and allowing perception to open in unexpected ways.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 01:51 Why calm matters 04:13 What samadhi includes 11:38 Nourishing practice 15:38 Less entanglement 18:55 Clinging in the body 24:11 Whole-body practice 31:16 Reservoir and perception 42:14 Questioning the image
Source: Dharma Seed

item.title: Stewardship and Intention as Gaia Practices
item.description:
What does it mean to live as a trustee of the earth rather than an owner? The talk moves through an ecological reading of Dharma principles, first building the case for interdependence, karma, and moral responsibility, then applying the idea of trusteeship to land, resources, and social inequality. From there it turns to the question of agency: if what we do matters, how do we move out of denial, discouragement, and rationalization into clear intention and action?
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:35 From Me to We 05:13 The Law of Trusteeship 09:58 Benevolence and No Ownership 12:56 Power as Responsibility 16:39 Duties of a Trustee 25:17 Taking the Commitment 27:45 Choosing Destiny 32:00 Stages of Change
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/16804/

item.title: Karma and Sila as Gaia Practices
item.description:
How do karma and morality become practical responses to climate crisis rather than abstractions? The talk moves from the shift from “me” to “we,” through interdependence and cause-and-effect, into a sobering look at ecological damage, then returns to mindfulness as a way to stay awake without being crushed by guilt. It closes by reading three commitments from Bob Doppelt’s From Me to We as a simple framework for seeing systems, taking responsibility, and living by shared principles of justice.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:01 Framing the talk 01:21 From me to we 04:14 Seeing the systems 06:44 Karma and accountability 13:16 The Buddha’s example 16:40 Climate as consequence 26:11 Hidden impacts 32:01 Mindfulness over guilt 36:45 Three commitments
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/16555/

item.title: Seeing the System You Are Part Of
item.description:
How do you stay clear and useful when the world feels like it is tipping toward collapse? The talk begins with a turn toward hope, drawing on Ernest Callenbach’s final ecological essay, then moves into a practical inquiry into five commitments from Bob Doppelt’s From Me to We. From there it narrows to the first commitment—seeing the systems you are part of—through images of interdependence, non-self, and the way a person is always already woven into larger natural and social processes.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:30 Framing the Tipping Point 03:26 Callenbach’s Final Vision 08:15 Hope and Mutual Support 13:30 Hard Times, Regeneration 24:22 From Me to We 30:16 Five Commitments 40:26 Interbeing in the Body
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/16516/

item.title: Appropriate Response at the Tipping Point: Concern for the Planet as Dharma Practice
item.description:
Climate collapse, in this talk, is not treated as an abstract issue but as a moment that tests practice: do we fall into apathy, panic, or a steadier kind of response? The speaker begins with a front-page warning about a tipping point in global warming, then moves through his own swings between urgency and overwhelm before turning to concrete examples from the Dharma world, including questions about travel, sustainability, and institutional responsibility. He draws on Bhikkhu Bodhi’s warning about greed, arrogance, and ignorance, and on Joanna Macy’s sense of deep time, before leading a short guided exercise that asks listeners to imagine speaking from 75 years in the future.
Location: Insight Meditation Community of Berkeley
Chapters: 00:29 The climate warning 04:21 From outrage to overwhelm 08:42 Systems change and Dharma 10:18 Institutions and responsibility 13:32 Bhikkhu Bodhi at the cliff edge 19:29 Joanna Macy and deep time 25:24 Seventh-generation ethics 27:20 Future self practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/16313/

item.title: This Matter of Emptiness
item.description:
What if the main problem is not the experience itself, but the way mind keeps making it seem solid? The talk begins by contrasting the immediate work of meeting experience in meditation with a larger question: whether practice stops at being with what is, or goes on to see how self and world are constructed. From there it moves through Nagarjuna, the Buddha’s language of emptiness, and a vivid shadow play of a wolf turning into a bunny rabbit to show how fear, relief, and even a sense of basic reality can all be fabricated. The closing movement returns to practice as training in ways of looking that loosen realism and open the possibility of groundlessness without collapsing into nihilism.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Footstep and map 05:59 Ultimate truth 12:59 Self and all things 17:53 How experience is built 26:27 Wolf and rabbit 34:12 Foam, mirage, magic 40:37 Realism and liberation 44:05 Practice as seeing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/15324/

item.title: The Beauty of Desire (Part 2)
item.description:
What if desire is not mainly a problem to suppress, but a current to understand? The talk moves from a brief return to letting go and the suffering of clinging, into a series of concrete case studies where asking “what am I really wanting?” shifts anger, craving, worry, and resistance into energy, tenderness, joy, or a wider sense of self. Along the way, the speaker questions simple spiritual assumptions about desire, draws on Buddhist and a few mystical references, and keeps returning to the possibility that the treasure is in the movement of desire itself rather than its object.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 02:34 Letting go of desire 08:51 A different approach 12:03 The bank story 20:18 Craving and hype 26:40 Desire beneath the pain 46:18 History and sacred desire 01:00:07 Methods and closing questions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14587/

item.title: The Beauty of Desire (Part 1)
item.description:
What if desire is not a single problem to overcome, but a field with different depths, risks, and kinds of vitality? The talk starts by questioning the familiar Dharma equation that craving simply causes suffering, then moves into a guided inquiry: imagine the end of life, ask what you truly wanted, and notice what arises in the body and heart. From there it opens into a more unsettling question about the inner critic, fear of failure, and whether some desires need protection and nourishment rather than immediate renunciation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 04:26 The usual Dharma frame 09:24 Why letting go is hard 12:32 Inquiry on the cushion 20:15 What do we want? 28:21 Inner critic and fear 40:36 Desire as development 55:02 No quick conclusion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14586/

item.title: Instructions on Mindfulness of Mind States
item.description:
What if the first thing to notice in practice is not the breath or the story, but the overall state of mind coloring everything else? The talk lays out citta, or mind-heart, as the third foundation of mindfulness and shows how emotions, subtle moods, bodily cues, and perceptions all feed one another in a loop that can either deepen suffering or open space. From there it moves into practical ways of working: recognizing the mind state, feeling it in the body, meeting difficult emotion with delicate attention, and also cultivating wholesome states like metta, compassion, and concentration.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Third Foundation Overview 03:20 Why Mind States Matter 06:18 Recognizing the State 10:36 Meeting Difficult Emotion 16:41 Insight and Non-Solidity 21:44 Skillful Responses 25:18 Cultivating Wholesome States 28:21 Freedom and Capacity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14585/

From there it moves into practical ways of working: recognizing the mind state, feeling it in the body, meeting difficult emotion with delicate attention, and also cultivating wholesome states like metta, compassion, and concentration.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Third Foundation Overview 03:20 Why Mind States Matter 06:18 Recognizing the State 10:36 Meeting Difficult Emotion 16:41 Insight and Non-Solidity 21:44 Skillful Responses 25:18 Cultivating Wholesome States 28:21 Freedom and Capacity
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14585/

item.title: Instructions on Mindfulness of the Body
item.description:
How do you meet the body without turning it into a problem of self-image, comparison, or pain? The talk moves from the Buddha’s instruction to contemplate the body in the body into a series of practical ways of looking: knowing posture and movement, opening to fine sensation, and working skillfully with discomfort by relaxing tension, widening awareness, and softening aversion. It closes by showing how even ordinary bodily experience, and even impermanence and not-self, can become a refuge when they are taken up as a meditation theme.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Practice Is 06:06 Body as Foundation 09:44 Close Bodily Attention 13:02 Working with Pain 20:07 Pleasure and Collectedness 21:44 Ordinary Sensation 23:20 Impermanence and Not-Self 26:08 The Body as Theme
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/14584/

item.title: Am I Free to Inquire?
item.description:
What if inquiry is not a search for the right answer, but a willingness to keep questioning what suffering, freedom, and experience are being made of? The talk moves from intimate mindfulness and simple questions like “who am I?” into a broader argument for asking how clinging, assumptions, and frameworks shape what the mind sees and feels. It then widens further through Buddha stories, psychology, science, art, and philosophy to ask whether practice has become too comfortable, too simple, or too closed to new insight. Near the end, the emphasis turns to courage: letting existing structures crack open so that questioning can stay alive, energetic, and not fixed on a single view of liberation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Inquiry as intimacy 03:43 Questions that open 08:05 Experiment and possibility 17:01 What blocks questioning 24:53 Ideas shape experience 32:48 Passion for truth 41:05 Comfort, certainty, and dissonance 55:28 Frameworks and observation 01:02:53 Cracking open the vessel
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13847/

item.title: A Mystical Kiss (...for Mystics, Anti-Mystics, Lovers and Realists...)
item.description:
Mysticism here is treated less as a set of strange experiences than as a question about how perception itself is formed. The talk moves from dictionary meanings of mysticism, through Christian via negativa and Buddhist emptiness, to the claim that gratitude, reverence, and letting go change what the world seems to be. Along the way it brings in poetry, devotional practice, and modern physics to ask whether the issue is not what is ultimately real, but what kind of seeing opens the heart and loosens the self.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 02:06 What mysticism means 04:21 Via negativa and via positiva 09:09 Sanctifying ordinary life 14:17 Gratitude and wonder 21:09 Bias, doubt, and science 30:33 Ways of looking 43:56 Devotion as deconstruction 53:32 New eyes and emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13846/
