item.title: Working with the Emotional Body (Instructions: Day Five)
item.description:
How do you stay with difficult emotion without getting swallowed by it? The talk begins by revisiting an open, spacious way of attending, then turns to practical forms of resourcing: friends, silence, the body, calm states, compassion, and the simple willingness to let pain belong to awareness. From there it becomes a close examination of what blocks warmth and ease—judgment, grasping, fear, isolation—and how allowing, softening the body, remembering shared suffering, or even meeting a feeling as if it were a child can change the whole field. It ends by widening the sense of resource again to include strength, power, desire, and life force, suggesting that these too can be felt without being turned into a problem.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing Spacious Awareness 05:21 External and Inner Resources 07:55 Space, Silence, and Body 10:43 Meeting Pain Directly 13:19 Warmth Around Difficulty 16:24 Allowing and Softening 18:40 Not Alone in Suffering 21:37 Strength, Power, Desire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13851/

item.title: At Home in the Universe
item.description:
What if the feeling of being out of place can be met, not by trying to fix yourself, but by widening the frame of what counts as home? The talk moves from social alienation, consumerism, and the measuring self into the four foundations of mindfulness, then into body, relationship, earth, and the mind’s own activity as ways of loosening that contraction. It then opens into a bigger existential question, bringing in physics, emptiness, and the idea of a participatory universe to suggest that non-separation is not a slogan but something that can be discovered in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Alienation Everywhere 04:16 Home With Experience 07:59 Sangha and Trust 13:52 Body and Elements 19:55 Earth, Convenience, Pilgrimage 27:34 Identity and Social Pressure 39:25 Death and Big Picture 49:15 Mystical Home 59:52 Space, Physics, Emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13848/

item.title: The Meditator as Revolutionary
item.description:
What if meditation is not leading toward private calm, but toward a wider capacity to meet suffering without turning away? The talk begins by asking what practice is really for, then moves from archetypes of serenity and action to the claim that contemporary Dharma has to reckon with social pain, climate crisis, consumerism, and the values that quietly shape our lives. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from self-correction to courage, concern, and a kind of creative nonconformity that can respond to the world without denial or collapse.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What practice is for 03:06 Archetypes of awakening 06:13 Sensitivity and suffering 12:15 Widening the picture 25:41 Values in a global age 33:58 Culture shapes the mind 43:14 Turning in and out 55:34 Justice, courage, and long view 01:08:05 Creative maladjustment
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13850/

item.title: Reflections on the Retreat and on the Path
item.description:
What if retreat is less about becoming a better meditator and more about uncovering what you actually long for? The talk moves from a warm welcome into a careful reflection on aspiration, longing, and the way silence can let lesser worries fall away so that deeper questions and emotions come into view. It then turns practical: meditation is framed as learning ways of seeing and relating that can meet emotions skillfully in daily life, with room for both heart and thought, and for a little questioning along the way.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Welcome and Context 02:41 Practice as Kindness 05:18 Making It Sacred 08:57 Longing and Aspiration 13:33 Desire and Self-Care 16:58 Working with Emotion 21:28 What Meditation Is For 25:41 Portable Skill and Confidence 30:20 Views, Strife, and the Path
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13849/

item.title: For the Sake of Life on Earth, part 2
item.description:
How do you stay open to the pain of the world without collapsing into fear, numbness, or isolation? The talk moves through the spiral of “the work that reconnects,” beginning with gratitude, then honoring pain for the world, and then shifting into a practice of seeing with new eyes through poetry, indigenous wisdom, and a guided outdoor exercise. From there it turns toward action: if we are part of the earth rather than separate from it, then ecological and social change calls for discipline, courage, and a willingness to go to the limits of our longing.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Spiral 00:44 Honoring Pain 04:21 Great Unraveling 07:17 Seeing With New Eyes 12:35 Through the Separate Self 17:51 Earth as Kin 22:03 Guided Sensing Practice 28:10 Going Forth 36:32 Courage and Discipline
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13572/

item.title: For The Sake of Life on Earth, part 1
item.description:
Can gratitude hold when the world is broken? The talk begins by showing gratitude as something that does not depend on favorable circumstances, then moves from that ground into the Work That Reconnects and a Shambhala warrior prophecy about facing ecological and political danger without turning away. Along the way, the speaker braids in a nuclear cleanup site, the tar sands, and a closing Rilke poem to ask how compassion and insight can become a practical strength for this time.
Location: Spirit Rock Meditation Center
Chapters: 00:00 Gratitude Beyond Circumstance 01:33 Facing the World Clearly 08:25 Grounded in the Work 11:54 Shambhala Prophecy 20:10 Suffering with the World 24:38 Tar Sands and Interdependence 35:51 Compassion Is Strength 39:12 Rilke's Bell Tower
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13571/

item.title: Healing Ecology - A Buddhist Perspective on the Eco Crisis
item.description:
If the separate self is a construct, what happens when we apply that same insight to civilization and the ecological crisis? The talk moves from a small tree-spirit story in the Vinaya to a wider argument that our habits of growth, control, and technological rescue mirror the very anxiety Buddhism points to in the individual mind. From there it widens again into evolution, cosmic creativity, and the possibility that practice is not only personal awakening but a collective change in how humanity lives within the biosphere.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 03:50 Trees and early Buddhism 09:22 Self and separation 14:56 Nonduality with earth 24:46 Civilization as construct 36:35 Growth and control 55:03 Cosmic creativity 01:05:05 Practice and transformation 01:19:46 Questions and closing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/13394/

item.title: Questioning Reality (Question and Answer Session 5)
item.description:
How do you work with emptiness without turning it into flatness or fear? The talk moves from a detailed exchange on "not me, not mine" into practical ways of balancing insight with metta, using examples from thoughts, the body, and even the simple act of looking at a tree or hearing birds. It then opens into a broader map of practice: how perception and knowing co-arise, how selfing and world-making fade together, and how clinging can sometimes be a healthy source of energy when it is gathered wisely.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the questions 05:07 Balancing with metta 11:46 What disidentifying means 17:17 Perception and knowing 24:12 Anatta and ownership 28:45 Trees, beauty, and fading 33:13 Flatness or aversion 46:22 Healthy clinging and desire 59:59 Three angles of practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12517/

item.title: Ways of Looking: (3) Anatta
item.description:
What if the point is not to prove whether there is a self, but to learn how identification is built and how to loosen it? The talk moves from the Buddha’s refusal to answer metaphysical questions directly into a practical exploration of anatta: noticing how body, feeling, thought, intention, and even awareness are quietly taken as me or mine. It then turns to a very concrete method—starting with the body, using light labels, and widening the field of disidentification—while warning against turning the practice into aversion or a project of wiping the self out.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The middle way 03:03 How self gets built 07:19 Practicing disidentification 12:26 Five aggregates map 16:02 Light labels and practice 20:16 Body and ownership 27:15 Expanding the range 34:06 Common pitfalls 40:36 Playful experimentation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12530/

item.title: Deepening Into Emptiness (Question and Answer Session 4)
item.description:
When does letting go reveal a thing’s emptiness, and when does it simply soften our relationship to it? The talk moves through a sequence of questions about vedana, aversion, clinging, fear, and lack, showing how these can loop back on themselves and how a genuine allowing can sometimes dissolve the whole pattern. From there it opens into self-inquiry, the felt construction of self, and a careful middle-way discussion of emptiness that keeps avoiding both reification and nihilism. Near the end, the talk broadens into silence, space, love, and the sense that experience is co-arising rather than fixed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and metta 03:08 Allowing the whole package 07:29 Loops of aversion 11:55 Clinging and pain 16:45 Lack and delusion 24:49 Body, self, and spaciousness 35:12 What emptiness means 48:19 Constructing self and phenomena 01:03:29 Dependent co-arising
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12520/

item.title: Ways of Looking: (2) Dukkha
item.description:
What if dukkha is less a statement about life and more a way of seeing how experience fails to fully satisfy? The talk moves from lightly labelling things as “unsatisfactory” into more practical instructions: noticing aversion and clinging in the body, using impermanence or dissolution to loosen the grip, and then getting close enough to allow or welcome what is there. It keeps returning to a quiet claim: most of the suffering comes not from the thing itself, but from the relationship with it, and that relationship can soften into relief, stillness, and a more subtle sensitivity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing ways of looking 01:44 Dukkha as unsatisfactory 04:22 Meister Eckhart's parallel 06:48 Not limiting life 12:55 Feeling push and pull 17:47 Using impermanence 19:49 Allowing and welcoming 25:17 Release and subtle dukkha 29:20 Direct or open practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12531/

item.title: Impermanence, Love, Emptiness (Question and Answer Session 3)
item.description:
Fear opens the talk: a listener describes terror in the stomach after seeing that what seemed solid and true may be only a mental construction, and the reply begins by naming that as a very common edge of emptiness. From there the talk moves carefully between spaciousness and care—meeting fear directly when needed, but also using metta and samadhi as cushions, and learning when to back off rather than over-effort. It then turns to love, consciousness, and emptiness as provisional ways of seeing, and to the deeper question of how practice can also expose the self that is doing the looking.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Fear at the edge 03:03 Groundlessness and fear 08:08 Kindness as cushion 14:21 Love and consciousness 27:20 Concepts as rafts 37:33 The question of effort 47:04 Turning inquiry inward 52:19 Self, moments, and change 01:01:18 Naming the self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12528/

item.title: Ways of Looking: (1) Anicca
item.description:
What happens when impermanence is treated not as an idea, but as a way of looking? The talk moves from simple examples of noticing change in sound, sensation, taste, and thought into a deeper inquiry into how perception builds solidity, self, and even suffering. It then turns to the practical question of how this lens interacts with metta and other forms of practice, with an emphasis on flexibility, direct experience, and what begins to loosen when the mind stops joining the dots so quickly.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 07:54 Three characteristics 11:40 Why this is subtle 14:22 Learning to notice change 23:55 Sense-door experiments 29:55 Seeing the visual field 33:30 Subtle change and intention 43:21 Letting go and self 50:42 Hard to overturn
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12529/

item.title: Contact and The Construction of Self
item.description:
How does suffering get built, and what happens if you stop feeding it? The talk traces emptiness as the construction of self, moving from the ways selfing intensifies through comparison, views, and aversion to two practical responses: loosening the story and staying at bare contact. It then turns to vedana, showing how pain, labeling, and the push away can thicken into a whole drama, and how simply staying with sensation can sometimes let that loosen or even dissolve.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 01:35 Noticing construction 04:04 Views and attention 09:59 Body and aversion 15:28 Comparison and conceit 20:32 Bare contact practice 25:51 Openness and luminosity 31:15 Addiction to self-making 41:50 Vedana and listening
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12513/

item.title: Question and Answer Session 2
item.description:
How do kindness, attention, and insight work when practice gets rough, or when it gets unexpectedly bright? The talk moves from a long exchange on meeting difficulty—through focusing, inner dialogue, and learning to rest or lean in as conditions require—into a widening account of metta as listening, as bodily spaciousness, and as compassion when the heart meets pain. From there it turns toward emptiness: how perception is shaped, how habits of mind are built and unbuilt, and how lovingkindness can become a direct route into seeing things as insubstantial and less fixed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 05:16 Working with difficulty 15:52 Metta as listening 21:43 Intensity and spaciousness 28:43 From pain to compassion 38:20 Devotion to kindness 43:53 Metta and perception 01:02:05 Emptiness and jhana 01:09:16 Fabrication and dependent arising
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12525/

item.title: Ending Blame
item.description:
What happens when blame is taken apart and seen as a narrowing of view? The talk starts with self-blame and moves outward through simple, concrete examples—mistakes at work, bad meditation, overeating, and relationship conflict—to show how actions and moods arise from many conditions, not a single failing self. It then turns to the practice of re-viewing: opening the frame, softening judgment, and using metta and emptiness together to loosen shame and self-criticism.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the problem 07:22 Conditions, not self 15:59 Food and fear 19:22 Relationships as dynamics 27:34 Re-viewing the past 38:05 Avijja and meaning 41:50 Metta softens the view 47:48 Practice, shame, and power
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12514/

item.title: Refining the Metta Practice (Question and Answer Session 1)
item.description:
How do you keep metta alive when you are walking, when the body is busy, or when pain and tension seem to pull the mind off course? The talk works through practical ways of refining the practice: using a visual field, centering in the heart, choosing an easier object, adjusting phrases, and letting the practice be both more local and more spacious as needed. From there it opens into a broader view of why metta matters at all: not just as a feeling to generate, but as a way of building enough inner nourishment for letting go, and as a path into insight about how perception is shaped by mind state.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 06:25 Walking with a friend 11:03 Cultivation and insight 14:07 Mindfulness and metta 19:52 Range, space, and choice 26:11 Words, tone, and deepening 33:53 Metta and perception 39:56 Entering metta in daily life 49:50 Pain, aversion, and fire
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12518/

item.title: Freeing the Self
item.description:
How does a rigid sense of self tighten suffering, and what happens when that grip is loosened? The talk begins with the Four Noble Truths and then stays close to practice, showing how self-definitions like “I’m a failure” or “I’m the kind of person who…” can feed a loop of pain, while careful attention can reveal relief, love, and a broader sense of possibility. It also distinguishes between seeing emptiness as a practical way of looking and misunderstanding it as nihilism, self-erasure, or a flattening of personality. Toward the end, the focus widens to the subtler felt sense of self across different moods, emotions, and meditation states, and to how that sense can become more open and less solid.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 00:59 Four Noble Truths 04:55 Middle way and emptiness 06:58 Practicing with selflessness 09:47 Responses to emptiness 13:33 Working with self-definitions 21:53 Love and relief 31:34 Stories of hidden dimensions 41:53 Sense of self and balance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12521/

item.title: Opening Talk for Metta and Emptiness (Level 1): Being on Retreat
item.description:
What if retreat is not a narrowing, but a way of seeing that can hold everything? The talk begins by framing the retreat as an experiment in support, spaciousness, and freedom, then turns to the pair of themes at its center: metta and emptiness, approached as two wings of practice. From there it moves through retreat logistics, a short body-based loosening, and a live inquiry into the words emptiness, love, kindness, and what they evoke in the body and mind. The result is less a fixed teaching than a careful opening of a shared field, where fear, relief, warmth, contraction, and tenderness are all allowed to belong.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:24 Opening Welcome 01:53 Freedom Through Seeing 04:51 Two Wings Of Practice 05:57 Emptiness And Inclusion 11:29 Retreat As Holding 17:20 No Splitting 25:01 Community And Form 38:48 Practical Grounding 54:46 Inquiry And Sitting
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12523/

item.title: Instructions on the Mindfulness of Thought
item.description:
How do you relate to thought without treating it as the enemy? The talk examines the way thoughts and mind states feed each other, how belief and identification tighten that loop, and how to work with obsessive thinking without trying to suppress it. Practical instructions move from returning to the breath or body, to metta, to noticing emotion and belief underneath the thinking, and finally to opening awareness wide enough to see thoughts arise and pass like brief flashes in space.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Thought Matters 04:25 Mind States and Feedback 07:36 Returning to the Anchor 09:03 Metta and Emotion 12:07 Body as Vantage Point 15:22 From Content to Process 17:19 Thoughts Arise and Pass 19:43 Space and Freedom 22:43 Guided Sitting
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11967/

item.title: Ending the Inner Critic (3)
item.description:
When the inner critic narrows attention into a tight loop, what helps break the spell is not argument but a shift in view. The talk moves through several ways of loosening self-judgment: seeing the critic as a conditioned process, turning toward what is genuinely beautiful or ethically important, and learning to relate to thoughts and sensations with more space. A personal story about asking what one’s deepest desire is shows how contact with a deeper direction can dissolve comparison and bring back openness, strength, and joy. It ends by making a strong case that the inner critic can be weakened, and even cut off at the root, through repeated practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Introducing the methods 03:02 The self-reinforcing loop 03:39 Shifting attention 04:47 Beauty and ethics 11:01 Deep aspiration 15:11 Seeing conditions 18:25 The web of causes 25:01 Thought and gullibility 31:44 Freedom is possible
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/35160/

item.title: Ending the Inner Critic (2)
item.description:
How do you meet the inner critic without feeding it more aversion? The talk moves through a set of practical approaches: softening with kindness, giving the critic space, feeling the bodily hurt it leaves behind, and then investigating what is actually being believed, joined together, and protected. From there it turns to dialogue, doubt, and finally reclaiming a sense of power through the body, the heart, and the thinking mind. A brief reference to mindfulness as “affectionate curiosity” helps frame the whole inquiry as a way of befriending what usually feels hostile.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the approach list 00:45 Mindfulness with kindness 03:25 Space and bodily tenderness 06:16 Investigating what is there 10:33 What the critic believes 15:49 Teasing apart the dots 19:48 Dialogue and skeptical inquiry 25:33 Protection, insight, and practice 30:59 Reclaiming power
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/35161/

item.title: Ending the Inner Critic (1)
item.description:
What happens when the inner critic turns practice into pressure, shame, and a constant sense of not being enough? The talk begins by naming that voice clearly, then shows how it distorts meditation into “should,” blocks inquiry, and even creates an inner rebel in response. From there, the speaker lays out a path for loosening its grip: acceptance, questioning, reclaiming inner power, and especially metta, or loving-kindness, as a way to retrain the whole pattern over time.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What the critic is 02:49 Not alone in this 05:49 Where it comes from 08:45 How practice gets pressured 12:07 Judgment blocks inquiry 15:00 Acceptance before inquiry 19:15 Challenging the critic 23:50 Metta as first practice 30:19 Love from others
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/35159/

item.title: The Psychodynamics in Meditation (Part 3 - Dimensions of the Personal Journey)
item.description:
How do we keep insight practice from flattening the human journey? The talk moves from a practical set of ways of working with emotion—simplifying attention, bringing warmth, changing view, and using old insights—into a larger question about whether the personality is merely something to drop or also a field of unfolding and discovery. From there it contrasts not-self practice with a more affirmative sense of a thread of becoming, and then asks what unlocks that thread: honest questioning, trust, desire, and a willingness to see difficult patterns as possible distortions of a deeper movement toward life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:05 Flexibility with emotion 05:22 Resources already in the field 08:58 Fabrication and emptiness 13:42 Milarepa's example 16:15 Four Noble Truths 18:24 The power of emptiness 26:53 Personality as journey 34:47 What locks it down 47:23 What unlocks it
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11504/

item.title: The Psychodynamics in Meditation (Part 2)
item.description:
How do emotions stay stuck, and what lets them move again? The talk returns to a central idea: emotional life has an innate dynamism that can be blocked, and meditation can help unfreeze it by differentiating what is actually present, sustaining attention, widening the field, questioning assumptions, and resourcing difficulty with qualities like compassion, confidence, and generosity. From there it moves into detailed examples of self-doubt, yearning and despair, inner images, and personal story, asking how practice can include more than bare sensation without getting trapped in judgment or a fixed self-view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:17 Reviewing the framework 04:12 Differentiation and attention 06:47 Questioning assumptions 10:23 Why inquiry needs love 17:22 Cultivating qualities 20:18 Finding inner resources 28:01 Yearning and hidden strength 36:55 Working with imagery 47:31 Using story and seeing anew
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11505/

item.title: The Psychodynamics in Meditation (Part 1)
item.description:
Can emotional stuckness be understood as something dynamic rather than fixed? The talk builds from that premise into a practical inquiry: how attention, body sense, questioning, and the wider field of experience can loosen repetitive emotional loops and let something more open emerge. Through examples ranging from inner ranting to sadness, romance, and self-view, the talk keeps returning to the same movement—notice more clearly, question the assumptions, and see what changes when the experience is felt in the body.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Emotions as dynamic 06:37 What blocks unfolding 20:01 Gathering attention 25:30 Broadening the field 33:48 Ranting and voice 40:01 Questioning assumptions 47:02 Self-view and fear 56:58 Body and self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11503/

item.title: The Mystery of Vedana: A Key to Liberation
item.description:
What if the small textures of pleasant, unpleasant, and neutral feeling are not minor details but the place where suffering first takes hold? The talk begins by widening the idea of freedom, then turns to vedana as a practical way of noticing how raw sensation becomes grasping, aversion, boredom, and papanca. From there it moves between simple instructions—stay with the bare feeling, loosen the clinging, let the body soften—and a deeper inquiry into how feeling, relationship, and even the sense of separate things may not be as fixed as they seem.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why vedana matters 05:28 Stationing attention 10:21 Three feeling tones 16:25 Reactivity and the second arrow 31:22 Stories that proliferate 39:45 Staying with bare feeling 47:55 Changing the relationship 55:17 From experience to emptiness 01:06:13 Come and see
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11502/

item.title: Selflessness and a Life of Love
item.description:
How do we live when time is short, suffering is real, and the heart keeps tightening around self-protection? The talk moves from mortality and the danger of apathy into a wide set of examples—Shantideva, Frankl, Rwanda, abolition, climate action—that ask whether love can stay engaged without collapsing into despair. From there it turns into a careful Dharma inquiry: how metta loosens fixed views of self and other, how emptiness changes perception, and how a more spacious heart can make compassion steadier rather than weaker.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Mortality and the question 05:38 Against docility 11:26 Radical teachers of love 16:49 Responsibility in the world 23:42 Hope, long view, and urgency 31:09 Emptiness and perception 41:23 Loosening self and other 52:38 Immeasurable heart 01:01:47 Radical love, quietly possible
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9989/

item.title: Heart Work
item.description:
When metta feels mechanical, what is really being cultivated, and what should we do when difficult emotions, fear, or judgment appear instead? The talk moves from the question of self-care into a careful map of how to stay with difficulty, when to turn directly toward it, and how to use simple supports like a softer body, a half smile, wonder, and kinder inner language. Later, it widens into the claim that the heart and mind are far more malleable than they seem, with examples from everyday practice and from Roz Savage’s radical life changes to show how small intentions and repeated actions can quietly reshape a life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Metta Has Nuance 03:24 Three Practice Modes 06:08 Caring for Suffering 12:48 Fear and Opening 18:44 Judgment and Space 23:14 Seven Skillful Responses 32:25 Story and Sensation 40:37 Feeding Metta 54:16 Intention and Change
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9982/

item.title: About Metta Practice
item.description:
How does metta move from a simple wish for well-being into a practice that can soften the heart, steady intention, and change the way the world is seen? The talk begins by placing loving-kindness within the wider Buddhist path, then distinguishes it from conditional or romantic love, stressing its boundlessness, unconditionality, and quiet strength. From there it turns to nourishment, the slow power of repeated intention, and the way practice can loosen the sense of separateness and open into a more communal, less self-enclosed view. It closes with a long guided opening of the body and awareness, extending well-wishing from oneself to the people in the room.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Welcome to Retreat 01:37 Awakening and Suffering 04:42 Two Wings of the Path 06:28 What Metta Means 11:16 Boundless and Strong 18:37 Nourishment and Skill 29:00 Cultivation and Intention 37:35 Bodhicitta and Widening 46:03 Shared Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9988/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
How does metta move from a simple wish for well-being into a practice that can soften the heart, steady intention, and change the way the world is seen? The talk begins by placing loving-kindness within the wider Buddhist path, then distinguishes it from conditional or romantic love, stressing its boundlessness, unconditionality, and quiet strength. From there it turns to nourishment, the slow power of repeated intention, and the way practice can loosen the sense of separateness and open into a more communal, less self-enclosed view. It closes with a long guided opening of the body and awareness, extending well-wishing from oneself to the people in the room.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Welcome to Retreat 01:37 Awakening and Suffering 04:42 Two Wings of the Path 06:28 What Metta Means 11:16 Boundless and Strong 18:37 Nourishment and Skill 29:00 Cultivation and Intention 37:35 Bodhicitta and Widening 46:03 Shared Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9988/

item.title: The Union of Appearance and Emptiness
item.description:
What happens when emptiness is not treated as a theory but as something that changes how solid the world feels? The talk begins with everyday craving and the way thought, memory, association, and mood build up the sense that things really exist, then moves into how mindfulness and equanimity can first sharpen and later dissolve that solidity. From there it opens into the harder questions: what the tradition means by cessation, why this can sound nihilistic, and how the middle way tries to hold both emptiness and causal life without collapsing into either extreme. The talk closes by returning to the union of appearances and emptiness, and to the claim that seeing that deeply makes care, ethics, and reverence more alive, not less.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the spiral 02:11 How solidity is built 05:15 Mindfulness and fading 12:02 What does fading mean? 17:49 Canon and cessation 23:34 No rebirth of appearances 31:47 Theravada and Mahayana 44:32 The razor's edge 59:07 Freedom with cause and effect
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9554/

item.title: Dependent Cessation and the Unconditioned
item.description:
What if the deepest freedom is not a special state you can possess, but the gradual unbinding of the way experience gets built? The talk moves from a broad map of practice—healing, ethics, intimacy, samadhi, insight—to a close examination of nibbana as the unconditioned, with a steady warning not to mistake absorption or vast awareness for the end of fabrication. Along the way, the speaker leans on Buddha passages, a Nagarjuna citation, and a few analogies of fading, sheaves of reeds, and a roof beam collapsing to show how craving, clinging, and the subject-object-time structure can cease together.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Practice as tools 05:48 Many strands of practice 11:19 Reasoning for freedom 14:05 What nibbana means 19:47 Transcendent or not 29:50 Fading and samadhi 38:09 Dependent cessation 49:10 Language and footing 56:33 Unbinding and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9541/

item.title: The Subtlety of Dependent Origination
item.description:
What if the sense of time is not the container for experience, but something fabricated along with self and object? The talk moves from everyday examples of waiting, dread, and meditation into a careful investigation of how past, future, and even the present can lose their solidity when clinging softens. From there it builds a three-part analysis of self, thing, and time, using Nagarjuna, the Buddha, and other references to show how dependent origination can be taken all the way into the structure of momentary experience. The result is not a dry argument, but a method for seeing how dukkha is assembled and how it can begin to unravel.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Map 02:17 Why Time Matters 04:08 Time and Clinging 09:40 Past, Future, Present 13:21 Seeing Through Now 20:44 Nagarjuna on Arising 26:13 Self, Thing, Time 33:41 The Tripod of Dukkha 46:09 Attention and Conceiving
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9553/

item.title: No Mind
item.description:
What if the thing that seems most basic — awareness itself — is also empty, dependently arisen, and not a final ground at all? The talk moves from a broad map of practice and the pull of the inner critic into a slow, careful dismantling of the tendency to make mind, luminosity, and vastness into something solid. Along the way, the speaker uses the poisonous tree image, a Zen mirror poem, early suttas, and later Mahayana and Tibetan sources to show how meditation can first seem to polish awareness and then, more deeply, reveal that there was never a thing there to grasp.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the map 07:38 Levels of practice 11:46 Awareness and suffering 17:51 The center of knowing 27:31 Luminous but not final 35:31 Translations and suttas 48:16 Looking for mind 01:01:04 Mutual dependence 01:09:48 Letting consciousness fade
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9547/

item.title: Love, Healing, and Emptiness
item.description:
If emptiness is real, why does it so often feel like it leads to less feeling, less warmth, or even nihilism? The talk turns that question around, arguing that seeing the empty, dependent nature of self and phenomena should loosen clinging, soften judgment, and open a more natural love and compassion. Through examples from metta, anger, healing, karma, and the Mahayana idea of bodhicitta, the talk keeps returning to one movement: less selfing, more spaciousness, and a fuller capacity to care.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Emptiness and love 03:26 Rumi and purification 08:19 Heart practices and self 14:47 Giving back and anger 23:56 Categories and perception 33:12 Mara and dependent arising 47:08 Healing and fabrication 58:37 Three levels of compassion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9545/

item.title: 'To see no-thing is to see excellently'
item.description:
The talk begins with self-doubt, self-criticism, and the reminder that many people on the retreat are already tasting emptiness in their practice, even if they don’t trust it. From there it moves into the emptiness of phenomena: how perception, feeling, body, and even consciousness are fabricated through dependent arising, and how seeing that can loosen suffering rather than flatten experience. The speaker works through this twice—first through close observation of how experience fades when its supports are withdrawn, then through Nagarjuna-style reasoning about parts, wholes, and motion, using examples like porridge, the body, and walking to show how solidity is mentally imposed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the field 02:20 Phenomena are empty 09:43 Dependent arising deepens 18:47 Fading and fabrication 33:27 Porridge, body, and parts 40:24 Ways of looking 44:40 Aggregates arise together 50:50 Walking and motion 01:02:36 Seeing no thing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9556/

item.title: Maps for the Journey (A Brief Overview)
item.description:
How does practice move from settling the mind, to looking at experience, to seeing how self, object, and even time are built? The talk begins as a review of earlier retreat themes—samatha, metta, the three characteristics, and open awareness—then widens into a map of emptiness, with detours into phenomenology, the Pali Canon, and later Buddhist philosophy. Later, the emphasis becomes very practical: how clinging changes pain, how perception fades when self-sense loosens, how the visual field can become less solid, and why ethical discernment still matters when emptiness deepens.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the Review 02:25 Samatha and Looking 05:51 Open Awareness and Methods 11:20 Finding What Works 15:13 Levels of Selfing 20:32 Phenomenology and Emptiness 26:29 Maps of Meditative Absorption 34:06 Pain, Pīti, and Sound 41:24 Grass, Field, and Projection
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9546/

item.title: Non-Duality and the Fading of Perception
item.description:
What if the world of experience is built by the way attention clings? The talk moves from a Buddha story about “the end of the world” into a close, practical examination of dualities, dependent origination, and how grasping helps make perceptions feel solid. It then follows that thread into meditation itself: pain, fear, boredom, and vividness are all treated as places where less clinging can let experience soften, fade, or even disappear. Along the way, the talk brings in Nagarjuna, Faith in Mind, the Buddha’s instruction to regard things as empty, and a few concrete examples from daily life and practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening intention 01:19 The end of the world 03:20 Dependent origination broadened 08:00 Dualities in experience 17:38 Practice without preferences 21:33 Clinging and solidity 28:35 From non-clinging to fading 41:37 Web of causes 50:34 Spectrum of fading
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9548/

item.title: Emptiness and the Vastness of Awareness
item.description:
What if the solid world, the self, and even awareness itself are all just ways of looking that can be loosened? The talk moves from the retreat’s basic instruction—less suffering, less clinging—into a careful review of emptiness of self and then emptiness of phenomena, using the morning meditation as a practical base. It then widens into spacious awareness, the way experience can seem to arise and dissolve within it, before turning to Chittamatra or mind-only as a provisional step rather than a final truth. By the end, the emphasis is less on holding a view than on using it skillfully, trusting experience while refining understanding.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 01:14 Retreat context 09:45 Dependence and solidity 12:14 Three characteristics 15:58 Fabrication and space 22:55 Awareness as backdrop 34:18 Flavors and conditioning 44:55 Mind-only lens 59:28 Not the final truth 01:09:31 Questions and application
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9542/

item.title: Concepts, Views, Reality
item.description:
How do concepts shape what we take to be real, and when does a useful practice turn into a fixed view? The talk moves from bare attention and the appeal of “going with the flow” into a tighter examination of emptiness, dependent arising, and the ways perceptions, selfing, and even spiritual language can quietly harden into assumptions. Along the way, the speaker keeps testing common Dharma phrases against the Pali Canon, arguing for views that can be used skillfully and then outgrown rather than clung to. It ends by turning emptiness back toward reverence, devotion, and a more careful relationship to practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Terrain 01:51 Views Shape Experience 06:44 Bare Attention And Suchness 16:37 Beyond Impermanence 27:32 Concepts That Undo Themselves 41:56 Right View, Not No View 54:02 Ethics And The Limits Of Relativism 01:04:43 Emptiness And Dependent Arising 01:16:18 Mutual Dependence And Reverence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9540/

item.title: The Seven-Fold Reasoning (The self cannot be found anywhere)
item.description:
What if the self can’t be found because it is never there in the way it seems to be? The talk starts by naming that tension directly: it’s easy to say the self is an illusion, harder to investigate it so closely that the claim becomes felt rather than merely believed. From there it unfolds a careful meditative reasoning practice, using Chandrakirti’s sevenfold analysis of the chariot and the five aggregates to test whether the self can be found as a part, apart from parts, a possessor, a collection, or a temporal continuum. The talk stays practical even when the logic gets intricate: first clarify the self-sense, then settle into conviction about what is being examined, then work through the seven possibilities until the sense of self begins to dissolve into a meaningful emptiness. Along the way it contrasts this approach with philosophical self-theories, warns against slipping into nihilism or a vague oneness, and keeps returning to the question of how insight can be taken into meditation and actually free the heart.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why This Matters 02:02 From Anatta to Inquiry 04:58 Chandrakirti and the Chariot 09:38 Preparing the Mind 20:20 Three Steps of Practice 29:43 The First Two Reasonings 42:13 Body, Feeling, and Possession 01:06:44 Continuity and Consciousness 01:28:05 Emptiness, Not Blankness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9552/

item.title: A Wise Relationship To Practice
item.description:
How do you relate to practice: as kindness, as measurement, or as a way of proving yourself? The talk follows that question into effort, showing how aspiration, confidence, joy, and rest can support practice when they are balanced, and how the inner critic can quietly distort the whole path. It then turns to progress, difficulty, and emptiness, asking how to keep questioning without slipping into self-judgment, complacency, or fear.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 04:20 Practice as kindness 06:43 What effort means 08:54 Aspiration and balance 13:37 Confidence and progress 17:38 Deepening without strain 30:17 Difficulty as fertilizer 46:36 Keeping practice alive 01:07:36 Anatta as a way of looking
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9538/

item.title: Three Characteristics - Three Avenues to Freedom and Joy
item.description:
What if the point of meditation is not to endure suffering, but to see how the mind builds it and then learns to build it less? The talk moves through impermanence, dukkha, and not-self as practical lenses: first noticing change, then relaxing grasping and aversion, then loosening the habit of taking experience as me and mine. Along the way, the speaker distinguishes this approach from darker readings of the three characteristics and keeps returning to the same criterion: does the practice bring less suffering, more freedom, and more ease?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Practice Matters 04:20 Three Lenses 08:24 Dukkha and Letting Go 11:51 Not-Self in Practice 21:28 Working With Difficulty 30:25 Impermanence, Emptiness, Suffering 37:39 Relational Craving 51:55 Self, Strategy, and Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9555/

item.title: The Experience Of Self (Personality and Beyond)
item.description:
The talk starts with a careful turn away from slogans like “the ego is the problem” toward a more practical question: what actually leads to suffering, and what leads to freedom? From there, it moves through the ordinary ways self is helpful in life, then into the ways we get locked into personality, story, comparison, and the inner critic. The speaker keeps returning to a gentler inquiry: can a particular self-definition be seen as not quite me, or not quite mine, and can loosening it bring a little more space? Near the end, the talk broadens into dependent arising, the role of actions rather than self-view, and the idea that emptiness does not erase functioning but can make it feel lighter and more open.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing selflessness 01:26 Self and suffering 05:11 Healing the person 08:16 Story and identity 12:25 Personality-level suffering 17:11 Questioning definitions 20:30 The spectrum of self 25:34 Meditation as selfing 33:45 Appreciation and familiarization
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9551/

item.title: Samadhi and Insight (a few pointers)
item.description:
What if samadhi is not a narrow effort to hold attention, but a whole-body settling that supports insight? The talk moves from correcting the usual idea of concentration, to showing how samadhi, emptiness practice, and even the hindrances all belong in one larger field of training. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to a practical question: how do we work without self-judgment, and let the mind become steadier, softer, and less fabricated?
Location: Gaia House

Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9550/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
What if samadhi is not a narrow effort to hold attention, but a whole-body settling that supports insight? The talk moves from correcting the usual idea of concentration, to showing how samadhi, emptiness practice, and even the hindrances all belong in one larger field of training. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to a practical question: how do we work without self-judgment, and let the mind become steadier, softer, and less fabricated?
Location: Gaia House

item.title: An Introduction to Emptiness
item.description:
Emptiness begins here as a challenge to how experience seems: the talk asks how things, selves, and situations come to feel so solid when they are so dependent on view, context, and the mind’s own constructions. Rather than staying with abstract philosophy, the speaker moves through ordinary examples—fear, relationships, work, nations, lunch, weather—to show how the mind joins the dots and makes suffering out of what is more fluid than it appears. The practice emphasis is on deliberate contemplation: noticing where attention gets sucked into difficulty, questioning the story, and seeing the gaps that appear when experience is examined closely. Classical references to Nagarjuna, the Buddha, and other teachers are used to keep the teaching anchored in a middle way between reification and nihilism.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 05:46 Why emptiness matters 12:44 What emptiness means 19:45 Root delusion 24:15 Everyday fabrication 31:18 Relationship and attention 39:14 Dot-to-dot experience 49:00 Dependent arising and mind 01:04:39 Middle way and practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9539/

item.title: Opening Talk - Orienting and Relating to the Emptiness Retreat
item.description:
What if emptiness is not a remote pinnacle, but the thread running through generosity, ethics, love, and freedom? The talk opens by orienting listeners to a four-week retreat for experienced meditators, then quickly shifts from logistics to the central question of how to meet the retreat itself: with openness, humility, and a willingness to experiment rather than just sit with what is already familiar. A strong emphasis falls on how emptiness and dependent origination are linked, how the teachings can feel challenging or even frightening at first, and why the heart needs to be kept warm through metta, gratitude, aspiration, and the precepts.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Welcome and logistics 02:49 Emptiness at the center 05:57 How to meet retreat 13:42 Finding your approach 19:03 Difficulty and openness 28:05 Keeping the heart juicy 33:17 Freedom and love 38:28 The retreat container 42:52 Aspirations and precepts
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9549/

item.title: 'There are hundreds of ways to kneel and kiss the ground' (A Talk on Devotion)
item.description:
What if devotion is not a foreign add-on to meditation, but something already shaping how we live, choose, and bow? The talk moves from the many forms devotion can take — praise, awe, surrender, aspiration, ritual, prayer, even attention itself — into a broader question of what we are actually aligning with. Along the way, it brings in Christmas imagery, Mary Oliver, Rumi, and stories about Copenhagen and Mother Teresa to show how devotion can become service, witness, and a quiet opening of the heart. It ends by asking whether practice is helping us love more widely, let go more deeply, and recognize the many ways to “kneel and kiss the ground.”
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Heart 03:07 Many Forms of Devotion 12:38 Ritual and Culture 18:19 Attention as Prayer 23:15 Pilgrimage in Action 31:44 Objects and Embodiment 41:13 Longing as Message 45:50 Feeding the Flame 53:00 Letting Go of Forms
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10029/

item.title: Emptiness and Insight Meditation
item.description:
What if the real problem in suffering is not pain itself, but the mind’s habit of making experience feel solid, fixed, and inherently real? The talk moves from the Buddha’s claim about suffering and its end into a close look at delusion, emptiness, and the ways mindfulness can reveal gaps in what seems so substantial. It then develops several practical ways of approaching emptiness through present-moment investigation, self-definition, labeling, and the gradual letting go that makes even painful experience begin to dissolve. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from a philosophical idea to a practice that can open into joy, compassion, and less burdened life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The root of suffering 03:51 Delusion and inherent existence 10:18 What emptiness means 15:41 Insight and convention 22:08 Construction as practice 27:22 The golden question 30:10 Five ways in 39:37 From presence to letting go 49:20 Joy, compassion, and service
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10025/

item.title: The Wisdom of Non-Duality
item.description:
What happens when practice is driven by striving, and when does it become a subtle way of making self and world more solid? The talk moves from early Buddhist language of seeking and the fruits of practice, through the pull of doing versus non-doing, into increasingly subtle questions about self, awareness, and the appearances of things. It includes references to the Dhammapada, the Kaccayana teaching on not falling into either existence or non-existence, and Rumi, but keeps returning to a pragmatic question: what actually reduces suffering and opens freedom?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Non-Duality Means 04:17 Pragmatic Early Buddhism 07:47 What a Good Path Shows 12:16 Doing and Non-Doing 19:00 Buddha Nature and Longing 28:51 No-Self and Vastness 43:27 Rumi and the Middle Way 50:06 Dependent Arising Deepens 58:49 Training the View
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10028/

item.title: Emotions and Freedom
item.description:
What if the problem is not the emotion itself, but the way the mind meets it? The talk begins by defining citta as both mind and heart, then moves through body sensations, thoughts, perception, and the subtle ways experience gets colored, amplified, or simplified by aversion, craving, and labeling. From there it widens into a more complex argument: emotions can be held with care, but they can also be seen as constructed and malleable, which opens a path toward cultivating wholesome states and understanding what freedom might mean in practice.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Citta and Mind States 01:31 What Mind States Include 04:13 Mind as Forerunner 08:49 Reading the Body 15:56 Intimacy and Delicacy 24:14 Feedback Loops 31:49 How We Relate 39:38 Fabrication and Cultivation 50:44 Freedom Through Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10024/

item.title: Truthfulness and Authenticity
item.description:
What if the main obstacle to practice is not lack of understanding, but the ways we quietly drift from what we know is true? The talk moves from ordinary dishonesty and self-protection into the subtler habits of self-deception, inner criticism, and the ways perception is shaped by fear, craving, and mind state. Along the way, the speaker uses examples from Joshua Bell’s unnoticed metro performance, climate-change perception, Rumi, and the Bodhisattva path to ask how honesty, discernment, and wholehearted intention can actually be lived rather than merely affirmed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 02:15 Speaking Truly 08:25 Self-Deception and Bullshit 13:35 Perception and Context 20:18 Slow Changes, Shifting Minds 28:07 What Practice Is For 35:51 Deep Desire and Intention 44:06 The Inner Critic 51:51 Honesty, Care, and Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10030/

item.title: Mindfulness, Insight, Liberation
item.description:
What if mindfulness is not mainly a matter of being calmly present, but of learning how to look so that suffering can unwind? The talk moves from common modern ideas like bare attention and simple acceptance into a closer reading of the Four Foundations of Mindfulness, where experience is categorized, held in mind, and examined through lenses that support letting go. From there it widens into the roles of appropriate attention, craving, clinging, and the way perception itself is shaped, arguing that mindfulness matters because of the freedom it can help reveal, not because it is the whole path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What mindfulness means 03:23 Bare attention and its limits 08:13 Appropriate attention 12:40 Not the same as awakening 18:30 The Satipatthana Sutta 24:41 Mindfulness with an agenda 31:52 Four Noble Truths in practice 39:51 How perception is built 49:41 What real insight requires
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10027/

item.title: Joyful Effort
item.description:
What if effort is not a grim push, but a way of finding what the heart actually cares about? The talk moves from the obvious hard work of practice into aspiration, faith, goals, self-criticism, and the way the Buddha reframes the path as skill: something learned through mistakes, adjustment, and attention to what leads toward suffering or away from it. It then widens into a more spacious view of effort as something offered for others, before returning to the practical question of how to balance persistence with rest so practice stays alive, buoyant, and joyful.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Effort Matters 04:18 Aspiration and Meaning 09:45 Faith and Capacity 18:49 Skill, Not Self 23:35 Heroic Dedication 27:16 Right Effort in Practice 36:48 Balancing the Energy 50:22 Joyful, Irreversible Effort
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10026/

item.title: Freedom, Reality, and the Razor's Edge
item.description:
Is freedom found in seeing things more clearly, or in seeing through the very habit of building reality at all? The talk moves from ordinary mindfulness, where story and self begin to quiet, into sharper inquiry: impermanence, the feeling of a stable existential self, and then the more radical question of how suffering, self, and even experience get fabricated. It keeps testing the listener’s assumptions, including whether spacious awareness or a sense of a ground of being is really final, and repeatedly returns to dependent origination, emptiness, and the difficult “razor’s edge” between appearance and emptiness. By the end, the emphasis is less on having a special state than on learning not to build solidity, while letting that understanding open into freedom, compassion, and trust.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Inquiry 02:05 Delusion Beneath Clinging 06:52 Quieting Story and Self 09:51 Ordinary Radiance 12:16 Impermanence and Groundlessness 18:56 Awareness as a Ground 30:21 Dependent Origination 44:00 Beyond Nihilism 50:32 The Razor's Edge
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9822/

item.title: Breath and Space - Reactivity, Emptiness, Piti (4th Instructions)
item.description:
What if the problem is not the pain or pleasure itself, but the grasping, pushing away, and drifting that arise around it? The talk moves from this basic picture of reactivity and suffering into the practice of calming reactivity so that space, selflessness, and the sense of solidity can be seen more clearly. It then turns to breath meditation as a way to cultivate modest comfort, gradually spread it through the body, and develop pīti—an energised, pleasurable quality—as a steady resource rather than a special event.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reactivity and suffering 02:30 Space and emptiness 05:28 Breath as resource 09:00 Colours of pleasure 12:05 Developing pīti 15:48 Enjoyment and skill 20:06 Dependent arising
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9814/

item.title: The Self and Its Search for Security
item.description:
What if the drive for security is quietly turning money, self-image, and even the future into sources of worry? The talk starts with the sense of a vulnerable self that reaches for ground, then turns to money as a revealing test case: how fear, scarcity, respect, and survival get tangled together, and how a different relationship to resources might be possible. It moves from social examples and a Harlem fundraising story into a closer inquiry into self-view itself, showing how the self is built and reinforced through memory, mood, and reactivity. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from trying to shore up a fixed self to cultivating inner qualities that can actually be trusted.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 03:03 Groundlessness and Money 07:58 Money as Flow 12:59 Scarcity and Distortion 21:44 Frog, Flood, and Stewardship 29:29 Wise Security 36:13 Self-View and Story 45:29 How Self Is Built 53:27 Putting It Down
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9819/

item.title: Breath Meditation (3rd Instructions)
item.description:
Breath meditation here is presented as a practical art of responding to changing mind states rather than forcing a fixed method. The talk moves through common hindrances—especially dullness, restlessness, and thinking—and suggests simple adjustments in posture, breath, effort, and attention: sometimes energizing with a long inhale, sometimes softening with the exhale, sometimes backing off to a lighter, more receptive stance. Threaded through it all is the insistence on kindness, patience, and not taking the mind’s waves personally, even when practice feels rough. The closing section settles into a long guided sit, opening awareness to the whole body and the more subtle feel of breath energy.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Breath as an Art 02:50 Kindness and Orientation 04:05 Working with Hindrances 07:34 Responding in Practice 10:23 Adjusting Effort 14:14 Thought and Breath 20:08 Guided Practice 22:31 Settling Into Space
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9821/

item.title: Blessing Life - The Power of the Generous Heart
item.description:
Generosity begins as ordinary giving, but the talk keeps pressing into a harder question: what if giving is not a side virtue, but a direct path into freedom? Drawing on the Buddha’s lay teachings, Jesus’ sayings on losing life to find it, and stories of service, the talk moves from practical habits around money, time, and attention toward the claim that stinginess, fear, and self-preoccupation shape how the world appears. It ends by turning generosity into a real practice of stretching, noticing, and letting the heart open enough for a different sense of self, abundance, and release.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Generosity in context 03:20 Three dimensions of giving 07:10 Giving beyond self-preoccupation 13:42 Fear, culture, and trust 24:19 Happiness and deepening 31:55 Training the heart 39:38 Practice, wisdom, and self 52:46 Freedom, emptiness, and release 01:05:26 Leap of trust
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9810/

item.title: Liberation From the Inner Critic
item.description:
How do you meet the voice that says you are failing, unworthy, or not doing practice right? The talk traces that inner critic from its shame and pressure into a more workable relationship through kindness, metta, spacious mindfulness, and careful inquiry. Along the way, it keeps returning to a practical question: can the critic be seen as a pattern of conditions rather than a truth about the self, and can that seeing actually loosen its grip?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Naming the critic 04:05 Where it comes from 07:01 How practice gets distorted 12:03 Metta as an ally 17:02 Friendly mindfulness 24:23 Precision and deconstruction 34:49 Questioning the story 47:00 From shame to action 59:59 Freedom and what remains
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9815/

Along the way, it keeps returning to a practical question: can the critic be seen as a pattern of conditions rather than a truth about the self, and can that seeing actually loosen its grip?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Naming the critic 04:05 Where it comes from 07:01 How practice gets distorted 12:03 Metta as an ally 17:02 Friendly mindfulness 24:23 Precision and deconstruction 34:49 Questioning the story 47:00 From shame to action 59:59 Freedom and what remains
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9815/

item.title: Opening the Sense of What's Possible
item.description:
What if practice is not only about learning to be with difficulty, but about refusing to let your sense of what’s possible stay small? The talk moves from the preciousness of retreat and the limits of a narrow focus on suffering into a wider invitation: to notice intentions, question self-doubt, and consider aspiration as something deep, honest, and life-shaping. It then opens out further into bodhicitta, ethical responsibility, and the possibility that practice can become a force for inner and outer transformation. Along the way, the speaker weighs spacious acceptance against the need to challenge ourselves, and ends by returning to silence, simplicity, and the conditions that let deeper longing surface.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:05 Welcome and tone 01:38 Retreat as devotion 05:14 Aspirations and intentions 11:08 Insight and its limits 15:54 Freedom as duty 19:38 Beyond the self 27:41 World and responsibility 32:44 Blockages and confidence 40:16 Cultivation and investigation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/9816/

item.title: Realizing the Nature of Mind
item.description:
What if the calm, spacious sense of awareness that meditation can open into is only a halfway view? The talk moves through several familiar ways of understanding mind — mirror-like clarity, momentary consciousness, vast space, one mind — and then keeps questioning each one, showing where it helps practice and where it can quietly harden into a view. By the end, the emphasis has shifted toward a harder but deeper inquiry: seeing how awareness, objects, and time are all dependently made, empty, and therefore less solid than they first appear.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the inquiry 03:30 Mind as mirror 09:27 Momentary consciousness 11:34 Awareness as space 17:11 Attractive but incomplete 23:29 Questioning the view 33:18 Dependence and emptiness 41:29 Reasoning about time 53:19 Groundless mind
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/6349/

item.title: No Self, No World
item.description:
What if the question is not whether the self exists, but whether a way of seeing is reducing suffering? The talk moves from a pragmatic reading of anatta into a close look at how self is built through story, past and future, and the subtle habits of push and pull that hold experience in place. From there it opens into the sense that self and world arise together, so that loosening one loosens the other, and the talk keeps returning to practice as a way of looking rather than a fixed view. It ends by widening this into dependent origination, with a strong warning not to turn emptiness into nihilism or a new philosophy to defend.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:42 Anatta as practice 02:24 Self and not-self 05:54 Healing through self 12:37 Examining identity 17:53 Mindfulness and story 21:41 Ways of conceiving 29:08 Self and world together 45:13 Fabrication and lessening 53:32 Emptiness beyond extremes
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/6300/

item.title: Into the depths of Silence
item.description:
What if silence is not the absence of life, but a way of hearing how life actually unfolds? The talk moves from retreat silence, fear, and the habit of filling space into a careful exploration of listening, stillness, and the way silence can soften grasping, loosen self-definition, and reveal what we truly care about. Along the way, the speaker uses stories, poetry, and contemplative examples to show how silence can be both disorienting and quietly transformative. The talk gradually widens from the silence of retreat to a more encompassing sense of space, awareness, and even love.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Theme 05:09 Silence as Threshold 11:16 Missing the Vastness 17:47 Listening to Silence 27:30 Neruda and Collective Stillness 29:26 Practice in the Field 39:53 Self, Reality, and Nothing 52:45 Beyond Measurement 57:47 Closing Silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/6275/

item.title: What is ‘Insight’?
item.description:
What is insight, really: a passing understanding, a useful way of looking, or something that can cut deeper into suffering itself? The talk starts with a simple map and then moves through personal, universal, and ultimate insight, showing how each can lessen dukkha and how some insights need to be used and repeated until they become part of the way we see. It then turns to the three characteristics—impermanence, dukkha, and not-self—as deliberate lenses, before opening into dependent origination, emptiness, and the middle way beyond the habit of making things either solidly real or unreal.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Forest Refuge
Chapters: 00:14 Opening the map 03:36 What insight means 12:33 Universal truths 15:53 Relative and ultimate 20:49 Using and consolidating 26:09 Learning ways of seeing 31:25 Three characteristics 42:19 Beyond fabrication 52:52 Middle way and emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/6254/

item.title: The Unfabricated, The Deathless...
item.description:
What happens when the path is traced from ordinary mindfulness all the way into the fading of subject, object, and time? The talk begins with a broad map of practice—presence, relationship, healing, beauty, and samadhi—before narrowing to the question of what the Buddha means by the deathless, the unfabricated, and the unconditioned. From there it moves through repeated warnings not to pre-decide the answer, a long series of quotations from the suttas, and a careful distinction between pleasant absorptions, deepening emptiness, and the cessation of fabrication itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice as a wide path 01:28 What the path includes 06:47 Nirvana and the deathless 13:20 Not pre-deciding truth 17:00 Fading and samadhi 23:19 Dependent arising deepens 31:45 Reed, house, and cessation 39:25 Beyond language and footing 48:17 Questions at the edge
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11135/

item.title: Dependent Origination, Awareness, and Time
item.description:
What if time is not the container everything happens in, but another dependently arisen pattern, held together by clinging, selfing, and the way consciousness meets objects? The talk moves from a careful analysis of the present moment—asking whether it is one or many, and whether it arises from itself, other, both, or neither—into a practice-oriented look at how time, attention, and the sense of self support one another. It then widens into dependent origination more generally, with the Buddha’s awakening used as a model for how mutual dependency can be seen directly rather than only thought about. The emphasis stays practical throughout: use the concepts, test them in meditation, and see what loosens when the sense of time is no longer taken to be solid.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 A Map of Practice 02:25 Time Feels Solid 05:37 Past and Future 10:05 Awareness and Time 13:26 One or Many 18:49 Arising From None 23:16 The Tripod of Self 36:00 Dependent Arising Deepens 44:22 Subtle Mind Factors
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11120/

item.title: On the Nature of Mind
item.description:
How far can the feeling of a knowing mind be trusted before it, too, has to be let go of? The talk moves from practical reminders about attitude and compassion into a careful map of stages: first seeing consciousness as not-self, then questioning the allure of luminous or spacious awareness, and finally asking what it means for mind itself to lack inherent existence. Along the way, the speaker leans on stories, canonical passages, and Zen and Tibetan sources to show why these distinctions matter for freedom rather than for theory alone.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 05:35 Why emptiness matters 12:57 From self to center 20:55 The mirror image 23:59 Provisional luminous states 28:36 Luminous mind revisited 38:52 What cannot be found 49:18 Knowing and known 59:45 Practice beyond the view
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11128/

item.title: The Freedom of No-thing-ness
item.description:
What if the solid thing you’re trying to work with in meditation is only being held together by the mind’s habit of naming, linking, and believing? The talk moves from a reminder to care for the heart on retreat into a detailed analysis of emptiness: how whole and parts depend on each other, how labels fix experience into fear or pain, and how the body, movement, and even time become harder to pin down than they first appear. Near the end, the reasoning is brought back to practice, with a suggestion to use these insights as portable ways of looking — while also knowing when to return to simple samadhi and metta.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Taking Care of Heart 03:22 Seeing No Thing 05:12 Wholes and Parts 16:11 Applying the Analysis 19:59 Body Boundaries 25:55 Aggregates and Labels 30:07 Dependence in Experience 38:05 Walking and Motion 44:58 Portable Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11133/

item.title: Love, Karma, and Healing
item.description:
If the self is a conditioned process, what happens to love, ethics, and healing? The talk keeps returning to that question, moving from emptiness and dependent arising into the way clinging builds self, world, and suffering—and how loosening it can open warmth, clarity, and care. It also stays practical, asking when to work intensely, when to simplify, and how to use compassion, mindfulness, and even imagination without making healing into a new form of self-drama. Along the way, references to Shantideva, Nagarjuna, Chandrakirti, and the Buddha help frame the argument that insight and compassion are not separate paths.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why emptiness opens love 03:17 Less clinging, less separation 08:13 Mindfulness and metta together 12:19 Heroic practice and release 17:16 Compassion without burden 24:36 Seeing the world as malleable 32:26 Karma, reasoning, and fading 40:16 Healing, storms, and clinging 54:01 Awakening and fabrication
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11127/

item.title: Two Talks: 1- Mindfulness and Emptiness - The Foundations; 2 - The Ending of the World
item.description:
What if the problem is not the weather, the pain, or the thought, but the way the mind makes them seem solid? The talk begins with mindfulness as a way of seeing how suffering gets built from fleeting sensations and mental labeling, then moves into emptiness as a deeper inquiry into dependent arising, dualities, and the mutual dependence of things and grasping. Along the way, it turns practical: how to work with fear, boredom, pleasure, aversion, and the subtle ways experience is fabricated and can begin to fade.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Mindfulness and suffering 04:43 Fabrication as root 09:05 Dualities arise together 11:00 Context and clinging 19:41 Practice without preferences 28:48 What clinging means 32:09 Objects fading away 43:24 Dependent arising in depth 51:49 Warnings and fear
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11132/

item.title: Cittamatra
item.description:
What if the suffering you take to be solid is being built, moment by moment, by the mind itself? The talk moves from a recap of three-characteristics practice into a more expansive inquiry: how self, emotions, pain, and even the world can seem substantial, then loosen into a spacious awareness that holds everything without strain. From there it opens into Chittamatra, using the language of mind-only, dream, and impressions in awareness to show one way this practice can be developed, while also warning that it is only a provisional step and not the final view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions 03:27 What Practice Is For 05:38 Self and Suffering 10:03 From Things To Experience 20:02 Three Characteristics To Space 23:31 Impressions In Awareness 33:32 Awareness, Names, And Rigpa 45:02 Mind-Only Reasoning 53:00 Provisional View
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11119/

item.title: Integrity and the Poetics of Unknowing
item.description:
What if the problem is not thought itself, but the deep habit of believing that what appears is solid, self-existing, and already true? The talk moves from mindfulness and bare attention, through a critique of romantic ideas of “flow” and “suchness,” into a sharper argument that emptiness is not just impermanence but seeing how self, world, and time are constructed through clinging and conception. It also keeps testing a live tension in the teaching: whether a serious inquiry into reality closes the heart, or whether it can open into a quieter, more precise kind of wonder without losing integrity.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Mindfulness and thought 05:12 Beyond vivid appearances 12:13 Impermanence is not enough 16:12 Non-construction of reality 24:46 Concepts shape suffering 34:36 No other cause to peace 47:01 Agnosticism and discernment 55:46 How self is built 01:07:55 Beyond words
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11126/

item.title: Chandrakirti's Chariot and the Unfindable Self
item.description:
How do you look for a self that feels so obvious and still fail to find it? The talk builds a practical emptiness meditation around Chandrakirti’s chariot, using seven possible ways a self could exist and showing, step by step, why each one falls apart. It moves back and forth between reasoning and direct experience, until the sense of self is treated less as a solid thing and more as something unfindable, dependent, and empty of inherent existence.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the practice 03:10 Inherent existence 11:00 Why the chariot matters 20:52 Sevenfold analysis 39:25 Perceiver and perceived 46:23 Continuum and time 54:00 Analysis and samadhi 56:28 Using the method
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11118/

item.title: Exploring the Relationship with Practice
item.description:
How do you keep practice alive without turning it into self-criticism, impatience, or a flat routine? The talk keeps returning to the feel of effort itself: whether there is aspiration, confidence, joy, and rest, and whether the listener is relating to practice with openness or with hidden pressure. From there it moves through progress, not understanding, and the temptation to settle too soon, ending with a practical image of practice as sailing—responding to changing conditions while still keeping a direction in view.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the Frame 05:58 Wise Effort 22:08 Progress and Patience 31:05 Learning to Understand 37:21 Seeds and Depth 46:46 Risk and Balance 54:01 Tightness and Sailing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11123/

item.title: Practicing with the Three Characteristics
item.description:
How do the three characteristics actually work as meditation, not as doctrine? The talk moves from a recap of morning instructions into a practical map: using impermanence, dukkha, and not-self as lenses that loosen grasping, calm the mind, and gradually reduce identification with body, thought, feeling, and even awareness itself. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to a simple refrain: don’t force experience into theory; use whatever is present—difficulty, tiredness, scatteredness, or ease—as a place to practice letting go.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the framework 03:17 The three characteristics 07:06 Balancing samadhi and insight 12:02 Working with difficulty 17:04 Impermanence and dukkha 23:41 Not-self through letting go 34:23 The anatta practice 50:59 Subtlety and support 01:03:57 Nothing owned
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11130/
