Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to a simple refrain: don’t force experience into theory; use whatever is present—difficulty, tiredness, scatteredness, or ease—as a place to practice letting go.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the framework 03:17 The three characteristics 07:06 Balancing samadhi and insight 12:02 Working with difficulty 17:04 Impermanence and dukkha 23:41 Not-self through letting go 34:23 The anatta practice 50:59 Subtlety and support 01:03:57 Nothing owned
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11130/

item.title: The Sense of Self
item.description:
What is the self actually doing when it feels so solid, and when does that solidity begin to loosen? The talk moves from a traditional distinction between the emptiness of self and phenomena into a detailed, practical investigation of selfing: self-story, inner critic, identity, guilt, and the habit of making everything mean something about “me.” It keeps returning to the question of suffering, showing how different ways of looking—at actions, at dependent arising, at moments when practice quiets the mind—can expose the self as constructed, useful in some contexts, but not inherently existing. The closing movement turns toward practice instructions: not identifying, not clinging to definitions, and learning to see where freedom begins.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing emptiness 02:36 Self as a tool 10:37 Story and identity 16:40 Seeing self-definitions 22:31 Inner critic and the early texts 27:46 Spectrum of selfing 35:44 Samadhi and fabrication 41:19 Conceiving self differently 54:11 Dependent arising and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11134/

item.title: Samadhi in the Practice of Emptiness
item.description:
What if samadhi is less about microscopic focus and more about a gathered, unified mind that feels spacious, steady, and workable? The talk moves from correcting common ideas about concentration to showing how samadhi supports emptiness practice: it cushions fear, steadies investigation, and keeps insight from tipping into dryness or nihilism. Along the way, the speaker treats meditation as a learnable skill shaped by happiness, patience, playfulness, and whole-body awareness, with breath and metta as the main ways in.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 What Samadhi Means 03:39 Why It Matters 09:49 Climate for Inquiry 11:57 Beyond Tight Focusing 18:57 A Learnable Skill 21:48 Working With Hindrances 28:33 Body and Tightness 32:42 Subtle Body and Insight 39:23 Practical Guidance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11131/

item.title: Emptiness
item.description:
How do things seem so solid when they are so dependent on the mind that sees them? The talk begins by clearing away a common misunderstanding of emptiness as a blank or depressed state, then moves through the Heart Sutra, ordinary examples like relationships, countries, and a chair, and gradually pushes toward the more radical claim that all phenomena lack inherent existence. It ends by stressing that this is not a dry philosophy: emptiness is something to be trained in, and seeing it more deeply is meant to loosen suffering without collapsing into nihilism.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What emptiness is not 03:00 Heart Sutra frame 04:38 Everyday dependence 14:07 Retreat and conditions 27:47 Mind imputes objects 37:51 Nothing is exempt 48:05 Practice over theory 55:56 Compassion and liberation
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11121/

item.title: Beyond Impermanence
item.description:
Impermanence is the starting point, but the talk keeps asking what lies beyond simply noticing that things change. It moves from ordinary daily shifts in mood and circumstance, through aging, death, and grief, toward the question of how practice changes the way we relate to experience, time, and self. Along the way, it distinguishes the tenderness of going with the flow from a deeper freedom that may include letting go of even subtle views and constructions.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Impermanence as practice 02:35 Tracking ordinary change 05:41 Beauty, grief, and loss 10:05 The scale of disappearing 15:48 Aging and inheritance 25:09 Death, urgency, compassion 33:20 Changing the way we see 43:02 Beyond the flow 50:56 Constructed time and objects
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10830/

item.title: The Meaning of Refuge
item.description:
What does it really mean to take refuge when money, relationships, comfort, and even our plans keep changing? The talk begins by stripping refuge away from ritual definition and asking what we actually rely on in life, then turns toward fear, impermanence, and the limits of worldly supports. From there it builds a case for refuge as practice: right view, the full eightfold path, the possibility of freedom in each moment, and the support of Sangha and admirable friends. The movement is quiet but exacting, ending with refuge as a way of taking responsible, courageous risks in the Dharma rather than hedging every bet.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What refuge means 03:23 Ordinary refuges fail 09:36 Fear and impermanence 20:24 Awakening and practice 24:12 The whole path matters 30:54 Right view as refuge 38:34 Freedom is possible 44:17 Sangha and admirable friends 48:34 Courage to take risks
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10834/

item.title: Maya and Nirvana (Beyond the Measure of Mind)
item.description:
What if suffering begins not with what happens, but with the mind’s habit of measuring, dividing, and believing in what it has made? The talk moves from the Buddha’s magic-show simile for maya into dependent origination, showing how contact, feeling, perception, conceiving, and clinging feed one another until the world of self and things feels solid. From there it turns to practice: the three characteristics, no-preference, and other ways of seeing that quiet the measuring mind until the built-up world begins to fade and the unmade can be glimpsed.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Framing the inquiry 03:25 Maya and the magic show 07:15 Papancha and fabrication 12:16 Signs and measurement 23:42 Dependent origination 28:59 Three characteristics 33:12 No preferences, no duality 43:33 The subtler prison 56:55 Beyond measure
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10832/

item.title: Living in the Truth
item.description:
What if the question is not only how to end suffering, but how to live in a way that can actually see clearly? The talk moves from personal patterns and the inner critic, through the Buddha’s three characteristics, into questions of perception, clinging, and the different ways practice can point toward something more ultimate. Along the way it weighs intuition against analysis, and keeps returning to whether truth is something to defend, investigate, or quietly let transform the heart.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Truth and freedom 02:19 What truth means 06:09 Mindfulness reveals patterns 11:58 Personal truth 22:52 A quieter story 25:13 Universal truths 32:49 Views and perception 45:01 Refining view 59:12 Living unburdened
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10831/

item.title: Sense Desire
item.description:
Sense desire is taken up as a difficult, ordinary, and often self-judging part of practice: the talk keeps asking whether this area is really a problem, or whether it has simply been left unexplored. Instead of giving a moral rule, the speaker moves through images, stories, and questions about food, sex, comfort, and renunciation to show how craving can narrow the heart and how another kind of happiness may be available. The talk keeps returning to one practical test: what are the effects, and what do they lead toward—hunger, or freedom and love?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 04:10 Danger and renunciation 06:41 Where the loop lives 12:08 Inner resource first 18:27 Joy and connection 24:06 Exploring pleasure directly 31:54 Mind shapes experience 41:24 Love, contentment, restraint 49:53 Beyond the senses
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10833/

item.title: Working with Thought in Meditation
item.description:
How do you work with thought without turning meditation into a war against thinking? The talk traces a careful middle way: first refusing the idea that practice is about blanking the mind, then sorting thought into what feeds suffering and what can be used for clarity, kindness, and inquiry. From there it moves into obsessive loops, papanca, and practical ways to interrupt them through the body, the breath, calm, and a more spacious way of seeing thought as passing and insubstantial. The result is less a recipe for silence than a patient re-education of attention and relationship.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Reframing thought 05:53 Three aims with thought 16:39 Working with harmful loops 20:14 What papanca does 30:58 Finding the hook 37:39 Emotion and well-being 50:41 Thought as process
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10835/

item.title: Approaches to the Hindrances
item.description:
How do you work with the hindrances without making meditation feel like a grim battle? The talk moves from a clear map of the five hindrances into a practical, almost workshop-like exploration of how they show up, how to tell them apart, and how different responses—softening, inquiry, firmness, relaxation, or more energy—can change the sitting. It also keeps returning to a bigger question: whether meditation is best understood as a craft, a form of doing, or a kind of non-doing, and what subtle habits are hidden inside each view. In the later discussion, the talk gets very specific about jealousy, self-worth, imagery, and how to make practice more personal and alive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the subject 03:38 Meditation as art 07:13 Questioning practice views 14:20 Mindfulness and release 20:56 Sloth and torpor 27:02 Restlessness and craving 36:54 Aversion and doubt 45:15 Personalizing practice 54:32 Not taking the ride
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10836/

item.title: The Unshakeable Heart (Part 2)
item.description:
How does equanimity grow when suffering is already here? The talk keeps returning to the way the mind gets pulled into pain, then shows how compassion, loving-kindness, and a softer relationship to unpleasant feeling can create a wider inner space. From there it turns to karma, praise and blame, and the self-sense itself, arguing that less aversion and less identification mean less is being built in experience, until the talk closes with the image of the house-builder being seen and ended.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Warmth Before Equanimity 01:54 Mind Sucked Into Suffering 04:04 Not Underestimating Practice 06:58 Karma Without Simplification 10:52 Generosity Changes the World 15:57 Praise, Blame, and Identity 18:56 Seeing Through Self 25:06 Letting Dualities Unwind 28:29 House-Building Ends
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10644/

item.title: The Unshakeable Heart (Part 1)
item.description:
How do impermanence and practice together loosen the grip of praise, blame, gain, loss, success, and failure? The talk moves from the eight worldly conditions to a more intimate question: what makes the heart less dependent on changing circumstances, and what kind of inner happiness can support that shift. It then widens the frame through cultivating wholesome qualities, working with reactivity, and asking what remains when experience begins to fade and simplify.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the frame 01:04 The eight worldly conditions 02:59 Practicing impermanence 07:21 Why insight is not enough 11:01 Qualities that support joy 14:45 Collectedness and conviction 24:33 The heart shapes perception 33:46 Working with reactivity 48:16 Simplifying and letting go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10643/

item.title: Second Day Instructions - Vedana
item.description:
How do you stay with experience before it turns into a story? The talk introduces vedana, the felt tone of pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral, and places it within the Four Foundations of Mindfulness as a simple but easily overlooked practice. From there it shows how craving, aversion, and boredom tend to build on top of that basic tone, and how noticing the reaction can let suffering soften. The emphasis is less on fixing experience than on learning to rest with it, see its change, and relax the push and pull around it.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Two Practice Directions 00:54 Mindfulness Foundations 02:19 What Vedana Means 06:26 Why It Is Overlooked 10:30 Practicing Sensitivity 12:41 Feeling the Reaction 15:41 Relaxing the Push-Pull 19:27 Two Retreat Practices
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10642/

item.title: The Development of Equanimity: Introduction and First Instructions
item.description:
How do you stay steady when life keeps shifting between praise and blame, gain and loss, pleasure and pain? The talk opens by rooting equanimity in the Buddha’s teachings, then carefully distinguishes it from indifference, showing it as something alive, warm, and spacious rather than shut down or cold. After setting the retreat in silence and trust, the speaker introduces a simple practice for meeting changing experience: stay with bodily sensation, notice impermanence, and repeat a phrase like “May I be at peace with the flow of experience.”
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 02:26 What equanimity means 04:53 Eight worldly conditions 09:14 The stone slab image 11:58 Not indifference 14:23 A spectrum of practice 21:01 Path, faith, and priority 30:54 Silence, trust, and practice 41:18 Guided meditation on change
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10641/

item.title: On the Essence of Being
item.description:
What happens when the mind keeps looking for an essence in self, in awareness, or even in being itself? The talk moves from the Buddha’s historical break with essence-seeking, through modern habits of reifying meditative experience, into a careful examination of why perceptions, moods, and views cannot be taken as a final self or ground. By the end, the emphasis has shifted from metaphysical answers to the practical question of what loosens suffering and what actually leads to freedom. The result is quiet but exacting: a sustained challenge to the urge to turn experience into something solid.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Question 03:23 Upanishadic Background 09:13 The Buddha's Shift 15:00 Meditation and Perception 24:32 Seeing Conditions 30:54 Razor's Edge of Views 43:12 Ground and Awareness 48:00 Being and Self 54:48 Freedom Beyond Conception
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10640/

item.title: Working with Anger and Aversion
item.description:
When anger flares, what is it really doing to perception, relationship, and the body? The talk starts by distinguishing the wish to hurt from the larger, more complex terrain of anger and aversion, then moves through consequences, self-fuelling loops, and the ways blame narrows the world. From there it offers practical ways to work: reflective questioning, empathy, a wider view of impermanence and mortality, and a mindfulness that returns from the story to the felt experience in the body.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the topic 02:48 Harm and restraint 06:49 Anger's many forms 13:23 Three kilesas 16:11 Consequences and stories 24:35 Empathy and death 29:08 Reflection and courage 36:04 Body, story, and letting go 44:17 How aversion builds reality
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/10645/

item.title: Talk Five: Samatha, Nibbana, and the Emptiness of Perception - The Relationship Between Concentration and Insight
item.description:
Can concentration be more than a calm prelude to insight? The talk follows that question into the way samatha quiets story, softens the sense of self, and changes the very way experience is perceived. From the inner critic to the jhanas, it keeps returning to one thread: the mind’s habit of building suffering, and the possibility of seeing that building process clearly enough to let it loosen.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Critic 02:50 Samatha and Insight 10:59 The Mind Builds 19:45 Jhana as Perception 25:12 Release and Relief 33:15 Perception Depends 40:36 Fabricating Experience 45:13 Nibbana and the Unfabricated 58:04 Rumi and Weaning
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11513/
item.itunes:author: Rob Burbea
item.itunes:summary:
Can concentration be more than a calm prelude to insight? The talk follows that question into the way samatha quiets story, softens the sense of self, and changes the very way experience is perceived. From the inner critic to the jhanas, it keeps returning to one thread: the mind’s habit of building suffering, and the possibility of seeing that building process clearly enough to let it loosen.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Critic 02:50 Samatha and Insight 10:59 The Mind Builds 19:45 Jhana as Perception 25:12 Release and Relief 33:15 Perception Depends 40:36 Fabricating Experience 45:13 Nibbana and the Unfabricated 58:04 Rumi and Weaning
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11513/

item.title: Fourth Morning Instructions
item.description:
How do concentration, perception, and comfort change when the talk stops treating the breath as a fixed object and starts treating experience as something we can gently work with? The talk reviews three practical levers in meditation: the quality of the breath, the way the breath is conceived in the body, and the amount of effort or receptivity in attention. From there it moves into how labeling can alter the felt experience of pain, restlessness, and tiredness, and ends by returning to steadiness as the quiet discipline of not feeding restlessness and letting samadhi deepen. Briefly, an anecdote about Ajahn Chah underscores the patience of this kind of training.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Review the practice 05:33 Breath and attention 09:06 Perception and labels 11:28 Working with discomfort 13:44 Seeing differently 15:24 Steadiness feeds samadhi
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11517/

item.title: Third Morning Instructions
item.description:
The talk starts with the fact that practice comes in waves: high, low, and in-between, and the speaker keeps returning to how attitude changes everything. From there it moves through patience, appreciation, and contentment, then into the practical work of meeting thoughts without feeding them, recognizing boredom and antsiness, and adjusting the balance of effort so attention stays light rather than forced. The last part turns toward a subtler breath practice: melting awareness into the whole body, sensing breath energy as a surrounding field, and letting the experience open, soften, and become more porous.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Expect the waves 02:50 Appreciation and patience 04:19 Working with thoughts 07:10 Restlessness and boredom 11:18 Lightness of attention 15:25 Breath energy field
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11518/

item.title: Talk Four: Jhanas One to Four
item.description:
What does it actually feel like when concentration deepens from a comfortable breath to full-body rapture, then to happiness, peaceful contentment, and finally still, bright awareness? The talk walks step by step through the four jhanas, using the Buddha’s images of bath powder, a mountain lake, lotus-filled water, and a white cloth to show the differences in each state and to stress that steadiness and whole-body suffusion matter more than dramatic intensity. It also pauses over practical questions: whether this is escapism, how precision can be embodied rather than intellectual, and how intense bliss can be worked with until it mellows.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the map 01:21 Listening and attitude 05:23 Pleasure gathers 09:37 First and second jhana 13:50 Contentment and peace 17:24 Fourth jhana 23:07 Signs and mastery 27:16 Escapes, not evasion 36:43 Prayer of practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11515/

item.title: Talk Three: Wise Effort and Wise Attachment
item.description:
How do we work with effort without turning practice into self-measurement? The talk moves from the uneasy Western habit of avoiding goals to a clearer account of wise effort: directing energy toward wholesome qualities, sustaining what helps, and learning how to loosen what does not. It then turns to wise attachment, the inner critic, and the practical balance of concentration practice, using images like the raft, the bird, and the potter’s hands to show how supports can be used without being clung to. The final movement is toward a more subtle, responsive effort—lighter, kinder, and less driven by should.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 06:49 Striving and aspiration 11:34 Craving as leverage 18:37 Wise attachment 24:58 Working with the inner critic 38:31 Learning the bigger picture 45:13 Conditions for success 50:24 Balancing the effort
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11516/

item.title: Talk Two: Understanding the Heart
item.description:
The talk begins with the question of why we understand so many things, yet not the one that matters most: the mind-heart, or citta. From there, it lays out a broad map of practice—mindfulness, cultivation, and insight—before narrowing to samatha as a way of building steadiness, well-being, and a more reliable basis for letting go. The speaker keeps returning to concrete questions: how generosity, ethics, sense restraint, and careful work with the breath shape the heart, and how a gentler, more spacious attention can meet hindrances, pain, and emotion without getting pulled around by them. The talk closes by contrasting fleeting happiness with the quieter, steadier joy that can deepen over time, and by asking for patience with a gradual path.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Understanding the heart 02:44 Three strands of practice 05:42 Happiness as foundation 08:28 Generosity and ethics 15:11 Guarding the senses 19:02 Progress and the path 25:04 Working with hindrances 30:26 Not taking them personally 40:01 Sensitivity and emotion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11514/

item.title: Talk One: Introduction to the Art of Concentration (Samatha Meditation)
item.description:
What if concentration is not a narrow technique, but a way of letting the mind and body settle into a more pleasant, workable home? The talk opens by framing samatha as a retreat into a quieter stream of practice, then moves into the real task: playing with the breath, the body, attention, and perception so that calm can grow into joy. Along the way, the speaker keeps widening the frame with stories of the Buddha, emphasis on generosity, silence, and appreciation, and a steady warning against making practice tight, self-preoccupied, or merely dutiful.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the retreat 03:05 Why samatha matters 07:11 The art of practice 10:41 Play and patience 15:01 Breath and body energy 21:39 Pleasant abiding 23:44 Practice for all beings 29:33 Joy, gratitude, and enough 36:52 Retreat discipline and silence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11511/

item.title: Nirvana and Transcendence
item.description:
What if the Buddha’s teaching points not only to the end of suffering, but to something beyond the six senses, beyond space and time, and beyond the ways we usually make a life feel complete? The talk moves from early Pali passages to Zen and Tibetan references, then turns to the listener’s own reactions: boredom, fear, relief, or a quiet sense that something deeper is being named. From there it asks how mindfulness, intimacy with life, and the inner critic fit alongside the harder question of fabrication, unmaking, and the deathless.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:58 The deathless question 04:32 Sutta passages 09:39 Huang Po and the void 11:18 Reactions and assumptions 21:21 Mindfulness and life 29:33 Life or transcendence 36:41 Awareness and language 47:50 Fabrication and unmaking 58:48 Beyond existence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11974/

item.title: Awakening
item.description:
What if awakening is not a vague spiritual ideal, but something specific, rare, and worth orienting a life around? The talk moves from the Buddha’s wide-ranging advice to laypeople, through renunciation, doubt, and common ideas about enlightened people, toward a more exact account of stream entry: seeing through self-view, grasping, and the fabrications of experience. It keeps returning to the tension between respecting many valid reasons to practice and still asking whether one is interested in the deathless, unbinding, and the end of suffering.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Shifting Dharma Emphases 03:33 Lay Life and Radical Aim 08:35 Why Awakening Matters 11:57 What Is on Offer 15:47 Views, Images, and Assumptions 23:01 Stream Entry Explained 30:55 Self-View and the Aggregates 40:07 Deathless and Emptiness 56:45 Practice, Depth, and Possibility
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11862/

item.title: Love/The Difficulties of Love
item.description:
What happens when the need to love and the need to feel loved start shaping almost everything we do? The talk moves from romantic love and cultural hype, through self-criticism, shame, rejection, and fear of being trapped, toward the quieter work of seeing how feelings are conditioned and how love is more than a passing emotion. It also turns toward metta practice as a way to soften the inner critic, open the heart, and discover that giving love can change what is actually perceived.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Questions About Love 05:28 Romance and Cultural Pressure 10:42 Self-Love and the Inner Critic 18:36 Worthiness and Hidden Shame 26:44 Feelings, Intention, and Love 35:31 Falling In Love 47:13 Relationship as Skill 54:12 Metta, Perception, and Spaciousness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11860/

item.title: Death
item.description:
Death is treated here as something to look at directly, not a topic to avoid. The talk moves from the shock of mortality into practical ways of using its contemplation: to wake up urgency, clarify what matters, and loosen grasping at comfort, pleasure, and security. Along the way, the speaker folds in stories, poems, and Buddhist teachings to show how death practice can lead not only to sobriety, but sometimes to joy, wonder, and a stronger wish to give.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why Contemplate Death 04:12 Not Depressing, But Liberating 09:00 Preciousness And Impermanence 17:02 Urgency And What Matters 25:59 Choices, Ethics, And Insight 32:17 Refuge In The Deathless 39:14 Body, Earth, And Recycling 48:14 Impermanence In Practice 53:02 Experience, Emptiness, And Freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11859/

item.title: Opening Talk for Work Retreat - Working and Awakening
item.description:
What happens when work, meditation, and ordinary life are not treated as separate worlds, but as one field of practice? The talk opens by questioning the usual split between retreat life and everyday activity, then moves into a practical emphasis on balancing service, formal practice, awareness, intention, and view. From there, it turns repeatedly to small but decisive questions—what is being put first right now, how is this difficulty being seen, and how can the heart be cared for through silence, gratitude, and the precepts?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What a retreat is 03:02 Work and meditation 07:54 Service as practice 12:16 Awareness and intention 17:13 Renewing the aim 20:40 View and difficulty 25:14 Caring for the heart 29:43 Silence and simplicity 34:08 Precepts and trust
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11861/

item.title: The Birth of a Bodhisattva
item.description:
What lets compassion stay open without turning into strain, attachment, or collapse? The talk begins with the bodhisattva ideal and Shantideva’s expansive vows, then keeps returning to a practical question: how compassion can be steady, balanced, and sustainable in the face of suffering that does not quickly change. From there it moves through equanimity, the hidden wish to be needed, the limits of action, and stories of radical service before turning to emptiness as what softens the view and makes compassion less brittle.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Bodhisattva Meaning 02:56 Shantideva's Vows 05:55 What Sustains Compassion 10:29 Balance in Practice 17:00 Self, Doing, and Space 25:17 Holding Suffering 33:59 Equanimity and Karma 46:41 Faith and Devotion 59:14 Emptiness and Compassion
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11954/

item.title: Love and the Emptiness of Things
item.description:
How does love change when it stops being aimed only at a person and starts meeting experience itself? The talk moves from basic loving-kindness and compassion, through the idea of wholesome nourishment and investigation, into a subtler account of emptiness where what we call objects, selves, and even suffering are seen as dependent on relationship and perception. Along the way, the speaker keeps returning to practice questions: what happens when we welcome pain instead of struggle with it, and how do love and non-grasping begin to make things dissolve, soften, or lose their grip?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Reviewing the path 03:31 Nourishment and insight 08:31 From practice to perception 16:25 Love beyond beings 27:25 Technique and freedom 40:08 Perception through relationship 54:39 Love and emptiness 01:08:03 Not stopping at states 01:18:49 Questions and practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11953/

item.title: Compassion Part Two (Compassion for Ourselves)
item.description:
How do you stay with your own pain without turning it into self-absorption or comparison? The talk takes up compassion for oneself as a practice that is intimate, healing, and also quietly insightful: instead of pushing away suffering or judging it against the suffering of others, the speaker shows how tenderness can meet pain while leaving space around it. From there the teaching moves into practical questions about balance, whether compassion feeds ego, and how a softer attention can reveal subtler forms of clinging and selfing. It ends by suggesting that compassion itself can become a way into non-self, and by urging listeners not to scatter their energy across too many methods.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening and transition 01:29 Why self-compassion feels difficult 03:10 Learning from our own pain 05:06 Balancing empathy and giving 07:08 How self-compassion works 08:43 Ego, phrases, and delicacy 11:26 Compassion as insight lens 15:25 Compassion and non-self 17:37 Choose one well
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11949/

item.title: Compassion
item.description:
What does compassion actually ask of the heart: to open to suffering, or to close down out of fear? The talk moves from defining compassion as a natural response to suffering, through its many qualities—empathy, giving, steadiness, wisdom, listening, joy—to the subtle ways it gets distorted by fear, anger, pity, and grief. From there it turns practical, showing how compassion can be cultivated through inquiry, the company we keep, service, self-compassion, and a wider sense of shared vulnerability and non-separateness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What compassion is 02:35 Many strands of compassion 06:25 Ambivalence and longing 15:45 Questions and practices 21:22 Conditions that nurture it 27:51 Four near enemies 44:04 Empathy and giving 53:49 Shared vulnerability 01:04:55 Emptiness and interpenetration
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11950/

item.title: Using Insight To Deepen Love and Compassion
item.description:
How can love deepen when the mind looks more closely at clinging, fear, impermanence, and self? The talk moves from the basics of metta practice into a series of practical experiments: softening fear, noticing how clinging closes the heart, and using reflections on death, change, and not-self only when they actually open compassion. Later, the speaker turns toward emptiness, questioning the idea of a fixed self and the assumption that things exist independently of how they are perceived, while keeping the emphasis on what is helpful in practice rather than on abstract theory.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Cultivating the heart 04:50 Insight as release 08:28 Fear and its grip 12:29 Working with fear 30:35 Impermanence in metta 43:13 Dukkha and selfing 58:33 Not-self and practice 01:35:29 Ways of seeing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11956/

Later, the speaker turns toward emptiness, questioning the idea of a fixed self and the assumption that things exist independently of how they are perceived, while keeping the emphasis on what is helpful in practice rather than on abstract theory.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Cultivating the heart 04:50 Insight as release 08:28 Fear and its grip 12:29 Working with fear 30:35 Impermanence in metta 43:13 Dukkha and selfing 58:33 Not-self and practice 01:35:29 Ways of seeing
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11956/

item.title: Exploring the World of Loving Kindness
item.description:
Can loving-kindness stay true when it meets need, hurt, anger, and the wish to be seen? The talk moves from the feel of metta in the body, through the near-enemy of attached love, into friendship, self-clinging, and the hard work of speaking honestly without harm. It then turns to anger, silence, and a gentler way of being in which love may feel less like something we make and more like something we learn to receive.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Metta Is 02:25 Compassion and Boundaries 05:05 Self-Love as Foundation 08:52 Friend, Benefactor, Near-Enemy 12:11 Friendship and Reparenting 17:12 Self, Clinging, and Identity 22:39 Gibran on Friendship 26:08 Anger and Skillful Speech 38:58 Conflict Without Harm
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11951/

item.title: The Place of Samadhi in Metta Practice
item.description:
What if samadhi in metta practice is not narrow concentration, but a steady, receptive way of dwelling that lets the heart settle, soften, and open? The talk starts by widening samadhi beyond the usual idea of “keeping the mind on one object,” then turns to the practical question of how concentration, kindness, and happiness support one another over a retreat. From there it moves through common objections—tightness, striving, attachment, suppression—and keeps returning to a quieter view: notice the conditions shaping experience, work gently, and let the practice become less fabricated and more livable.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Orientation 03:00 Metta and Concentration 12:02 Why Samadhi Matters 21:40 Steadiness and Well-Being 32:20 Whole-Body Softness 41:19 Dwelling Places of the Heart 53:12 Pleasure and Fear 01:03:02 Emptiness in Experience 01:15:01 Questions and Practice
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11955/

item.title: Joy
item.description:
What if joy is not a bonus to practice, but one of its foundations? The talk begins by questioning the assumption that Dharma is mainly about grim endurance, then opens into a wide-ranging exploration of worldly and non-worldly joy, from beauty and kindness to ethics, generosity, and meditative attention. It moves steadily from argument to practical experiment: how joy can be cultivated, how it nourishes and clarifies the mind, and how it can even mature into equanimity and a quieter freedom. Along the way, the speaker uses stories, poems, and everyday examples to show that joy is trainable, not accidental.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Joy on the path 02:31 Kinds of joy 06:07 Joy as nourishment 10:16 Training the mind 16:50 Relating differently 24:56 Cultivating joy 32:19 Ethics and appreciation 40:51 What feeds joy 49:55 Attention and freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11971/

item.title: What is Insight?
item.description:
Insight here is not treated as a single flash, but as a way of seeing that reduces suffering. The talk moves from a broad map of insight, through personal and universal forms of understanding, into deliberate practices with impermanence, dukkha, and not-self, and finally toward the very deep end of the path where clinging itself fades. Along the way, the speaker keeps asking a practical question: does what we see actually change how we live, relate, and let go?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the frame 03:14 What insight means 10:02 Personal and story 14:07 Universal truths 20:12 Insighт deepens 26:46 Using insight as a lens 35:11 Three characteristics 43:47 Fading and release 52:24 Dependent arising
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11972/

item.title: Time and the Emptiness of Time
item.description:
What if the time we feel ourselves living inside is not as solid as it seems? The talk begins with everyday struggles around time—wanting things over, fearing what’s ahead, measuring ourselves across past, present, and future—and then carefully turns that whole assumption inside out. It moves from conventional clock time and Newton’s picture of absolute time, through Einstein, into meditation, where the speaker explores how craving, selfing, and perception help fabricate the sense of time and how that sense can dissolve into timelessness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Ordinary time 04:33 Questioning the obvious 08:25 Newton and Einstein 14:35 Time and seeking 20:26 Now is not enough 28:24 Reasoning in meditation 36:40 Self, things, and time 48:10 Timeless unbinding 54:06 Living from insight
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11929/

item.title: Into the Depths of Silence
item.description:
How can silence be more than a retreat rule, and what opens when we actually listen to it? The talk moves from the strangeness of a silent retreat into silence as a living practice: it reveals priorities, brings remorse and judgment into view, softens the push and pull around experience, and can even change the way sounds, thoughts, and the self are felt. Along the way, the speaker uses Rumi, Thomas Merton, Pablo Neruda, Wordsworth, and Dōgen to point toward a silence that is not empty deprivation but spacious, receptive, and quietly loving.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Silence Is For 02:52 Responses to Quiet 06:03 The Ocean and the Puddle 09:46 Listening and Priority 24:05 Neruda on Quiet 26:21 Sound, Thought, and Space 40:14 Beyond Objects and Self 52:43 The Immeasurable
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11928/

item.title: A Question of Faith
item.description:
What do you actually put your faith in when life feels unstable, and what happens when the things you rely on start to break? The talk begins by probing faith as something hidden in ordinary choices, then moves through suffering, doubt, and the ways practice can either narrow into fear or open into confidence. It draws on stories of a prisoner choosing compassion, a Cambodian nun’s vow, and the Buddha’s encounter with aging, sickness, and death to show faith becoming less about belief and more about the lived possibility of transformation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the question 04:18 The human predicament 11:06 What we trust 17:14 Faith in care 22:50 Cultivating the beautiful 29:21 When forms break 40:04 Path of questions 45:54 What is possible 50:25 Experience-based confidence
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11927/

item.title: "A Body At Peace With Itself"
item.description:
How do you stop fighting the body and begin to meet it as a source of practice rather than a problem to solve? The talk moves from direct bodily awareness, through pain, comfort, and loving-kindness, into a subtle argument that perception itself can be trained: sensations are not just endured, but seen, softened, and sometimes re-seen altogether. It then widens into impermanence, not-self, and the body’s fragility, using images of foam, mirage, and giving the body back to nature to loosen identification without turning cold or dismissive. Quietly and steadily, the talk keeps returning to the same practical question: can this body be met with less pressure and more freedom?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 05:40 Body at peace 06:53 See body as body 11:22 Bare attention 16:07 Working with pain 22:16 Pleasure and metta 28:54 Body as deep refuge 40:07 Letting body go 49:19 Foam and deathless
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/11926/

item.title: The Self and the World
item.description:
What if the question is not whether the self exists, but where suffering is being built? The talk moves from the usefulness of self-story for healing, through the practice of not identifying with body and mind, into a subtler inquiry: how self and world are co-created by concepts, moods, and the push-pull of experience. Along the way, it uses examples of childhood harm, inner-child healing, and everyday self-images to show how loosening identification can bring lightness, not blankness. It ends by pointing toward dependent origination and nibbana as unbinding, with less self, less world, and more freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Setting the question 02:12 When self-story helps 07:57 Choosing the right lens 10:09 Mindfulness loosens identity 19:05 Not me, not mine 25:55 Self and world co-arise 30:35 Helpful concepts, harmful concepts 41:47 Fabrication and empty experience 48:47 Dependent origination and unbinding
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12223/

item.title: Ocean of Patience
item.description:
How do we stay with practice when the mind wants a result, a breakthrough, or an escape? The talk moves from the grit of retreat life—aches, boredom, hindrances, waiting in line, waiting for the bell—into a steadier inquiry into what patience actually is: perseverance, calm endurance, and the willingness to meet difficulty without adding more suffering. It then widens into daily life, showing how impatience is fed by time-thinking, self-judgment, and the belief that we need to get somewhere else before things can be okay.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Reflections 02:53 Practice Needs Patience 04:53 Joyful Acceptance 09:00 Long View of Effort 13:12 Gradual Transformation 18:43 Urgency and Wisdom 23:22 Investigating Impatience 30:55 Present Moment and Allowing 43:27 Time, Solidity, and Release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12222/

item.title: Freedom From Fear And Anxiety
item.description:
Fear is treated here as something ordinary, layered, and workable: body sensations, thought, aversion, and the shrinking of mental space. The talk moves from a brief Buddha story of fear in the forest to practical ways of meeting anxiety through mindfulness, kindness, and small acts of challenging its authority. It also explores how fear can hide inside desire, self-judgment, approval-seeking, and even the wish for safety, and how cultivating wholesome qualities can slowly widen the ground we stand on.
Location: Insight Meditation Society - Retreat Center
Chapters: 00:00 Fear is universal 03:23 Many forms of fear 11:37 Three approaches 15:58 What fear feels like 24:12 Kindness and metta 33:18 Opening the space 39:55 Questioning assumptions 49:28 Cultivating the future
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/868/

item.title: Jhana Practice
item.description:
What happens when meditation stops being about pushing through difficulty and becomes a careful deepening of ease, pleasure, and letting go? The talk maps the jhanas as a sequence of increasingly refined states, from bodily pīti to profound stillness, infinite space, and the collapse of ordinary self-sense. Along the way, it keeps returning to a practical question: what are the fruits of these states, and how do we avoid turning them into another project of attainment?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Practice in broad view 02:40 Why jhana matters 04:39 What jhana means 08:11 First two absorptions 12:31 From contentment to space 17:13 Fruits of the states 24:52 What counts as jhana 30:20 Confidence and insight 39:40 Pitfalls, questions, and conditions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12311/

item.title: Developing Samatha/Concentration
item.description:
What if concentration is less about narrowing down and more about a calm, collected depth that can hold the whole life of practice? The talk moves from working with hindrances and softening tight effort to a broader case for samatha as a full path, not just a preliminary technique. Along the way, it keeps returning to the practical supports that let calm deepen: appreciation, body sensitivity, ease, and the willingness to stop making practice into a struggle. In the later part, the focus shifts toward pīti, fear around pleasure, and the surprising way deepening calm can reveal that emotions and problems are more condition-made and less fixed than they seem.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Samatha Means 05:25 A Lifelong Practice 10:18 Why It Matters 15:13 Insight Needs Support 20:45 Happiness as Basis 26:16 Working with Hindrances 32:56 Softness, Not Strain 39:16 Goals and Attachment 46:45 Pīti and Fear
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12309/

item.title: Working with the Hindrances
item.description:
The talk starts with a simple retreat-day reality: practice often meets mixed conditions, not uninterrupted calm, and that is exactly where the work begins. From there, the speaker moves through the five hindrances one by one, treating them not as failures but as familiar human energies that can be recognized, softened, and worked with through posture, breath, attention, and honest questioning. The emphasis stays practical: cultivating steadiness by learning when to stay with difficulty, when to widen awareness, and when to choose a more nourishing object. Along the way, images like the hungry ghost, the burning coal, and trading candy for gold make the path feel concrete rather than abstract.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Retreat and difficulty 02:00 The five hindrances 05:58 Active practice 10:20 Craving and hunger 15:13 Pleasure and nourishment 22:52 Aversion and anger 27:40 Boredom and energy 40:22 Sloth and torpor 46:25 Worry, doubt, and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12315/

item.title: Samatha (Concentration Practice) - Introductory Talk
item.description:
What if calmness is not a side benefit of practice but a skill the heart can deliberately learn, deepen, and live from? The talk opens by placing samatha inside an ancient human current of retreat, then argues that calm, insight, and the heart practices are complementary paths rather than rivals. From there it turns practical: how intentions slip, why silence and simplicity matter, how breath practice can be approached, and why ethics and care for others are the ground that lets calm actually take root.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the Retreat 02:18 Samatha in Human Life 05:24 Why Calmness Matters 08:13 Three Practice Paths 09:21 Inner Resource and Method 16:17 Breath, Control, and Letting Go 19:52 Intentions on Retreat 25:11 Gratitude, Silence, Simplicity 31:26 How to Support Calm
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12312/

item.title: God and the Ultimate Truth
item.description:
What changes when God is treated less as a belief to defend and more as a question of what actually shapes the heart? The talk moves through devotion, doubt, fear, and modern non-belief, testing them against their fruits: compassion, freedom, and the hidden assumptions that sit behind our views. Along the way it brings in Krishnamurti, Mother Teresa, Buddhist bodhisattva imagery, Christian mystics, and the language of emptiness to ask whether the deepest orientation in practice is toward a person, a principle, or the release of grasping itself.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 03:38 Devotion and belief 08:40 Images of God 12:57 What lies beneath belief 17:24 Bodhisattvas and practice 21:36 Surrender and discernment 26:45 The heart as divine 31:14 Mystical perception 36:22 Silence and letting go
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12342/

item.title: Passion, Desire, and the Path
item.description:
Can desire actually belong on the path, instead of being treated only as something to eliminate? The talk moves from examples of fierce renunciation and quiet devotion, through questions of death, practice energy, and the four factors of balanced effort, toward a more searching look at desire in sexuality, romance, fatigue, and retreat life. It ends by contrasting the work of cultivating well-being with the subtler practice of letting go, asking what happens when aspiration, effort, and even the idea of a fixed goal are seen more deeply.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Passion on the path 02:12 Renunciation with fire 06:36 Urgency and possibility 09:59 Quiet passion sustained 13:32 Goals, aspiration, and effort 20:10 Where desire moves 25:47 Sex, romance, and retreat 34:10 What feeds experience 43:14 Energy, tiredness, and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12343/

item.title: Home and Homelessness
item.description:
Security is treated here as a question the talk keeps turning over: where do we try to find home, and what do we actually protect ourselves from? The speaker moves from the early Buddhist image of leaving household life, through money, possessions, and roles, to the more difficult possibility that real home may lie in practice, ethics, refuge, and the heart’s ability to stay with uncertainty. By the end, the talk draws together a paradox: the deeper the inquiry into homelessness, the more it points toward a freedom that does not depend on fixed ground.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 01:28 Leaving household life 05:04 Lay life and its risks 11:20 Money and self-protection 18:01 Rooted in the Dharma 23:53 Seeing groundlessness 29:41 Changing experience, changing homes 38:38 Inner resources and refuge 44:38 Security, emptiness, and freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12344/

item.title: Equanimity in Compassion
item.description:
How can compassion stay tender without tipping into overwhelm, and what lets the heart remain steady with suffering that cannot be fixed? The talk moves from clarifying mudita as a deeper, non-egoic joy into equanimity as balance, spaciousness, and sustainability within compassion. It then widens to the limits of care in relationships, the web of conditions behind any situation, and the role of samadhi, emptiness, and the eight worldly conditions in making that steadiness real in life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 The Brahma Viharas 03:21 What Equanimity Means 06:09 Compassion and Balance 11:45 Samadhi and Spaciousness 19:48 Needs and Conditions 29:33 Karma and Non-Separation 42:18 All Conditions 52:16 Subtle Letting Go 01:01:41 Questions and Integration
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12289/

item.title: Love and Emptiness
item.description:
What changes when love stops being aimed only at people and begins to meet experience itself? The talk starts by reviewing mettā and compassion as ways of reconditioning the mind, then moves into a more subtle question: whether love can open perception toward emptiness rather than merely improving the self. A teaching from the Aksayamati Sutra, a Lotus Sutra line about seeing all things with compassion, and extended discussion of grasping, fear, and dependent origination gradually push the practice from heartfelt nourishment toward non-clinging and freedom.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Review 03:27 Nourishment and Insight 07:19 Conditions and the Unconditioned 11:48 Seeing Things with Compassion 17:47 Acceptance and Perception 29:18 Ease and Relationship 40:32 Fear and Present Time 48:10 Grasping and Fading 01:01:11 Questions and Practice Guidance
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12293/

item.title: The Practice of Compassion
item.description:
Compassion here begins as the heart’s response to suffering, then keeps widening through empathy, wisdom, and action. The talk traces what helps it grow—metta, confidence, wise company, discernment, and a willingness to touch pain without collapsing into fear, anger, pity, or grief. It then turns toward practice questions, self-compassion, and the way emptiness can loosen the sense of a separate self so compassion becomes less burdensome and more natural.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What compassion is 02:19 Compassion's qualities 07:21 Why the heart resists 12:27 How it develops 18:31 What nurtures compassion 31:04 Near enemies 38:58 Balance and spaciousness 47:08 Self, emptiness, and oneness 58:32 Interpenetration and practice questions
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12295/

item.title: From Insight to Love
item.description:
Can insight actually soften the heart, rather than make practice colder? The talk starts from that question and moves through a careful argument that love and compassion are not separate from insight: when clinging relaxes, love can appear more naturally, and when fear takes over, the heart closes. From there, the speaker works through fear itself, then turns to practical ways the three characteristics—impermanence, dukkha, and not-self—can be brought into metta practice as living lenses rather than abstract doctrine. The talk ends by stressing that these are possibilities to try lightly, not standards to measure oneself against.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Insight and metta 03:59 What insight means 06:31 Baba Amte's example 10:47 Working with fear 24:53 Insight in practice 26:55 Three characteristics 36:17 Not-self and openness 48:45 Unhooking me and mine 57:42 Habits, grooves, and trust
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12291/

item.title: Expressions of Metta
item.description:
How does metta stay real when it meets self-judgment, attachment, anger, and the messiness of relationship? The talk starts by widening lovingkindness beyond a single feeling, then moves through self-metta, the benefactor and friend, and the way practice can include compassion, boundaries, and the difficult person without becoming rigid or sentimental. It turns toward the body, silence, remorse, and finally a quieter sense of oneness that can unsettle ordinary ideas of separation.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Metta Has Many Flavors 02:43 Self-Kindness and Boundlessness 07:52 Attachment and Friendship 13:24 Uniqueness in Context 18:52 Neutrality and Selfing 25:22 Anger with Love 36:12 Hearing Hurt Well 45:40 The Body and Everyday Life 55:05 Silence and Held Experience
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12290/

item.title: Samadhi in Metta Practice
item.description:
What if samadhi is less about narrowing the mind and more about letting the mind, body, and heart gather into one place? The talk unfolds by rethinking samadhi as collectedness, depth, and unification within metta practice, then moving into the practical questions that shape it: steadiness, happiness, gratitude, effort, and the way kindness softens tightness. It also explores why deepening can bring fear, how hindrances are recognized without self-blame, and why love and receptivity matter if practice is to bear fruit. Near the end, the talk turns toward emptiness through the simple insight that many emotional states only arise when the conditions are there.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 What samadhi means 03:07 Collectedness and depth 07:41 Why it matters 12:14 How it develops 17:21 Happiness as a support 21:02 Working with obstacles 29:51 Effort, softness, and enjoyment 39:06 Rapture and fear 53:26 Samadhi and emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12294/

item.title: Views In Practice (...They make all the difference)
item.description:
What if the decisive factor in practice is not the thing itself, but the way it is being seen? The talk moves from ordinary opinions and cultural assumptions to the Buddha’s right view, then shows how suffering is built in the present through grasping, self-story, and subtle identification. It keeps returning to a practical question: what view here is actually leading toward freedom, and what is only keeping experience in place?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question of view 02:34 Attached or unsteady views 07:10 Self-story and culture 11:26 Four Noble Truths 17:24 Happiness and cultivation 20:30 Long-term practice 28:26 Ways of looking 34:14 How suffering is built 43:26 Beyond views
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12308/

item.title: Equanimity
item.description:
Equanimity is introduced as steadiness of mind and heart in the face of all conditions, then tested against the eight worldly conditions: praise and blame, success and failure, gain and loss, pleasure and pain. The talk moves carefully away from the idea that equanimity means indifference, showing instead that it can be open, alive, and spacious rather than cold or dull. From there it turns practical: returning to the breath, contemplating impermanence and death, cultivating metta, compassion, and spiritual joy, and examining how push and pull create suffering. It closes by pointing toward a quieter freedom in which things are seen as less measurable than they first appear.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What Equanimity Means 02:01 The Penguin Story 03:34 The Eight Worldly Conditions 06:06 Open Heart, Not Indifference 09:09 Cultivation and Practice 15:49 Impermanence and Death 21:48 Happiness From Inner Qualities 31:09 Wonder and Spiritual Joy 39:55 Reactivity, Pain, and Spaciousness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12307/

item.title: The Nature of Awareness
item.description:
Is awareness a clean mirror, a vast space, or something even more elusive? The talk moves through several familiar ways of understanding mind and consciousness—clarity, impermanence, spaciousness, oneness—and keeps asking whether any of them is final, or whether they are still conditioned ways of seeing. From there it turns toward not-self and dependent origination, suggesting that even the sense of a witness or knowing mind is not a separate ground, but something empty, dependent, and ultimately unfindable.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 02:06 Practice over theory 04:02 The mirror metaphor 07:11 Clean but still seen 11:02 Spacious awareness 16:31 Vastness and oneness 24:10 Not to stop there 32:15 Undercutting the witness 40:00 Groundless and free
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12484/

item.title: Emotional Healing
item.description:
How do difficult emotions heal: by digging deeper into the pain, or by changing the way the mind meets it? The talk moves through mindfulness, kindness, and the role of cultivation, arguing that presence alone is often not enough and that a balanced practice also includes metta, rest, and the growth of wholesome qualities. Along the way, it questions common assumptions about therapy, self-focus, and the idea that the deepest truth is always the most painful one, ending in a careful look at emptiness and the release of views.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 02:23 Attention and presence 06:45 Healing as relationship 11:36 Kindness and accommodation 18:59 Seeing without self 20:57 Two wings of practice 24:51 Happiness and self-view 31:09 Therapy and psychologizing 43:02 Emptiness and release
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12481/

item.title: Samadhi
item.description:
What happens when samadhi is treated not as a tight, microscopic focus but as a gathered, unified depth of mind? The talk moves from a basic definition of collectedness into a wide view of practice: one-pointed attention, open awareness, vipassana, the hindrances, happiness, whole-body sensitivity, and the way calm can support insight without strain. It also keeps returning to a quieter question underneath the technique—how to work with effort, pleasure, fear, and emotion without turning practice into another form of grasping.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 What samadhi means 03:49 Right samadhi in context 12:03 A path to awakening 17:35 Happiness and appreciation 25:20 Tightness and balance 31:29 Whole-body practice 38:13 Effort, goals, and pīti 45:57 Fear and self-softening 54:40 Less doing, less self
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12483/

item.title: From Feelings to Freedom (Exploring Vedana)
item.description:
How can feeling tone be the doorway to freedom rather than another trap? The talk begins with bodily pain and the ordinary urge to endure, adjust, or recoil, then moves into a careful exploration of vedana as pleasant, unpleasant, or neutral across breath, sound, thought, and movement. From there it turns to the practical question of what feeds feeling tone and what lets it fade, ending in a deeper inquiry into dependent arising, non-self, and the groundlessness of experience.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Working with bodily pain 02:52 Turning toward difficulty 04:19 Relaxing the whole body 06:11 Microscope and space 09:21 What vedana means 15:08 Sensitivity to feeling tone 21:44 Seeing vedana as not self 28:43 Grasping and aversion 33:03 Conditions and groundlessness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12482/

item.title: Letting Go of Judgement
item.description:
Judgment is traced back to a basic habit of the mind: the way it turns experience into self-criticism, comparison, and pain. The talk moves from distinguishing judgment from discernment, to practical ways of challenging judgment through attention, appreciation, kindness, and seeing thoughts as passing events rather than identities. It closes by widening the frame further, toward conditions, the shared human roots of greed, aversion, and delusion, and finally a Zen pointer to letting go of preferences altogether.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 02:19 Practice and freedom 05:26 Judgment versus discernment 10:20 Working with judgment 14:53 Imagination and perspective 18:57 Metta and kindness 25:28 Views and self-making 31:20 Conditions over self 44:40 Beyond preferences
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21014/

item.title: Aspects of Mindfulness (Part 2)
item.description:
What is mindfulness for, if not just staying present? The talk begins with that question and keeps widening it: from suffering and the end of suffering, to truth, love, and the whole Eightfold Path that mindfulness belongs to. It also pushes back against familiar reductions of meditation as stress-release or self-improvement, and instead turns to practical qualities to check for when practice feels stuck: recognition, acceptance, investigation, and non-identification. By the end, mindfulness is presented as something that can deepen into sensitivity, receptivity, and a quieter kindness toward life.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why practice mindfulness 01:08 Freedom from suffering 05:39 Truth and love 09:39 Mindfulness within the path 13:20 What mindfulness is not 19:37 Presence and sensitivity 29:42 RAN: a practice check 36:44 Embodiment and heartfulness 42:46 Active inquiry
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21011/

item.title: Aspects of Mindfulness (Part 1)
item.description:
What is mindfulness, really: a way of being present, or a tool for seeing how the mind gets caught? The talk moves from a broad framing of the Buddhist path into a careful unpacking of attention, self-inquiry, and the places we most often suffer—body, feeling, thought, intention, and habitual reaction. It then becomes practical, contrasting bare attention with clear comprehension and emphasizing that meditation is less about eliminating thought than about repeatedly noticing drift and returning without judgment.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 03:43 Mindfulness and awareness 06:57 What to attend to 11:55 Presence, not emptiness 15:50 Holding one thing in mind 19:06 Bare attention and clarity 22:47 Practice and repetition 26:24 Patience with wandering
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21012/

It then becomes practical, contrasting bare attention with clear comprehension and emphasizing that meditation is less about eliminating thought than about repeatedly noticing drift and returning without judgment.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the theme 03:43 Mindfulness and awareness 06:57 What to attend to 11:55 Presence, not emptiness 15:50 Holding one thing in mind 19:06 Bare attention and clarity 22:47 Practice and repetition 26:24 Patience with wandering
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21012/

item.title: Beyond the Prison of the Self
item.description:
What if the tight, familiar sense of “me and mine” is not something to defend but something to examine? The talk moves from the ordinary wish for relaxation into a deeper inquiry: how self-sense gets built, how it fuels stress and comparison, and how practice can loosen its grip. It then turns practical, tracing paths of generosity, service, and a changed way of looking at experience so that life feels less like a prison and more open, lighter, and less burdened.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Why practice turns inward 03:40 Not-self, not nihilism 08:30 Cultivating the heart 13:26 Generosity as release 18:51 Service beyond self 24:47 Looking without possession 28:35 Self-views and conditions 32:46 Nothing to identify with 37:32 Breath, grasping, and freedom
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12445/

item.title: Renunciation and Joy
item.description:
Renunciation is usually treated as deprivation, but the talk keeps asking whether it might actually open the heart. Moving from the Buddha’s Eightfold Path to the rich young man, from consumerism to retreat life, the speaker argues that letting go is not about grim self-denial or willpower; it is about freedom, love, clarity, and a deeper capacity for joy. The talk keeps testing that claim through stories, examples from ordinary life, and a long closing poem by Mary Oliver that points to amazement and enoughness.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening the question 01:56 Path and tradition 06:13 Beyond stereotypes 09:41 Freedom, not self-control 13:27 Consumerism and the path 16:59 What renunciation gives 21:58 Hunger and depth 27:04 Joy in ordinary life 37:30 Wise company and amazement
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/12860/

item.title: The Place of Love in Insight Meditation
item.description:
What if the real question in practice is not whether you can feel loving-kindness, but what relationship you are bringing to each moment? The talk moves from kindness as a basic support for investigation, to the claim that love is more than a feeling, and then into a radical welcoming of experience that can soften pain, resistance, and judgment. Along the way, the speaker questions the hidden agenda of self-improvement and shows how a change in view can change how things appear.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Nourishment and inquiry 02:17 Kindness as climate 05:41 Love beyond feeling 07:18 Sangha and silence 12:25 Beyond self-concern 21:49 Compassionate seeing 24:24 Welcoming resistance 33:33 Love and emptiness 38:50 Changing the view
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21006/

item.title: The Art of Letting Go
item.description:
What if letting go is less about getting rid of something than about seeing how clinging, pushing away, and even self-judgment keep suffering going? The talk begins by tracing attachment through body image, emotion, roles, relationships, and ownership, then keeps turning toward a quieter question: can the relationship itself be relaxed. It also warns against a cold version of non-attachment and instead points toward mindfulness with warmth, patience, and acceptance, where suffering is touched directly and seen to arise through grasping.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Letting Go Begins 03:51 Where Attachment Lands 06:11 What It Feels Like 09:11 Kindness Over Self-Judgment 13:48 Mindfulness And Training 20:12 Relationship And Strength 27:45 Roles, Ownership, Love 37:20 Suffering As Teacher 47:44 Welcoming And Emptiness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21013/

item.title: Compassion and Emptiness
item.description:
What happens when compassion is treated not as a mood, but as a way of seeing through self-interest, fear, and blame? The talk starts with a working definition—compassion as the heart's response to suffering—and then keeps widening it, showing how empathy, steadiness, wisdom, kindness, joy, and even humour all belong to it. From there it moves into the practical question of what helps compassion grow and what blocks it, bringing in questioning, self-compassion, and a series of stories and reflections that lead toward emptiness, interdependence, and a less separate sense of self.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 Opening Definition 02:30 Compassion as a Composite 05:55 Why We Value It 10:48 Questioning the Right Way 13:24 What Nurtures Compassion 19:46 Fear and Anger 24:59 From Blame to Understanding 31:29 Empathy and Spaciousness 39:53 Emptiness and Oneness
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21007/

item.title: Contemplating The 3 Characteristics (Pt 2) - Anatta (Not-Self)
item.description:
What happens when the sense of self is not taken as a fixed fact, but as something that keeps appearing, tightening, and releasing? The talk moves from the felt pain of self-referencing through the five aggregates, then into subtler territory: even awareness can become an identity unless it too is seen through. It stays practical throughout, with suggestions for noticing “not me, not mine” in ordinary experience, while also warning against turning anatta into another view to cling to.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Framing Anatta 02:34 Why Self Matters 06:22 Pain And Self-View 14:12 Fear And Appreciation 20:12 Buddha And Practical View 24:10 Five Aggregates Practice 30:04 Avoiding Experience Clinging 35:48 Objects And Awareness 48:14 Mutuality And The Self Agenda
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21009/

item.title: Contemplating The 3 Characteristics (Pt 1) - Impermanence and Dukkha
item.description:
Impermanence here is not treated as an idea to admire, but as a way of looking that can loosen clinging and expose the subtle suffering built into grasping and pushing away. The talk moves from the three characteristics, through concrete reflections on loss, body sensations, and daily change, to a careful warning that insight must not slide into aversion or disgust. It also reaches for a larger question: if everything conditioned keeps passing, what kind of practice can open to peace, spaciousness, and something beyond the endless cycle of change?
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:01 Opening the Three Characteristics 03:21 Why Practice Must Be Deliberate 06:56 Facing Impermanence 11:46 Grief, Loss, and Time 16:48 Working With Change 26:38 Dukkha and Letting Go 31:46 Avoiding Aversion 39:44 Beyond the Conditioned 49:26 Closing Reflections
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21008/

item.title: Mindfulness of Mind States
item.description:
What happens when a mind state starts to feel like the whole of reality? The talk moves through the third foundation of mindfulness by showing how emotions, moods, thoughts, body sensations, perception, and self-belief all shape experience, and how mindfulness can notice these pieces without collapsing them into “me.” It then shifts from simple observation to practical work: accommodating difficult states with steadiness, cultivating wholesome ones with right effort, and using spaciousness and kindness to see that mind states are changing, not fixed. The result is a quiet, detailed guide to meeting whatever is present without adding unnecessary suffering on top.
Location: Gaia House
Chapters: 00:00 From body to mind 04:07 What mind states include 10:11 Reading the whole constellation 15:00 Self-belief and view 20:09 Accommodating difficulty 24:49 When to rest and return 30:18 Cultivating the wholesome 35:50 Insight into change 43:25 Letting the talk settle
Source: Dharma Seed
Original page: https://dharmaseed.org/talks/21010/
